2. **Opponent pattern:** Minni's consistent checking from small blind through river indicates she's holding a marginal/weak hand herself, likely wanting to keep the pot small
3. **Pot situation:** The pot is only 200. Even if Minni folds to a bet, the gain is minimal and doesn't justify the risk of getting called by a better hand
4. **Position & stack:** While my stack advantage is significant, value betting with the worst hand is theoretically unsound
5. **Showdown:** Going to showdown with j-high against an opponent who's shown this much weakness is reasonable - I likely win some portion of these matchups

Betting would be speculative bluffing on the river with the worst hand, which is unprofitable. Minni's weakness is already evident; I can take down this small pot with showdown value. Checking allows the hand to conclude efficiently and respects proper hand rankings.

Button vs two blinds in a 3‑handed pot — 9♣5♣ is a suited, playable hand with good post‑flop potential and I have positional advantage. Pot is only 150 and to call is 100; I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds while keeping sizing sensible versus the stacks. A standard 3x BB open to 300 (total) gives good fold equity, is small enough relative to my 6,750 stack to fold to a big 3‑bet, and still builds a pot when I hit. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet from Claude, re‑evaluate vs a shove from the shorter Flash (likely fold unless his shove range is extremely wide), and if called play aggressively heads‑up with c‑bets on favorable flops and pot control or check back in multiway scenarios.

Folding 6h-Js from the small blind. This is a weak, unpaired hand that plays poorly out of position. Although the pot odds are reasonable (1.8:1 to call 250 into 450), the actual equity against Minni's button range and potential BB action is poor. I have a healthy 27,400 chip stack and should reserve my capital for better spots. Folding preserves my chips for hands where I have positional and card strength advantages. With both opponents still to act, there's significant risk of facing aggression or getting drawn into a multi-way pot with a marginal hand from the worst position at the table.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9h 2d, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni opened from the Button to 3BB (300). While the pot odds are roughly 2.25:1, 92o is very difficult to play out of position and has poor equity realization. In a three-handed game, ranges are wider, but this hand is still significantly below the threshold for a profitable defend. I will fold and wait for a better spot to use my remaining 57 Big Blinds.

Button position with Q7o provides significant leverage. While Q7 is a weak holding, I have 3:1 pot odds to call, positioning advantage, and a large chip lead over both opponents. I can profitably play post-flop in position and exploit my edge with position betting.

I am in the Small Blind with J4s. The Button (Claude) has limped in. I only need to call 50 more to see a flop. While J4s is a weak hand, the pot odds are 4:1, and it has some post-flop playability due to being suited. Given the deep stacks (especially Claude's), I will complete the small blind to see a cheap flop, though I must be cautious if Minni in the Big Blind raises.

I have K♣Q♦ in the big blind and both opponents have limped. KQo is too good to play passively multiway — its equity drops with extra players and I prefer to take the initiative. I raise to isolate or take the pot preflop: 500 is a healthy iso size (5x the BB) — big enough to discourage some limp-calls and isolate one opponent, but small enough to keep worse hands calling and leave plenty of postflop maneuvering room with my ~68bb stack. Plan: take heads-up action if Claude folds; if I face a large 3-bet I will re-evaluate (likely fold to a very large 3-bet, call or flat smaller ones and play postflop).

Call 400 more to reach 500 total. Q7o is a weak holding, but I have several strong reasons to proceed: (1) I'm on the button with significant positional advantage for post-flop play; (2) I have a dominant 27,300 chip stack compared to Flash (5,600) and Minni (6,400), allowing me to leverage chips as pressure; (3) Pot odds are approximately 1.75:1, and my position advantage combined with implied odds against short-stacked opponents makes this call profitable; (4) I can exploit my information advantage post-flop and control the action; (5) Minni's raising pattern suggests a wide range from the big blind, so I likely have more equity than initially apparent. The key is to see the flop in position and outplay these opponents with my chip advantage. A call also keeps both opponents in, allowing me to potentially win a larger pot if I connect with the board.

I originally completed the Small Blind for 50 more, but Minni has now raised to 500 from the Big Blind. Claude has called the 500. While I have a suited hand, J4s is very weak, and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents, one of whom (Claude) is the chip leader and playing very actively. The pot odds are 700 to call into a total pot of 1100 (approximately 1.6:1), which is not favorable for a hand that is likely dominated or behind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I was the preflop aggressor and the flop (Qc 9c 2c) gives me top pair (Q) with a very strong kicker (Kc) plus a one-card club flush draw (I have Kc, there are 3 clubs on board → 9 outs). This is a high-value, protection-oriented spot heads-up: I want to charge club draws and extract value from worse Queens, pocket pairs and club draws. I will bet ~2/3 pot (750) — large enough to deny correct odds to many draws and to get value, but not so large as to commit my stack unnecessarily. Plan vs responses: if Claude folds I take the pot; if he calls I will re-evaluate on the turn and continue with many turn cards; if he check-raises big I will put him on a made flush/two pair and give it up to heavy aggression.

Call 750 to see the turn in position. I have top pair with Q7, but the three-flush board (9♣ Q♣ 2♣) creates significant vulnerability. By calling from the button, I maintain positional control and can better evaluate my hand strength on the turn card. The weak kicker makes overcommitting risky, and my dominant chip stack allows me to be selective. If the turn is safe, I can apply pressure; if it's a club or unfavorable card, I can control the pot size. Position and information gathering outweigh raising with a marginal hand here.

I have K♣Q♦ on Qc9c2c5d — top pair with the best kicker and a one-card club flush draw (9 outs). Claude’s flop call of 750 is consistent with worse Qx, medium pocket pairs or a single-club draw; a slow-played made flush is less likely since he didn’t raise the flop. Checking hands the villain a free river to many draws and misses value from worse Queens/pairs. I want to charge club draws and extract value, while keeping enough fold equity if I face heavy aggression. Betting ~2/3 pot (~1700 into 2600) accomplishes this: it denies the ~20% club draw correct pot odds (threshold ≈840) and prices many weaker hands into a call, but doesn’t overcommit my ~56bb stack. Plan: take it down if he folds; call/continue cautiously on the river if he calls; fold to a large check-raise/shove that strongly represents a made flush or two pair.

I'm facing a 1,700 turn bet from Minni on a board of 9c Qc 2c 5d with Q7. Here's my decision:

HAND STRENGTH: I have top pair with a weak seven kicker—a marginal holding on a concerning three-flush board. Minni's aggressive flop raise (750) and turn bet (1,700) suggest a strong hand or semi-bluff.

POT ODDS & EQUITY: The current pot is approximately 4,250. I'm getting 2.5:1 odds to call 1,700. Against Minni's likely range (strong Q, sets, or flush draws), I estimate 30-35% equity—right around breakeven or slightly positive.

STACK DYNAMICS: The critical factor here is Minni's stack position. After betting 1,700, he has only 2,250 remaining—he's extremely short. This severely constrains his options on the river and gives me enormous positional leverage. With my 26,150 chip stack, I can control the final action and extract value or minimize losses based on river runout.

POSITIONAL ADVANTAGE: From the button, I'll act last on the river. This allows me to evaluate Minni's river check/bet decision and respond optimally. If a blank hits, his short stack forces a difficult decision that I can exploit.

STRATEGIC RATIONALE: Calling maintains flexibility, keeps Minni in the pot, and lets me extract value on favorable rivers while protecting against the worst outcomes. Folding surrenders 1,250 in invested chips. Raising into his short stack is unnecessary—the river will likely finish the action anyway.

CALL is the correct play for equity and position.

I have top pair (Q) with the best kicker (K) and missed the club flush on the river. Claude’s line (limp/call preflop, call 750 on the flop and call 1700 on the turn) is very consistent with Qx, single pair (9x/Tx), or medium pocket pairs; a made club flush or two-pair/set is possible but less likely since he never raised earlier. He checked the river, giving me a value-extraction opportunity. I want to get called by worse Qx and medium pairs but avoid overcommitting to a potential strong made hand — so I size to ~1/3 pot to maximize calls from worse hands while keeping fold equity against bluffs and preserving some stack if he check-raises. If he calls I go to showdown; if he check-raises heavy or shoves, I will fold (his line would be polarized to very strong hands). Therefore I bet a medium value size of 2000 into the 6000 pot.

River call with top pair (Q7) on 9c Qc 2c 5d Ts. I'm getting 2.5-3:1 pot odds and need ~28% equity to break even. Against Minni's likely range, I estimate 30-35% equity. His short stack (1,950 remaining) constrains his bluffing range and suggests potential desperation. Top pair is a made hand that can win against busted draws and weaker queens. The math slightly favors calling, and folding here surrenders too many chips in a tournament where stack building is crucial.

I am on the button with 7h 4d, which is a very weak starting hand. My stack size of 5600 (56 big blinds) is the smallest at the table, but I am still deep enough to wait for better opportunities. Both Minni and Claude have been active and are capable of defending their blinds or 3-betting. There is no reason to enter the pot with such a low-equity hand from this position.

I have 10♠2♣ — a very weak offsuit hand. Heads-up from the small blind I can see a flop very cheaply: the pot is 150 and I need to add 50 more (total commit = 100), which gives good immediate pot odds. I don’t want to raise — Claude is deep-stacked and likely to call or 3-bet, which would put me in tough spots OOP with a marginal hand. I'll call to see a cheap flop and proceed cautiously: fold to strong aggression unless I pair the ten or pick up a realistic draw, otherwise avoid bloating the pot out of position.

I'm in the big blind with 2h-9d, one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni (small blind) merely called 50 chips, showing weakness rather than aggression. Flash folded on the button. With a trash hand and no initiative, the correct play is to check and see a flop. The pot is currently 200 chips and I'm 100 deep, so taking a cheap look at the flop is prudent. If the flop misses me completely, I can fold to aggression. If I catch something, I can continue.

I have T♠2♣ on 3♥6♣3♠ — no pair and no meaningful draw (only a backdoor spade). I’m out of position vs Claude and he checked preflop, so his range is wide and will continue enough hands that a bluff here is not worth bloating the pot. Best play is to control the pot and see a free turn: check and fold to any sizable flop bet. If he checks back I can reassess on the turn; if he fires a small probe I may fold or occasionally call very small bets, but I will not build the pot with air here.

Holding 2h-9d on a 3h-6c-3s board, I have zero equity - no pair, no draws, no potential whatsoever. Minni checked from the small blind, showing weakness. With a trash hand and no showdown value, bluffing here is poor risk/reward. I'm deep-stacked (22,350), so taking unnecessary risks with trash hands is unprofitable long-term. Checking allows me to see the turn for free, and I can fold to any aggression. If Minni checks again, I can reach showdown cheaply and potentially pick up the pot if she has air as well.

Board: 3♥ 6♣ 3♠ Q♦. I hold T♠2♣ — no pair, no meaningful draw and effectively no showdown equity. Claude checked preflop and checked the flop, so his range is wide but could include any Q, a 3, or many overcards and medium pairs. I’m out of position and the pot is small relative to our stacks, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with air. Pot control is the priority: check and fold to meaningful aggression, re-evaluate only if he checks back to see the river and possibly attempt a light bluff on a favorable card.

Holding 2h-9d, I have absolutely zero equity on the 3h-6c-3s-Qd board. No pair, no draws, zero showdown value. Minni has checked on both flop and turn, demonstrating weakness. With a deep stack (22,350) and a trash hand, checking to see the river for free is the optimal play. Bluffing here with no value is poor risk/reward - if Minni has any hand she'll call, and if she has nothing we win the pot either way on a river check-check or when she folds to a river bet. The turn check allows me to reach the river cost-free and make a better-informed decision with complete board information. If Minni bets, I can easily fold.

I have T♠2♣ and have missed the entire board (3♥ 6♣ 3♠ Q♦ A♣) — no pair, no real draw and essentially zero showdown equity. Claude has been passive (check/call lines) and historically is a sticky caller, so my fold equity on a river bluff is low. The pot is tiny (200) versus deep stacks, so risking chips to try to steal is unnecessary. Optimal line is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful river bet; if Claude checks back we preserve chips and move on.

Hand is complete with Minni checking all three streets (flop, turn, river). I hold 2h-9d with zero equity - the worst possible hand on board 3h-6c-3s-Qd-Ac. Checking is optimal because: (1) I have no showdown value and cannot win if called on a bet, (2) The 200-chip pot is negligible relative to my 22,350 chip stack, (3) With 126 hands remaining in the tournament, I should avoid variance with trash hands, (4) Minni's complete checking pattern shows weakness, so she likely checks behind, allowing me to reach showdown cheaply. A bluff here has poor risk/reward - risking 100 for a 200 pot when I have literally no hand is marginal at best.

Button with Qc6c — a playable, suited queen with position in a 3‑handed pot. Pot is 150 and to call is 100, but open‑raising from the button is standard to seize initiative and get fold equity from the blinds. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB): large enough to discourage easy defense but small enough to keep pots manageable with a marginal hand. Plan: if called, use position to c‑bet on dry boards and realize equity with flush/queen-portion hands; if I hit top pair or strong draw I’ll build the pot, otherwise control size and fold to heavy resistance. Versus a big 3‑bet (committing a large portion of my stack) I’ll fold; I may consider calling a small 3‑bet from the BB to see a flop and play postflop in position.

Folding 9-6 offsuit. This is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position against Minni's raise. While I have a large stack (22,300), there's no strategic advantage to getting involved with marginal hands when the situation is unfavorable. From the small blind, I'm already out of position post-flop. The hand lacks both high card strength (only a nine) and flush/straight draw potential. Minni's aggressive style means they're raising with a wide range, but I don't need to get involved with every hand just because I have chips. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities while maintaining tight play from out-of-position.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q4 of hearts. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button, and Claude has folded. In a 3-handed game, opening ranges from the button are typically very wide. I am being offered 2.25:1 pot odds (need to call 200 to win a total pot of 650). Q4s has enough equity against a standard button opening range to justify a call, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. I'll see a flop and look for a Queen, hearts, or straight potential.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Queen of hearts, 4 kicker) on a board of Td-Qd-6h. In a 3-handed game against a button open, top pair is a strong hand, but the kicker makes it vulnerable to better Queens (AQ, KQ, QJ). The board also has several draws (diamonds, KJ, J9). Since I am out of position and Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play. This allows Minni to continue with bluffs or semi-bluffs, and I can comfortably call a bet to see the turn. Donk-betting here would likely only be called by better hands or strong draws, putting me in a difficult spot.

Flop Td Qd 6h and I hold Qc6c — I have two pair (Queens and Sixes). It’s heads-up vs Flash who defended a 3x preflop raise, so his range includes lots of Qx, Tx, medium pocket pairs and some diamond draws. My hand is very strong here; only sets (QQ/TT/66) or a better two‑pair (QT) really beat me. I should bet for value and to charge diamond draws. Pot is 650, so a ~2/3‑pot value/protection bet of 450 is appropriate — it gets value from worse queens, tens and single pairs and makes draws pay to continue. If he jams I’ll seriously consider calling given my equity vs his calling range; if he just calls I’ll re‑evaluate the turn for further value or control.

I have top pair (Queens) on a board with several draws (diamonds and straights). Minni is betting 450 into a 650 pot, which is a standard continuation bet from a button opener. While my kicker (4) is weak, folding top pair here would be too exploitable in a 3-handed game against a wide button opening range. Raising is not ideal because I would likely only get action from better Queens or strong draws. By calling, I keep Minni's bluffs in her range and can reassess on the turn based on the card and her subsequent action.

The turn Ten pairs the board, which is a bit of a scare card. While I have Two Pair (Queens and Tens), any Ten in Minni's range now has trips. Additionally, any Queen with a better kicker (which is most of them) has me beat. Checking is the most prudent move to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts. If she checks behind, it's a good result. If she bets, I'll have to evaluate the bet sizing.

I hold Qc6c on Td Qd 6h Th. My best five is Qc Qd Td Th 6c — two pair (Queens and Tens) with a 6 kicker. Flash called my flop bet, so his range contains lots of Qx, Tx, medium pockets and some diamond draws. The turn pairing the Ten creates trips/full-house danger (any T, pocket 6s, or pocket Qs), but I’m still ahead of many of his Qx/Kx/Jx and worse T combos. I want to extract value while keeping the pot manageable vs potential traps, so I’ll bet a medium size (~55–60% pot) to charge worse hands and deny a free river. Pot = 1550; bet 900 to get value from worse pairs and vulnerable tens while still allowing a fold to a large check-raise/shove if necessary.

I have Two Pair (Queens and Tens) with a very weak kicker (4). The turn Ten is a scare card because it puts three Tens to a boat or trips in play, and Minni's wide button opening range certainly includes many hands containing a Ten (AT, KT, JT, 9T, 8T). However, folding top pair on the turn in a 3-handed game for a 58% pot-sized bet is likely too tight and exploitable, as Minni could be semi-bluffing with diamond draws (AdXd, KdXd), straight draws (KJ, J9), or simply betting a better Queen. I am getting roughly 2.7:1 pot odds, requiring about 27% equity. Given the wide ranges in short-handed play, I will call to see a river and evaluate based on the final card and Minni's aggression.

On the river, the board is Td-Qd-6h-Th-4c. My best five-card hand is two pair, Queens and Tens with a Six kicker (Q-Q-T-T-6). While this is a decent hand in a short-handed game, it is primarily a bluff-catcher at this point. Any Ten in Minni's range has improved to trips or better, and most Queens in her button-opening range (AQ, KQ, QJ) have me out-kicked since my 4 is lower than the board's 6. There are several missed draws on this board, specifically the diamond flush draw and straight draws like KJ or J9. By checking, I avoid value-owning myself against better hands and give Minni the opportunity to bluff with those missed draws. If she checks back, I have a fair chance of winning at showdown against her bluffs or smaller pocket pairs.

Board Td Qd 6h Th 4c. I hold Qc6c — two pair, Queens and Tens, 6 kicker. Villain (Flash) called my preflop open, my 450 flop bet and my 900 turn bet and now checks the river: a passive, calling line consistent with lots of Qx, some Tx, medium pocket pairs and missed diamond draws. Hands that beat me (full houses/quads: TT, QQ, 66) are possible but would more often have raised earlier; most of villain's range is actually behind (single-pair Queens, weaker Tens, pair+draws). Checking surrenders value to many worse hands; betting for thin value is correct. I want a sizing that gets called by worse Qx/medium pairs but doesn't overcommit if I face a large shove. A bet of 1,500 (~45% pot) accomplishes that — extracts value from worse hands while keeping the pot and my risk controlled.

I have Two Pair, Queens and Tens with a Six kicker. On a board of Td-Qd-6h-Th-4c, any Ten has me beat with trips, and most Queens in a standard Button opening range (AQ, KQ, QJ) have me out-kicked. However, the river bet of 1500 into a 3350 pot (total pot 4850) offers me pot odds of approximately 3.2:1, meaning I only need to win about 24% of the time to break even. Given the 3-handed nature of the game and Minni's wide opening range from the Button, she could be bluffing with missed diamond draws (AdKd, etc.) or missed straight draws (KJ, J9). The sizing is small enough that folding a hand as strong as top pair (now two pair) would be too exploitable. I will call to catch potential bluffs.

T4o is a very weak holding that ranks poorly in hand equity. While I'm on the button with good position, this hand doesn't justify entering the pot even at favorable odds. The combination of weak card strength and vulnerability to both remaining opponents makes folding the disciplined play. With a solid 22,300 chip stack, I can afford to wait for premium hands in better spots. This preserves my chips for higher equity situations where my position advantage can be leveraged with stronger holdings.

Claude folded on the button, leaving the action to the blinds. I have Seven-Four of diamonds, which is a speculative suited hand with some connectivity. It costs only 50 chips more to see a flop against Minni, and while I will be out of position, the price is attractive enough to see if I can connect with the board.

We’re heads-up now (SB limped 50). Holding K♦8♦ in the big blind is a hand I want to take the initiative with — it plays well HU and has flush/straight potential. Raising isolates the caller, gets fold equity, and lets me define Flash’s range rather than letting him see a cheap flop with a wide range. I’ll make a standard isolation raise to 300 total (small, pressure-raising size > min raise) — keeps the pot manageable if called. If he folds I win the pot immediately; if called I’ll continuation bet favorable flops and control pot size on dry/unfavorable boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Seven-Four of diamonds. While it is a suited hand, it is very weak and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against Minni, who has shown significant aggression. After my initial completion, Minni's raise to 300 signals strength or a desire to take the pot immediately. Calling 200 more into a pot of 400 with such a marginal hand from out of position is generally a losing play in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
I am on the button with King-Five suited in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. I will raise to 3x the big blind to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the blinds.

I’m in the small blind with A♥6♠ facing a 3x raise (300) from the button. This is a marginal but playable hand — A-high has decent equity against a button opening range and can flop top-pair+good redraws. Folding is too nitty given my stack and the price; 3-betting A6o as a bluff is unattractive (hand is easily dominated by many Axs and a larger 3-bet bloats the pot out of position and invites calls/squeezes). Calling 250 keeps the pot controllable, preserves stack depth to play postflop, and lets me see a flop with reasonable equity. Postflop plan: if I hit an Ace or improve, bet for value; if I miss, play cautiously — check/call small C-bets for equity and fold to large aggression unless I pick up strong redraws.

With 7d-Td from the big blind, this is a marginal hand that doesn't warrant continued investment. While I'm getting decent pot odds (3.5:1 to call 200 into a 700 pot), several factors make folding the correct play:

1. Hand strength: 7-10 offsuit is below the calling threshold from the big blind against a raise. It's not a strong enough hand to profitably defend.

2. Position: I'll be out of position postflop against two opponents (Flash on button, Minni in small blind), which significantly reduces my ability to play profitably.

3. Opponent stacks: Flash is relatively short at 5,225. If I call and miss the flop (which happens ~65% of the time), I'll be in difficult positions trying to balance my range.

4. Expected value: Despite reasonable pot odds, my positional disadvantage and hand weakness make this a negative expected value spot in the long run.

Better big blind defending hands would include pocket pairs, strong broadway combinations, or suited connectors. 7-10 offsuit doesn't meet that threshold. This is a profitable long-term fold.

I flopped top pair (A♠ with A♥6♠) on 3♥–3♦–A♠. This is a clear value/protection spot: the board is paired so trips/full house combos (3x, A3) are possible but rare; the preflop opener (button) has a wide range that contains many worse A‑hands, medium pairs and broadways that will pay a bet. I want to charge those hands and deny free cards rather than slow‑play with a weak kicker. Size to about half the pot so I extract value from worse Aces and call-down hands while keeping the pot controllable against a shove from Flash (he’s only at ~5.2k). Plan: bet 350; call a small raise and re-evaluate the turn, but fold to a large check‑raise/shove representing trips/full house.

I started with a pre-flop raise on the button with King-Five of clubs. The flop comes Ace-Trey-Trey, which is a very dry board that favors either a hand with an Ace or a three. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 350 into the 700 pot. My hand has very little equity here; even if I hit a King, it would likely be second-best to any Ace in her range. While she could be bluffing or testing the waters, I have no pieces of this board and no real draws. Rather than wasting chips on a marginal float or a high-variance bluff, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed game with 9♠6♦. In 3-handed play the button can open a fairly wide range — this hand is marginal but playable in position. I want to take the initiative, pressure the blinds, and keep the pot manageable, so I will open to a standard 3×BB (300). If I face a large 3-bet from Claude (very deep and aggressive) I'll fold — I don't want to play a bloated pot out of position with a weak offsuit hand. If Flash (the shorter BB) gives a small defense and I see a flop, I'll play cautiously for pairs/draws and use position to control the pot.

Folding 2-3 offsuit from the small blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and has virtually no playability post-flop. While I have a large chip stack (22,150) and could theoretically play loosely, there's no strategic advantage to playing such a weak hand - I should preserve chips and wait for better opportunities. Even though Minni's raise could be wide from the button, my hand plays poorly against most hands and lacks meaningful equity. A straightforward fold.

I am in the Big Blind with K2o. Minni, who has been quite active from the button, raised to 3 big blinds. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), K2o has poor playability post-flop, especially out of position against an aggressive opponent. I prefer to fold and wait for a hand with better structural potential or a stronger high-card value to defend my blind.

Folding 2d-Jc from the button. This is a weak, unconnected hand that doesn't warrant a call despite position. With a healthy 22,150 chip stack and 121 hands remaining, disciplined hand selection is more valuable than marginal calls. Even though Flash (SB) is short-stacked at 5,075, speculative calls with weak hands aren't profitable long-term.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten of spades, which is a very strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. Since the Button folded, I only have to contend with Minni in the Big Blind. I want to raise for value and to take control of the pot. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

Flash (button/small blind) opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Kc7d — calling requires 200 more (300 total committed). Heads-up the button’s opening range is wide, and K7o is playable; calling is the lowest-variance, +EV option here. I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand by 3-betting; calling gives good pot odds and lets me see a flop and re-evaluate. Plan: proceed cautiously OOP — continue with top pair/strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, and exploit spots postflop.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Ten of spades and was called by the Big Blind. The flop is 5-3-8 rainbow, which is fairly dry. While I haven't hit a pair, I still have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage on this board. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to try to take the pot down immediately or to extract value from weaker high cards and draws.

Villain (Flash) opened wide preflop and now makes a small c‑bet of 300 into a 900 pot on a very dry 5♠‑3♥‑8♦ flop. I’m out of position with K♣7♦ — no pair and no real draw. The bet is only ~1/3 pot, so calling 300 to win a pot that will be ~1500 gives ~20% pot‑odds. I have ~6 outs (3 kings + 3 sevens) for roughly a 24% chance to hit by the river, and against a wide SB button range my showdown equity is in the neighbourhood of the required pot odds. Raising OOP with K7o is thin and unnecessary — it bloats the pot and risks getting committed or exploited. Folding here would be too tight given the price. Plan: call to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold to heavy turn aggression on a blank, but value/raise if I pair up.

The turn 6s is an excellent card for my hand, giving me the nut flush draw in addition to my two overcards. While Minni called the flop, the board is still relatively coordinated and I want to maintain the initiative. A second barrel here puts pressure on her middle-pair hands (like 8x or 5x) while building a pot for when I hit my flush or an Ace on the river. If she raises, I have more than enough equity to call.

Re-evaluated on the turn and this is an easy fold. I have K♣7♦ on 5♠-3♥-8♦-6♠ with no pair and no spade. On the river I only have 6 outs (3 kings + 3 sevens) ≈ 13% to improve. Flash’s turn bet (800) into the current pot requires roughly 28–29% equity to profitably call (800/(2000+800) ≈ 28.6%). My raw equity (≈13%) is far below the required pot odds, so calling is -EV. Raising as a bluff OOP with no blockers or backdoor draw is too thin against a player who has shown aggression and can easily have a pair or better; it would just build a pot I’m unlikely to win. Preserve the stack and fold.

I am on the button with 8d 3h, which is a very weak hand. I have 57.25 big blinds, and both opponents have significantly larger stacks. While I am in position, these cards do not offer enough equity to justify a raise or a call against Minni and Claude, who have been active and willing to call or re-raise. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up after Flash folded; I'm in the small blind with 2♣4♣. Pot is 150 and I only need to add 50 (total contribution 100) to see the flop. 2‑4 suited is a speculative hand with backdoor flush/straight potential and plays well heads‑up, and calling preserves my postflop positional advantage. Calling is cheap (I need ~25% equity to break even) and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal hand versus Claude's large stack. If Claude 3‑bets hard I can fold preflop; otherwise I'll evaluate the flop and proceed only with clear equity or favorable situations.

I'm holding 9d 4h in the big blind against Minni's small blind limp. This is a weak hand that doesn't warrant aggressive action pre-flop. Minni's limp from the small blind suggests a weak or speculative hand. With no raise and heads-up play, I should take a conservative approach and check to see the flop. This allows me to evaluate my hand strength against the board texture before committing additional chips. Checking here keeps my options open and avoids inflating the pot with a marginal hand.

I have 2♣4♣ and hit a pair of 4s on 6♠9♠4♦. Heads‑up and on the button, I have position and a real but vulnerable hand (bottom pair with a weak kicker). Claude checked preflop and we have a relatively wide range; the flop is fairly dry aside from the two spades (only players with two spades have a real flush draw). With position and a hand that can get value from worse pairs/weak tens/eights/overcards that will call a small bet, I prefer a small c‑bet to extract value, deny equity to some overcards and keep the pot manageable. I will bet ~½ pot (100). If Claude raises large/polarizing (pot‑sized or bigger) I will be inclined to fold this thin pair; I will call smaller raises to evaluate the turn.

I flopped two pair (9s and 4s) which is a strong holding in this heads-up spot against Minni's limp-check pre-flop followed by flop aggression. Her betting pattern suggests a weak hand or draw. Raising to 300 total (200 more) builds the pot while I'm likely ahead of her range and keeps her committed. This maximizes value from my strong hand while maintaining fold equity against weaker holdings. The raise is sized to get called by marginal hands while not over-committing.

I have 2♣4♣ on 6♠‑9♠‑4♦ — bottom pair with a weak kicker. I c‑bet 100 and Claude raised to 300; the pot is 600 and I must call 200 more (my total on this street would be 300). Calling is the correct line: the call requires ~25% equity (200/(600+200)), which my hand has vs a raising range that contains many spade draws and weaker pairs I beat. I keep positional advantage and can fold to a big turn barrel or re‑evaluate if I improve. Raising from this holding is an overaggressive bluff (I have no spade blocker and a poor kicker) and folding now surrenders value from worse pairs/draws. Plan: call to see the turn, control the pot with position, and fold to pot‑sized/large continuation bets unless I improve.

I have 2♣4♣ and only bottom pair on 6♠ 9♠ 4♦ 9♣. Claude showed real strength by raising my flop c‑bet, so his range is weighted to value (pairs, 9x, maybe sets) plus some spade semi‑bluffs. The turn pairing the board to a second 9 makes many of those hands (or trips on the turn) much better than my hand, and I no longer have a realistic flush route (only one club on board and one card to come). With position and a marginal holding I want pot control: check and see what Claude does. Plan: fold to a pot‑sized or large barrel, call small‑to‑medium bets to keep worse pairs/draws in and realize equity, and if checked to on the river re‑evaluate (I only really improve to a full house with a 4 or to a workable two‑pair with a 2).

I'm holding 9d 4h on a board of 6s 9s 4d 9c, which gives me a full house (Nines full of Fours). This is an extremely strong hand - I'm heavily favored to win. Minni checked the turn after showing flop aggression, suggesting weakness. Betting 400 chips (roughly half the pot) extracts value from her likely weaker holdings while keeping her engaged. This sizing balances value extraction with fold equity, avoiding both under-betting my premium hand and over-betting to the point of scaring her away. The goal is to maximize chips won on the river while maintaining the best possible hand."

Pot is 1,200 and Claude just bet 400 on the turn (I must call 400). Calling requires ~25% equity (400/(1200+400)=25%). I hold 2♣4♣ — bottom pair with a poor kicker and no spade blocker. Claude’s line (raising my flop c‑bet and betting the turn) is weighted to value (9x, sets, big pairs) but also includes spade semi‑bluffs. Raising myself would be an overaggressive bluff without good blockers; folding is too surrendering given the cheap price to see the river and the fact I’m in position. I’ll call the 400 to realize equity and keep worse pairs/draws in; on the river I will fold to a pot‑sized or larger barrel unless I improve to a clear winner (otherwise call small bets or check to showdown).

I hold 2♣4♣ and the river gives me two pair (9s and 4s: 9♠ 9♣ 4♦ 6♠ A♣). Claude raised my flop c‑bet and barreled the turn, a line heavily weighted to 9x, sets or other made hands that now beat me (any 9, pocket 4s, pocket Aces, or combos that make full houses). Claude checked the river, which is ambiguous (either a missed spade semi‑bluff or a slowplay), but betting here is risky — a bet will either get called by hands that still beat me or induce a raise that commits me to a large pot when I’m likely behind. Checking preserves pot control and takes the cheapest line to showdown; if he bets a small amount I can call to realize equity versus missed draws, and I can fold to a large river raise. Given the board texture and his line, the lowest variance, highest expected EV play is to check to showdown.

River value bet with a full house (9s full of 4s). Minni has shown weakness throughout the hand - she limped pre-flop, called my flop raise, checked the turn after calling, and now checked the river. Her action pattern indicates a marginal hand at best (likely a pair or missed draw). The Ace doesn't help her. Betting 700 into the 1600 pot is a well-sized value bet that keeps her engaged with hands like pairs or weak holdings while maximizing chips won. This is the optimal play to extract value from my premium hand against an opponent showing consistent weakness.

Pot is 2,300 and Claude bets 700. Calling 700 (pot after call = 3,000) requires ~23.3% equity to break even. I hold two pair (9s and 4s) — a strong but vulnerable hand: I lose to any 9, any Ace in hand (which makes A+9 two‑pair/full house combos), and to pocket 4s/quads/full houses. Claude’s line (raising my flop c‑bet, barreling the turn, then a small river bet) is value‑heavy but not fully polarized — it contains trips/full houses and also missed spade semi‑bluffs or thin value bets. The river sizing is small relative to the pot, so folding would surrender too much equity against bluffs and weaker holdings; raising would be overaggressive with poor blockers and will mostly fold out worse hands or get me crushed by the stronger combos. I will call 700 to realize equity and see showdown, but fold to a large raise/re‑raise since that line is highly indicative of a hand that beats me.

I’m on the button with A♣ Q♦ — a strong, positionally-powerful hand (106 BBs). Against just two blinds (Claude deep in SB, Flash medium-short in BB) I want to seize the initiative, isolate, and deny cheap flops. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) gives good fold equity, discourages limp-calls, and builds a pot when I have position while keeping my commitment reasonable if I face a 3-bet. If called I’ll c-bet favorable textures (ace- or queen-high or dry boards) and re-evaluate against resistance; if I face a large 3-bet or shove I’ll fold or call depending on sizing and ranges (AQ has decent equity versus a short-stack shove but is more marginal versus a polarized 3-bet from the deep SB). Raising to 300 maximizes EV here.

I'm holding 6-7 offsuit in the small blind facing a raise from Minni (button) to 300. This is a marginal hand at best, and I'm out of position with more action likely to come from the big blind. 

Looking at my previous hands, I've taken several beats with weak hands played out of position (particularly hands 6, 8, 23). 6-7o is not strong enough to defend from SB against an aggressive opener, especially when the big blind still has action remaining with 5,625 chips.

While the pot odds appear reasonable at first glance (450:250), my hand is too weak to play OOP without a strong reason. There's no kicker, no immediate draw potential, and I'm likely dominated by most reasonable raising hands Minni could hold.

This is a clear fold. I should preserve my 23,600 stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7h 5s. Minni opened from the Button to 300, which is a 3x raise. 7-5 offsuit is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position against a stack that covers me. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 450), the post-flop playability of this hand is poor. I would rather wait for a better spot to commit chips given my 56BB stack size.

Playing J7o from the button with position advantage against two blinds. This is a standard raising situation - I have the best positional equity on the table. A raise to 250 accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) applies pressure to the blinds, particularly Flash who is short-stacked at 5,575, potentially folding out weaker holdings; (2) allows me to play the hand profitably post-flop where my position gives a significant advantage; (3) maintains aggression and doesn't let blinds walk in cheap. J7 has decent playability with two overs and connected cards. The raise size is appropriate relative to my 23,600 stack and the current pot. From the button, this is an exploitative line against typically weaker blind defenses.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The Button (Claude) has raised to 250. Q3o is a very weak hand to play out of position, even in a three-handed game. Given that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand and the hand's poor equity against a standard Button opening range, the most prudent move is to fold and preserve my stack.

Facing a 250 open from the button in a 3-handed spot, QTs is definitely defendable but not strong enough to commit to a large 3-bet out of position. Claude's open size (2.5x) suggests a fairly wide range; I have good playability and equity with QTs (suited, connected). With ~106BB effective and ICM pressure 3-handed, I want to keep the pot controlled and realize equity rather than inflate the pot OOP with a marginal 3-bet. I will call the extra 150 (making my total 250) to see a flop and then decide based on texture — play aggressively on strong/fit flops (top pair, strong draws) and frequently check-fold or pot-control on dry boards or heavy action. If Claude shows significant aggression (large 3-bet/turn shove) without me improving, I will fold to avoid a bloated, unfavorable spot.

Flop A♠ 9♥ 6♥ with Q♠T♠: I have no pair and only weak backdoor prospects (a two-card spade/straight is very unlikely). Claude opened 2.5x from the button, so his range is fairly wide and will c‑bet a lot, but I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Pot control and hand‑reading are my priorities — check and see what Claude does. If he makes a small continuation bet (roughly ≤1/3–1/2 pot) I will call and try to realize equity; I will fold to large sizing or a raise without improving. If I hit a pair or a workable draw on the turn I’ll shift gears and play more aggressively. For now: check to keep the pot small and gather information.

Continuation bet on the flop after pre-flop raise. With J7d on 6h-As-9h, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) plus some overcard potential. Minni's check indicates weakness - this is a standard spot to apply pressure. The 400 bet (roughly 73% of pot) is a reasonable c-bet size that accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) takes down the pot immediately with some frequency given Minni's passive check; (2) if called, I have excellent equity with 8 straight draws plus two cards to come; (3) maintains my aggression from pre-flop; (4) pricing makes it -EV for Minni to call with marginal hands. Given stack depths (Minni has ~10.4k remaining), this bet is not too polarizing and keeps the hand in playable territory. The board (wet with flush possibilities and straight draws) favors an active aggressor.

Claude opened fairly wide from the button and has c‑bet 400 into a 950 pot on A♠‑9♥‑6♥ (~42% sizing). I hold Q♠T♠: currently no pair, only weak runner‑runner/spade possibilities and six immediate pair outs (Q/T) — roughly ~34% to pair by river (plus tiny backdoor flush/straight chance). Calling 400 into the 1350 pot gives me ~29.6% break‑even equity, so this is a defensible, +EV call to realize equity and keep the pot controlled out of position. I will call to see a turn, fold to sizable raise/river shove if I don’t improve, and turn up the aggression only if I pair or pick up a clear draw.

Turn 8♥ makes the board 6♥ A♠ 9♥ 8♥ — three hearts. I hold Q♠T♠: no pair, no heart, only ~6 clean outs (three Qs + three Ts) to pair on the river (~13%), and no realistic backdoor draw. Claude opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop; his turn check can be weakness or pot control, but the 3‑heart board dramatically increases the likelihood he has a made flush. Betting OOP with a marginal hand here is high risk — I can get check‑raised by heart combos or called by hands that still beat me, and I don’t have the equity to pursue a big bluff. My prior plan was to realize equity and keep the pot small after calling the flop, so I will check the turn to control the pot and see a free river. If I pair on the river I can proceed; if Claude bets the river and I don’t improve, I will fold to reasonable/large bets (only calling very small bets that give correct pot odds).

Betting 650 on the turn with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs: K or 5). The 8h makes the board highly coordinated, and Minni's turn check indicates weakness. This bet applies pressure on a vulnerable opponent with strong draw equity (roughly 30-35% to make the nuts). Bet sizing at ~48% of pot maintains balance and controls pot growth while maximizing fold equity and win rates with my drawing hand. Position and the wet board texture strongly support continuation betting here.

I checked the turn to control the pot and see a free river. Claude now fires 650 into a 2000 pot (~1/3 pot). With Q♠T♠ on 6♥ A♠ 9♥ 8♥ I have only 6 clean outs (3 Qs + 3 Ts) — ~13% to improve on the river. Calling 650 here gives me poor pot odds (I need ~20% equity to break even) and even if I hit I can still be dominated by Ax, two-pair, sets or hands containing two hearts that already make a flush. Claude’s line (button open, flop c-bet, turn barrel on a three-heart board) is consistent with lots of strong/value hands. With weak equity, bad pot odds, and significant reverse implied odds if I do pair, folding is the correct, stack-preserving play.

I am on the button with 8d 7h. In a three-handed game, the opening range from the button is typically wide, but 87 offsuit is a marginal hand. Given that both Minni (SB) and Claude (BB) have been quite active and Claude has a significant chip lead, I expect a high probability of being called or three-bet. With a stack of 55.75 BB, I am the shortest stack at the table and prefer to wait for more favorable cards or positions to commit chips, especially against aggressive opponents.

Heads-up on the button with K♦Q♠ — a strong, standard opening hand. I have ~100 BB effective and will want to play the hand in position, take the initiative, and isolate the big blind. A 3x BB open (to 300 total) is a clean, balanced size: gets value from worse kings/queens/pairs and folds some of Claude’s weaker holdings, while keeping the pot manageable versus his deeper stack and postflop aggression. Postflop plan: continuation-bet on favorable flops, control size on coordinated boards, and fold or proceed cautiously to large preflop 3-bets depending on Claude’s sizing and tendencies.

I'm in the Big Blind with Ad2h facing a raise to 300 from Minni (Small Blind). I need to call 200 more (I've already posted 100). 

Pot odds analysis: The pot will be 600 (current 400 + my 200 call). My required equity is approximately 33% (200/(400+200)). With Ad2h against Minni's raising range, I have roughly 32-35% equity - right at the breakeven point. However, several factors favor a call:

1. Position: I'll act last postflop, allowing me to respond to Minni's actions
2. Opponent profile: Minni has been aggressive throughout the tournament, likely raising a wide range from the button/SB
3. Stack sizes: My 24,200 stack is healthy and allows for optimal postflop play
4. Ace-high: Even with a weak 2-kicker, any Ace on the flop gives me a strong hand with room to play

At 50/100 stakes with deep stacks, marginal calls with implied odds are acceptable, especially against an aggressive opponent. The close pot odds justify seeing a flop where my superior postflop positioning becomes an advantage.

I have K♦Q♠ — top pair with a good kicker, but the Q♦ J♦ A♠ board is dangerous (an Ace beats me and there are two diamonds/straight possibilities). Claude defended preflop fairly wide, so his range includes Ax, Qx, Jx and diamond draws. I have position and the pot is only 600, so I want to take the initiative: a value/protection c-bet denies free cards to draws and gets called by worse queens/jacks/draws while keeping the pot manageable against Ax. I’ll make a half‑pot bet of 300. If he check‑raises big I’ll give strong credit to A/sets and re‑evaluate (likely fold to extreme aggression); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

Calling the flop bet with Ad2h. Despite the weak 2-kicker, top pair has sufficient equity against Minni's range. Pot odds of 3:1 justify the call, requiring only 25% equity. Key advantages: (1) Position - I act last post-flop to gather information; (2) Minni's likely range includes many draws and weaker pairs; (3) Stack depth allows optimal play; (4) A2 has better equity on the turn/river than right now. This is a textbook marginal call that wins through superior post-flop positioning rather than immediate hand strength.

I have top pair (Q) with a king kicker on Q♦ J♦ A♠ 9♠. I c‑bet the flop (300) and Claude just called, showing a fairly wide calling range that includes Ax, Qx, Jx, Kx (and some straight combos like K‑T), plus some diamond draws. The 9♠ on the turn is a relatively neutral card — it can give some opponents a pair/boat combo but doesn’t meaningfully improve my hand vs Aces, and it doesn’t change that I’m vulnerable to any Ace or made straight. Pot is 1,200 and I’m in position, so I want to control the size of the pot and avoid bloating it when I could be behind. I’ll check to keep worse hands and bluffs in, allow Claude to bluff with missed draws, and see his reaction: call a small bet to realize equity, fold to polarizing large check‑raises that represent A/set/straights, and re‑evaluate on the river if we go check‑check.

Minni's turn check after a flop raise indicates weakness or a draw. With Ad2h on QdJdAs9s, I hold top pair. The wet board with multiple draws justifies a value bet to charge draws and extract value from weaker Aces while protecting against free cards. Betting 650 achieves optimal balance between value extraction and pot control.

Board Q♦ J♦ A♠ 9♠ — I have Q♠ K♦: second pair (queens) with a strong kicker but behind any Ax and vulnerable to K‑T straights. I checked the turn to control size and induce bluffs; Claude’s 650 into a 1,200 pot is a medium probe bet that can contain value (Ax, Qx, JT/KT straights) and bluffs (missed diamond/backdoor combos). Calling 650 buys a cheap look (pot after call ~2,500; required equity ~26%) — my hand has enough equity versus his likely calling/turn‑betting range and I keep worse queens, jacks and bluffs in. Raising would bloat the pot and commit against many hands that currently beat me, while folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call and use position to re‑evaluate on the river — fold to polarized large raises/shoves or proceed cautiously to medium river bets depending on the card and action.

River: Qd Jd As 9s Qh. I hold Qs Kd — trips (three queens). Claude checked to me on the river after calling my flop c‑bet and betting the turn, which signals relative weakness or pot control. Very few hands beat me here — only Q+A or Q+J (Qc with A or J) — so most of his calling/turn‑betting range (Ax, Jx, Kx, missed draws and bluffs) is behind my trips or will call a reasonable value bet. I should extract value but avoid overbloating the pot and inviting a rare check‑raise by the polarized few hands that beat me. A half‑pot / medium bet (~1,200 into 2,500) targets calls from Ax, second/third pair (Jx, Kx) and some bluffs while folding out pure air. Action: bet 1200 to get value while keeping the river decision manageable if Claude shows strength.

Calling with two pair (Aces and Queens with Jack kicker). Pot odds are 1200 to win 4900 total, requiring approximately 24.5% equity. Minni's turn check on a wet board suggests weakness or a draw, not a strong made hand. His river raise after the board pairs Queens could represent a Queen (one pair that loses to my two pair), a straight with KT (I lose), or a bluff/semi-bluff. Given Minni's aggressive tendencies throughout the tournament and the close pot odds, calling is justified. My two pair has solid showdown value and I'm winning against a significant portion of his bluffing/semi-bluffing range.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed game — excellent spot to open a wide range and steal the blinds. Q3o is marginal but acceptable as a button open: it plays reasonably well in position and has some showdown value. Flash (BB) is medium-stacked (~55bb) and will defend sometimes; Claude (SB) is very deep and can 3‑bet/pressure out of position. I’ll make a standard 3x open to buy fold equity and avoid giving good odds to callers — raising only to the 200 minimum is too small and invites multi‑way action. Plan: raise to 300; if called I’ll use position to apply pressure with c‑bets on favorable flops and control pot size on wet boards. Fold to large, strong 3‑bets from Claude; re-evaluate vs a shove or small 3‑bet from Flash depending on sizing and pot odds.

I'm holding Tc-Qd in the small blind facing Minni's 300 raise from the button. This is a decent broadway hand with reasonable playability.

Analysis:
- Hand strength: T-Q offsuit is a marginal but playable hand that connects well with many flops
- Pot odds: I'm getting 450:250 (1.8:1) to call, which is reasonable for a hand that has decent flop equity
- Opponent: Minni has been raising frequently from position, indicating a wide range. Their 300 raise is not necessarily a sign of premium strength
- Stack depth: I have a healthy 21,800 chip stack, allowing me to play post-flop comfortably if I get a favorable flop
- Position: While I'm OOP post-flop, I can see the flop and evaluate from there

Strategy: Calling allows me to see a flop with decent odds. If I flop a pair, multiple broadway cards, or a draw, I'll have good decisions. If I whiff, I can fold to aggression. A raise doesn't seem warranted with this hand - I should keep the pot manageable and play position.

I am in the Big Blind with 2d 7s, which is statistically one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. The button (Minni) raised to 3x the big blind, and the small blind (Claude) called. While the pot odds are tempting (calling 200 to win a pot of 700), my hand has virtually no post-flop value and I would be playing out of position against two opponents. It is better to conserve my stack for a more favorable hand.

I'm holding Tc-Qd on a flop of 5d-6c-8d, heads-up against Minni who raised to 300 pre-flop.

Hand evaluation:
- Current hand strength: Queen-high with an over-card Ten
- Equity: Decent but not dominant - I have overcards to the board and a gutshot (any 7 makes a straight)
- This is a marginal situation where my hand is likely ahead of Minni's entire raising range, but vulnerable to many possible continuations

Strategic considerations:
1. Minni's range from button: Wide (AA-22, all broadways, suited connectors, etc.)
2. Flop connection: The 5-6-8 flop doesn't strongly hit typical button raising ranges, which is favorable for me
3. Pot dynamics: The pot is 700, and I'm out of position - I need to gather information
4. Stack depths: Both stacks are healthy (mine 21,550, Minni's 12,275), allowing for multi-street play

Action rationale:
Checking is optimal here because:
- I preserve my hand with a marginal but decent holding
- I get to see Minni's flop action and infer hand strength from bet size/pattern
- Checking doesn't give away strength and keeps Minni's range wide
- I can comfortably call a reasonable bet here given my outs and pot odds
- This enables better decision-making on subsequent streets with more information

Board is 5♦6♣8♦ — my Q♠3♣ completely missed. I opened from the button and Claude (SB, very deep) just check-called preflop and now checks the flop. This texture is somewhat coordinated and two‑suited (two diamonds), which hits a lot of Claude’s calling range (small pairs, 7/9 straights, diamond draws). Claude has shown a tendency to be sticky and call down/pressure later, so a continuation bet here will frequently be called and can open me to difficult turns with virtually no equity. With no pair and no meaningful draw, I want to keep the pot small and realize my equity in position. Plan: check and see a turn. If Claude bets a small probe (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot) I can call to see improvement; fold to large aggressive bets since I have nothing; if he checks back I can take a free card or fire a small turn-bluff/value only when I actually improve (Q/3) or on a very safe runout. For these reasons I check behind.

Betting the turn with a pair of Queens. The Queen on the turn is excellent for my hand and unlikely to have significantly improved Minni's range given the flop check. I'm representing strength after both players checked the flop - this is a natural continuation point. The 500 chip bet (70% of the 700 chip pot) is reasonable for value extraction, encouraging calls from weaker hands like T-high, 9-high, or flush draws while building the pot when I likely have the best hand. Minni's mid-stack position (12,275) suggests they'll need reasonable odds to continue, and this sizing accomplishes that balance.

I have Q♠3♣ on a Q♣ turn — top pair but a very weak kicker. Pot was 1,200 and Claude (very deep, sticky caller) has bet 500 (~40% pot). This sizing looks like a probe/value-bet with medium pairs or a draw rather than a polar big-value shove. Raising here would bloat the pot with a vulnerable kicker and can get me 3‑bet/raised by stronger hands or jammed by draws; folding is too weak because I beat many hands in his range. Calling 500 keeps the pot under control, uses my position to see the river, and gives correct pot odds to continue against a wide betting range. I’ll re-evaluate on the river — continue vs small bets, and be prepared to fold to large, polarizing aggression that represents sets/straights/flushes.

River value bet with a pair of Queens. Minni's passive checking pattern (especially on a three-diamond board) indicates weakness and likely a hand like a lower pair or missed draw. My pair of Queens is almost certainly ahead. A 2/3 pot bet of 1100 chips extracts value from weaker holdings while remaining balanced. This sizing should get called by worse Qx hands, lower pairs, or draw misses while not overcommitting.

I have Q♠3♣ on 5♦6♣8♦Q♣7♦ — top pair but a very weak kicker. Pot is 2800 and Claude bets 1100; calling 1100 to win 3900 gives ~28% break-even equity. Claude called preflop, checked the flop and then bet a medium-sized turn (500) and now a medium/large-ish river (1100). That line is consistent with a mix of Qx (turn-catch) and some two-pair/sets/straights/flushes, but it also contains many missed diamond draws and weaker hands that I currently beat. Raising would bloat the pot with a vulnerable kicker and risk a shove from hands that dominate me; folding is too passive because I beat a sizable portion of his betting range and the pot odds are reasonable. With position and these pot odds I’ll call 1100 to reach showdown and fold only to a large raise or shove.

Calling Q3o on the button with 1.5:1 immediate pot odds, excellent position, and short stack in the small blind. Position allows me to exploit this marginal hand profitably post-flop against weakened blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit. Claude has limped from the Button. The pot is offering me 5:1 odds (50 to call into a 250 pot). While the hand is marginal and I will be out of position, the price is too good to pass up for a hand that has some connectivity and high-card value in a potentially multi-way limped pot. I will call the 50 to see the flop.

Three-handed with both opponents limping; pot is 300 and I’m on the big blind with 7♣6♠. In 3‑handed play I should be more aggressive and not give two players free flops. 7‑6o is marginal but playable heads‑up and has good postflop playability against one opponent. I want to either take the pot now or play heads‑up with initiative, so I’ll make a modest isolation raise to put pressure on the limpers. Raising to 400 (total) is big enough to generate fold equity—especially versus the shorter SB—yet small relative to stacks so I’m not overcommitting with a marginal hand. If both call I’ll evaluate the flop and continuation‑bet dry boards; if I face a substantial 3‑bet from Claude I’ll give up most of the time since my equity is poor vs a strong 3‑bet range.

Calling Q3o on the button for 300 more with 2:1 pot odds. Position is critical here - I'll act last post-flop against both opponents. Deep stack-to-pot ratio (23,750 in play) gives me excellent implied odds and post-flop playability. Minni's frequent BB raises suggest a moderate range that Q3o has reasonable equity against, and with superior positional leverage, I can win this pot through flop aggression or by outplaying opponents. Flash's short stack (5,375) is not a significant threat to my equity. This is a profitable EV+ situation leveraging position and depth.

I am holding Ten-Eight offsuit in the Small Blind. After Minni's squeeze to 400 from the Big Blind and Claude's call on the button, I am facing a 300-chip call into a 900-chip pot. Although the pot odds are 3:1, T8o is a weak hand that performs poorly out of position in a multi-way raised pot. As the short stack with approximately 50 big blinds, I should prioritize chip preservation and wait for a more favorable spot rather than committing more chips with a marginal hand that is likely dominated. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

I have 7♣6♠ on 7♥6♥ K♦ — I’m holding two pair (7s and 6s) heads‑up vs Claude who called my preflop isolation. This is a strong value hand but vulnerable to sets (KK/77/66) or K‑7/K‑6 two‑pair combinations. Claude’s calling range still contains many worse one‑pair and straight/overcard hands that will pay to see a bet, and there are some two‑heart combos that have a four‑flush draw. I want to extract value and deny equity while keeping the pot controllable, so I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (450). This size charges worse hands and draws, leaves room to fold or call a large check‑raise (which would strongly indicate sets/KK), and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn (especially if a K, 7, 6 or heart arrives).

Q3o is a marginal hand that completely whiffs this K-7-6 flop. Against Minni's bet, I hold only queen-high with minimal equity (likely 20-25%) and face unfavorable 3:1 pot odds. My preflop positional advantage is negated when Minni acts first post-flop. Even if a queen arrives on the turn, it's uncertain whether it's the best hand. The risk-reward profile doesn't support continuing in this spot against an aggressive opponent's flop bet.

I am on the button with 4d 5h, which is a very weak offsuit hand. While I am in position, 4-5 offsuit does not play well against the current stack sizes and the aggressive tendencies shown by Minni and Claude. With about 54 big blinds, I am the shortest stack at the table and prefer to wait for a stronger hand to engage, rather than attempting a steal with a hand that has very little post-flop equity if called.

I have Ac Kc in the small blind and Flash (button) folded, so it's heads-up vs Claude (big blind). AKs is a premium, high-equity hand that plays best when played aggressively preflop. Limping/calling 50 is too passive — it gives Claude good odds to see a flop and lets him realize equity cheaply. I want the initiative and to isolate Claude's wide defending range, so I open to a standard ~3x BB sizing. Raising to 300 (total) builds the pot while keeping plenty of stack behind to play postflop and maximize EV. If Claude folds I scoop the blinds; if he calls I plan to c-bet/continue based on board texture; if he 3-bets large I'll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies.

Folding 95o out of position against an aggressive raiser. While I'm getting decent pot odds (5:1), the hand has poor equity and playability from the BB against Minni's raising range. With a healthy 23,350 stack, I should wait for stronger holdings rather than bleed chips with marginal hands out of position. Minni's aggressive profile makes this an easy fold.

I’m on the button with J♥5♦ facing a preflop raise (to where I must call 100). J5o is a weak, poorly playing hand that’s often dominated by typical opening ranges in 3-handed play. 3-betting would be a pure bluff with bad blockers and calling to see a flop is marginal — I’ll be out of position postflop vs some ranges and my equity is low. With a healthy 11,275 stack I should preserve chips for better spots and stronger holdings, so I fold and wait for a more profitable opportunity.

I'm in the small blind with 4s8c - a very weak hand. While Flash has a short stack (5,275) and is vulnerable, 4-8 offsuit is one of the worst holdings in poker. Even in a heads-up situation with positional advantage, this hand doesn't merit defending. I only have 50 to call, but speculative hands with poor equity should be folded from the small blind. Better to preserve my chip advantage (23,300) and wait for stronger holdings. Flash's short stack will create better opportunities to apply pressure with premium or semi-premium hands.

Pocket sevens on the button is a solid holding that warrants aggression. I'm in an excellent positional advantage with two opponents still to act. Flash is particularly short-stacked at 5,375 chips, making this an ideal moment to apply pressure. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is standard sizing that accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) takes down the blinds immediately if both fold, (2) builds a pot with a hand that plays well in position, (3) keeps flexibility for flop play if called. Minni has been active but is manageable in position. This raise is mathematically sound and strategically optimal given stack dynamics.

I am in the Small Blind with a small pocket pair (3d 3h). The Button (Claude) has raised to 3x the big blind. In a three-handed game, pocket pairs have significant value. While I could 3-bet to try and take the pot immediately, Claude is the chip leader and has been active, making a call more prudent to see a flop and attempt to set-mine without bloating the pot out of position. I will call the 250 additional chips to match the 300 total bet.

Facing a button open to 300 with the small blind calling, I’m in the big blind with Q♥5♦ — a weak, easily dominated hand with poor playability out of position in a multiway pot. Calling 200 into a ~700 pot requires roughly 22% equity, which Q5o rarely has against a button open and a caller. 3-betting as a light bluff is possible but undesirable here because Flash (SB) is already in and can call, leaving me OOP and in tough spots postflop. I’ll conserve chips and fold to wait for a better spot.

I have a pocket pair of threes on a 10-6-4 rainbow board. My hand did not improve to a set, and I am currently holding an underpair to both the Ten and the Six. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Claude, the most standard and disciplined play is to check and see how he reacts. Claude has a massive chip lead and has been very active, so I expect him to continuation-bet frequently. I will check and evaluate based on his bet sizing, but I am prepared to fold to significant pressure given the vulnerability of my hand.

Pocket sevens on a relatively dry T-6-4 flop is a solid holding. Flash is extremely short-stacked at 5,125 chips and checked the flop, indicating weakness. Betting 450 (roughly 2/3 of the 700 chip pot) maintains my pre-flop aggression, builds the pot with a strong hand, and puts critical pressure on Flash's diminishing stack. This is a standard continuation bet with good equity and position.

I have an underpair (3-3) on a 10-6-4 rainbow board. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor and chip leader, has made a standard continuation bet of 450 into a 700 pot. While Claude could be betting with a wide range of hands including air or overcards, my hand is extremely vulnerable and has very little equity to improve. Being out of position makes it difficult to realize whatever small value I might have. I am facing a bet that represents nearly 9% of my remaining stack, and continuing here would likely lead to more difficult decisions on the turn and river. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am on the button with pocket fours. In a three-handed game, any pocket pair is a strong opening hand, especially from the button. My stack is the shortest at the table, so I need to look for opportunities to pick up blinds and antes or build a pot when I have a structural advantage. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. If I get re-raised by the larger stacks, I will have to evaluate based on the size of the raise, but folding would be too passive given the strength of a pair in this position.

I’m in the small blind with K♠8♥. Flash (button) opened to 300 — a standard, fairly wide button raise in a three-handed game. With deep stacks (~111 BB) and Claude (BB) likely to defend, a small 3‑bet (min to 500) will often just invite a multiway pot or a caller from the BB and I don’t have the hand to build a big bloated pot out of position. K8o is good enough to defend but not a 3‑bet bluff candidate here. I’ll call the 300 total (put in 250 more) to see a flop, realize equity against a wide raising range, and proceed cautiously post‑flop — folding to heavy resistance unless I hit a top pair or a strong draw.

Calling with 6cQs for 200 more into a 700 chip pot with three-way action. The pot odds (4:1) justify the marginal hand, and I retain flexibility post-flop with my larger stack advantage. Flash's short stack and Minni's revealed hand values make this a profitable call situation.

I called preflop with K♠8♥ to see a flop; the 9♥7♦7♠ board leaves me with K-high and no real draw (no pair, no meaningful straight/flush outs). I'm first to act and out of position to both opponents, and betting into two players with almost no equity would be a thin bluff with low fold equity. I’ll check to control the pot and realize my (small) showdown equity — plan is to fold to a meaningful c-bet, but I may call a very small probe to see a turn and re-evaluate if I pair or pick up a draw.

Checking with Q6o on a paired flop (9h7d7s). This hand has no draws and marginal showdown value. With Minni having checked from the small blind, controlling pot size is prudent. Flash will act next from the button, and I can make a more informed decision based on their action. Betting with this weak holding would be overplaying a marginal hand.

The flop of 9h-7d-7s is a paired board where my pocket fours have marginal showdown value. Since I was the pre-flop raiser and both opponents have checked to me, I will check back to control the pot size and see a free turn card. Betting into two opponents on this texture is risky as it's hard to get better hands to fold, and I'd like to reach showdown as cheaply as possible or potentially hit a set on the turn.

Out of position with K♠8♥ on a dry, paired 9♥-7♦-7♠-4♥ board and no pair or realistic draw. Both opponents checked the flop (and turn action is to me), so there’s little fold equity betting into two players and I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, realize my (small) showdown equity, and fold to any meaningful bet. If it checks through to the river I’ll re-evaluate based on the river card and any bets; I’ll only call very small probes but avoid getting committed without improvement.

Checking with Q6o on a 9h7d7s4h board. This hand has no made hand, no draws, and marginal showdown value at best. Both opponents have shown weakness through multiple checks. Betting would overcommit chips with a weak holding. Checking controls pot size and allows me to see the river before making a final decision. With my large stack advantage, taking showdown value is more appropriate than forcing action with this marginal hand.

I have turned a full house (Fours full of Sevens). After the flop checked through, my hand is well-disguised. Now that I have a premium hand, I must start building the pot to get value from hands like a Nine, pocket pairs between 4 and 7, or even straight draws. A bet of 600 into the 900 pot is large enough to build the pot but looks enough like a delayed steal or a semi-bluff to potentially get called by weaker hands.

I entered this hand with K♠8♥ out of position and planned to check and fold to any meaningful turn aggression. Flash’s delayed turn raise to 600 into a 1500 pot (~40% pot) after both opponents checked is a believable value bet (represents a 9, a 7, two‑pair, or a strong one‑pair hand) and there’s still Claude to act behind. I have no pair and only 6 outs (three kings + three eights) to improve on the river — ~13% chance — while the pot odds to call are roughly 1500:600 (~2.5:1) meaning I’d need ~28.6% equity to justify a call. My raw equity is well below that, and raising as a bluff OOP into two players with K8o is not constructive. Preserve the stack for better spots and fold.

Folding Q6o on the turn facing a 600 chip raise from Flash after all-check flop action. This hand has marginal showdown value (~20-25% equity) against Flash's range, which likely contains 9s, 4s, pocket pairs, or stronger holdings given the turn raise. Pot odds of 3.5:1 don't justify calling with such a weak hand. Flash's short stack and aggressive play pattern suggest genuine strength rather than pure bluff. With a comfortable 23400 stack, disciplined folding of marginal hands maximizes long-term EV. No value in continuing with Q6 high.

T3o (Tc3d) is a very weak, unsuited, non‑connected hand. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call — that gives ~40% pot odds, but versus an opener’s range in 3‑handed play my equity with T3o is well below that (likely <25%), so calling is a negative‑EV play. The raiser is likely a fairly strong/stealing range and the leftover player (Flash) has a relatively short stack that could shove or fold, creating awkward multi‑way spots. I’ll preserve my stack on the button for a better spot rather than calling or bluff‑raising with a dominated hand. Fold.

K2o is too weak to play. Even with heads-up position and a chip advantage, K2o is a clear fold from the small blind. The 2 kicker is problematic, and there's no value in risking further chips with such a marginal hand when I can wait for better opportunities. Disciplined folding is essential in tournament play.

7-2 offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Despite having positional advantage on the button, this hand lacks the fundamental qualities needed to profitably enter the pot: no pair, no connectivity, no suited cards, and extremely limited straight potential (only makes the 5-6-7-8-9 straight, the worst possible).

Even in position, calling 100 into a 150 chip pot against two opponents with 7-2o creates a difficult situation post-flop where we'd rarely have the best hand and would likely fold to any aggression. The risk/reward ratio is unfavorable.

Fold is the standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-5 offsuit. The Button (Claude) has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind (Minni). While it is only 50 more to call, 9-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. As the shortest stack at the table, I need to be more selective with the hands I play to avoid chip leakage. Folding is the most disciplined play here.

In a 3-handed game, the Button should open with a wide range of hands to take advantage of position. King-Seven offsuit (K7o) is a standard opening hand in this spot, as it is ahead of many random hands the blinds might hold. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open-raise size that puts pressure on Minni and Claude while allowing me to play the pot in position if called.

K♦3♦ is a very marginal offsuit king with a terrible kicker. I'm in the small blind and would be out of position to both the button (who opened to 300) and the big blind postflop. Calling 250 to see a flop likely creates a multiway pot where K3o is frequently dominated and has poor equity; 3-betting to 500 would be an overly thin bluff and easily exploited. With healthy stacks and plenty of hands left in the tournament, preserving chips for better spots (or stronger Kx hands) is the right play here. Fold.

6-5 offsuit is too weak to defend in the big blind against a raise, even with Flash's short stack. The hand lacks playability and I'm out of position post-flop. With a healthy 23k stack, I should fold and wait for better spots. Pot odds don't justify playing such a weak hand with poor equity.

I have J♠2♥ on the button in a 3-handed game. J2o is a weak hand, but on the button I can open a wide range to exploit position and steal the blinds. Opponents: Claude (SB) is deep and aggressive; Flash (BB) is the shortest stack (~57 BB) and will defend sometimes. I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300) to maximize fold equity and keep the pot manageable if I get called. Plan: fold to a sizeable 3‑bet (I don’t want to play J2o against heavy preflop aggression); if called I’ll play postflop in position — c-bet on many dry flops, and proceed cautiously or check back on coordinated boards. This sizing is a good balance of pressure and risk given the stacks and table dynamics.

Raising with pocket eights from small blind after Minni's button raise. Pocket eights is a solid medium pair that plays well in position post-flop. The 3-bet to 800 applies pressure to both Minni's wide opening range and Flash's big blind position, while also building a pot favorable for a pocket pair. My deep stack gives me significant leverage in this spot.
