I am holding AK suited (diamonds) in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the button and Claude 3-bet from the small blind. AKs is a premium hand that performs very well against wide opening and 3-betting ranges. With my stack size of approximately 58 big blinds, I should look to 4-bet for value and to seize the initiative. A cold 4-bet here is a strong signal, but given Claude's frequency and the button's wide range, it is the standard play. I will raise to 2200 to build the pot and potentially end the hand immediately.

Fold. I opened wide on the button with J♠2♥, but both opponents showed heavy preflop aggression: Claude 3-bet to 800 and Flash shoved/4-bet to 2200. Calling 1,900 more into a 3,300 pot would require ~36.5% equity — J2o has far less against their 3-bet/4-bet ranges, especially multiway. Flash is short (≈1,475 left) and could shove any further action, and Claude is deep and capable of committing with a stronger range. A raise/bluff here is poor: it risks a shove or a call from Claude when we are dominated. Preserving ~10.6k chips for better, higher-expected-value spots is the correct play.

Calling Flash's 4-bet with pocket eights. Stack is short (~1,475 left) making them essentially committed. Against Flash's range of 4-bet hands (likely QQ, JJ, TT, AK, AQ), pocket eights has approximately 35-45% equity depending on their exact holdings. Pot odds require ~30% equity, which pocket eights exceeds. Early tournament with healthy stack (22,450) makes this a mathematically sound call with an all-in draw.

Betting 2,400 on the flop with pocket eights. Flash is short-stacked (3,675 remaining) and nearly committed. His 4-bet pre-flop range contains many hands where I have solid equity (AK, medium pairs, etc.). This bet applies maximum pressure while sizing appropriately relative to the 4,700 pot. Against his short stack, even if marginally behind to the top of his range (QQ, AQ), the bet forces an all-in decision and I'm closing the action. My 21,050 stack gives me room to absorb a loss here and maintain flexibility post-hand.

I have a very strong hand on this flop with the nut flush draw and two overcards (Ace and King). The pre-flop action (4-betting) significantly narrowed ranges, and while Claude's lead on a Queen-high paired board is strong, I have massive equity against almost his entire range, including hands like Aces, Kings, or Ace-Queen. With only 3675 chips remaining and a pot that has already grown to 7100, the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is approximately 0.5. At this SPR, I am mathematically committed to the pot with a draw this strong. Shoving for my remaining 3675 chips is the most effective play to maximize my equity and put pressure on Claude's marginal holdings like pocket Jacks or Tens.

Flash is all-in with 3,675 (shoving on my flop bet). I need to call 1,275 more to reach their total of 3,675.

Equity analysis:
- My hand: Pocket eights (8c 8h)
- Board: 2d Qd 2s (paired board with queen)
- Flash's 4-bet pre-flop range: Likely QQ, JJ, TT, 99, AK, AQ, occasionally AQs/suited broadway hands
- My equity: Against this mixed range, pocket eights likely has 35-45% equity. Even against the premium portion (QQ), I have ~18-22% equity

Pot odds:
- Current pot: 10,775
- To call: 1,275
- Odds: 10,775:1,275 = 8.45:1, requiring ~10.6% equity to break even
- My equity significantly exceeds this threshold

Stack considerations:
- Remaining stack after call: 17,375 (more than half my total)
- If I win, I eliminate Flash and gain significant stack advantage
- If I lose, I'm still reasonably positioned with 12,000+ in chips

This is a mathematically sound call with favorable pot odds and positive expected value against Flash's range. The board (paired deuces, queen high) doesn't significantly favor Flash's range over my pocket eights.

Calling 100 with Q♣6♣ from the button. Position is my greatest asset here - I can play in-position post-flop against two opponents and control the action. Q-6 suited has decent playability (broadway card, flush draw potential). The pot odds (1.5:1) are acceptable, and calling preserves my stack flexibility while allowing me to leverage positional advantage. This is a standard play that takes advantage of button strength without overcommitting marginal holdings.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 8s, a strong suited connector. Claude has limped from the button for 100. With a 'to call' amount of only 50 chips, this hand is too strong to fold and plays very well in multi-way pots. I will complete the bet to see a flop and evaluate my strength from there, keeping the pot size controlled.

I have 7♦4♠ in the big blind in a limp pot (300). This is a very weak, unsuited hand and it’s multiway against players who have shown aggression in earlier hands (Claude especially). There’s no reason to invest more preflop — check to see a cheap flop. Plan: fold to sizable aggression on most flops unless I make a pair or pick up a strong draw; consider a small bluff only on very favorable, uncontested runouts.

On a flop of Q-9-6, I have flopped middle pair (nines). While I have some showdown value, the board contains an overcard (the Queen). In a three-way limped pot, I want to keep the pot small and see how my opponents react. Claude's button limp could contain many marginal hands, and Minni in the big blind is also a wildcard. I will check to see if anyone bets, intending to likely call a standard-sized bet to re-evaluate on the turn.

This is a multiway limp pot on a Q♠ 9♦ 6♥ flop and I hold 7♦4♠ — no pair, no meaningful draw (only a backdoor diamond). With two opponents left, and both capable of aggression from the history, my hand has almost zero equity and very poor bluffing prospects multiway. There’s no reason to invest chips here: check and concede to any sizable bet. If checked around to the turn I’ll re-evaluate only if I pick up a pair or a clear draw; otherwise fold to pressure. Therefore I will check and preserve my stack.

Betting 200 on the flop with two pair (Queens and Sixes). Both opponents checked, indicating weakness. My hand is strong enough to build value against potential draws, lower pairs, or weak broadway hands. The 2/3 pot sizing is standard and designed to extract chips while maintaining balanced play - not so large that it polarizes my range, but confident enough to reflect the strength of two pair in position.

I have flopped middle pair (nines) with a respectable kicker in a three-way limped pot. The board is Q-9-6 rainbow. Claude, who limped from the button, has led out for 200 into a 300-chip pot. This bet size suggests he might have a piece of the board or is attempting to take it down. Middle pair is a classic 'bluff-catcher' and showdown value hand that doesn't want to bloat the pot but is too strong to fold to a single bet. Calling allows me to see a turn card and evaluate Minni's reaction behind me. If Minni raises, I will likely have to fold, but a call is the most standard play here to keep the pot manageable while remaining in the hand.

Fold. I have 7♦4♠ on Q♠9♦6♥ — no pair and only a remote backdoor diamond. Claude’s raise to 200 in a multiway limp pot and Flash’s call strongly reduce my fold equity and make a successful bluff unlikely. Pot odds (call 200 into ~700) require roughly 22% equity; this hand’s actual equity vs two calling/raising ranges is far below that. Raising as a bluff here is poor multiway and calling to see a turn is a low-expected-value play. Preserve the 10k+ stack for a better spot against fewer opponents or with real equity.

On the turn, the board pairs with a second Queen. I currently hold two pair (Queens and Nines) with an eight kicker. Since I am out of position against Claude, who was the aggressor on the flop, checking is the most prudent move. My hand has significant showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value, as I would likely only be called by a Queen or a Nine with a better kicker. By checking, I control the size of the pot and give Claude the opportunity to continue any bluffs or check back with weaker hands.

Betting 450 on the turn with Queens full of Sixes. Flash checked, indicating weakness. My full house is nearly the nuts and deserves value extraction. A 2/3 pot bet (450 into 700) is standard sizing - large enough to build the pot and get called by weaker pairs or broadway cards, but not so large that it only attracts strong holdings. This maintains consistent aggression while extracting maximum value from Flash's marginal holdings. The turn card (second Queen) significantly strengthens my hand from two pair to a full house, making this a clear value bet situation.

On the turn, the second Queen appears, meaning I now have Queens and Nines. Claude's continuation bet of 450 into a pot of 700 is significant, but the board pairing actually makes it slightly less likely that he holds a Queen. Given his pre-flop limp on the button, his range is wide and could include middle pairs, straight draws (like 7-8 or 5-7), or air. While I am vulnerable to a better Nine or a Queen, my hand is too strong to fold to a single bet in a limped pot. I will call to see the river and evaluate. Raising would likely only get called by hands that beat me (Qx or A9/K9/J9/T9), so calling is the best way to keep the pot manageable while realizing my showdown value.

I have a Full House, Nines full of Queens (9-9-9-Q-Q). This is a very strong hand, but it is not the nuts. Any Queen in Claude's hand makes a better Full House (Queens full of Nines). Claude limped from the button and followed up with bets on both the flop and the turn, which is consistent with a wide range including Qx, 9x, or even pocket pairs and draws. Since the river paired the Nine, I now beat all of his non-Queen hands (like 6x or pocket pairs), and I chop with any other Nine. There is little value in betting myself, as a Nine will likely just call and result in a split pot, while a Queen will raise me. By checking, I allow Claude to continue bluffing with hands that have missed their draws or to value-bet a worse hand, while also controlling the cost if he does indeed have the Queen.

River value bet with Queens full of Nines. Flash has shown weakness by checking a river where most of their likely holding (pair of Q, 9, or 6) loses to my full house. The 900 bet (56% of pot) is a standard value extraction sizing - large enough to build the pot but not so large that it only gets called by strong hands. Flash has demonstrated willingness to call down throughout this hand (called flop and turn bets), making this a profitable value bet in a tournament context where every chip matters. The hand strength combined with opponent tendencies makes this a clear value situation.

I have Nines full of Queens (9-9-9-Q-Q). The board is 9d-6h-Qs-Qd-9h and I hold the 9s. There is only one Nine left in the deck. Claude limped from the button and has bet every street. While my hand is very strong, any Queen in Claude's hand (which is well within his button-limping range like Q-7, Q-8, Q-J) makes a superior full house (Queens full of Nines). If I raise, I am almost exclusively called by a Queen that beats me or the last Nine which results in a chop. There is no value in raising because weaker hands like a 6 or a bluff will fold. Calling allows me to win against bluffs or missed draws and chop if he somehow has the last Nine, while minimizing my losses if he has the Queen.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of sixes. In a 3-handed game, pocket pairs are strong starting hands and should almost always be opened for a raise when folded to on the button to take control of the pot and utilize positional advantage. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here.

I'm in the small blind with K♠T♠ facing a button open to 300 in a 3-handed game. Button opening ranges are very wide here and KTs is a strong, playable hand with good equity and useful blockers — calling keeps me out of position and lets the big blind enter cheaply. I want to seize the initiative and isolate the opener, so I will 3-bet rather than just call. I raise to 1000 total: large enough to generate fold equity and charge speculative hands, but small enough to leave deep stacks for postflop play. If Claude 4-bets big I'll re-evaluate (likely fold to extreme aggression); if called I'll proceed postflop with a strong, playable holding.

T8o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet out of position. Minni's re-raise to 1000 from the small blind suggests a strong range (pairs, AK, AQ). I don't have the equity or position to justify the 900 call. Folding preserves stack for premium opportunities.

I opened for 300 from the button with pocket sixes, a strong hand in 3-handed play. Minni responded with a 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. With pot odds of 3:1 (700 to call for a 2100 total pot) and the advantage of position throughout the rest of the hand, calling is the most profitable play. It allows me to set-mine or play the hand based on board texture and her post-flop behavior without bloating the pot further pre-flop against a potentially stronger range.

Situation: heads-up after my preflop 3-bet to 1000; pot is 2100. I hold K♠T♠ on J♥ 2♥ J♣. Assessment: KTs has almost no showdown value on a paired jack board and cannot make a flush (no spades on board). Flash’s calling range to my 3-bet will include lots of broadways, Ax, and medium pocket pairs — some of which hit this flop (Jx, sets, 2x) and many of which missed. Plan: use my preflop initiative to apply pressure with a small continuation bet to fold out pure air/overcards and some weak broadways while keeping the pot manageable if called. I want a size that buys the pot often but preserves chips to fold cheaply to turn aggression from hands that actually beat me. Execution: bet ~1/3 pot (700). If he folds I take down 2100. If he calls I will re-evaluate on the turn and fold to sizable aggression unless I improve (K/T or a heart that changes dynamics). If he raises, I’ll respect that as strong and fold.

I have pocket sixes on a paired board [Jh, 2h, Jc]. Minni's 3-bet pre-flop suggests a strong range, but her 1/3 pot lead on the flop is a standard continuation bet that could represent trips, overpairs, or simply overcards like AK/AQ. Given the excellent pot odds of 4:1 and my positional advantage, calling is the most appropriate play. It allows me to see a turn card and evaluate her aggression level without committing too many chips to a board where I am currently behind her value range but ahead of her bluffs.

I did not improve on the turn — I still have K♠T♠ on J♥ 2♥ J♣ 3♥. My flop c‑bet was small and got called, so Flash’s continuing range is heavy in Jx/sets, medium pairs and broadway combos and includes some two‑heart combos that now make a flush. With essentially no showdown value and the 3rd heart completing some of his better holdings, betting for fold equity here is weak and would only inflate a pot I’m a big favorite to lose. Pot control is the right play: check and fold to meaningful aggression (I’ll re‑evaluate on the river only if checked to or if I pair/improve).

Minni's check on the turn after 3-betting pre-flop and leading the flop suggests she is either giving up on a bluff (like AK/AQ), pot-controlling an overpair (QQ/KK/AA), or potentially trapping with a Jack or a completed flush. My pocket sixes have moderate showdown value but are not strong enough to bet for value, and betting won't reliably force out better hands like overpairs. By checking behind, I keep the pot manageable, realize my equity, and prepare to see a river where I can evaluate whether to call a final bet or check down for a showdown against her missed overcards.

Board: J♥ 2♥ J♣ 3♥ Q♠. I hold K♠T♠, pot 3,500, action on me. Line: I 3‑bet pre, c‑bet small on the J22 two‑heart flop and was called, both checked the turn when the third heart came in. Villain’s continuing range after that line is heavy in Jx (trips/top pair), medium pocket pairs, Qx/Kx/broadways and the heart‑flush combos that completed on the turn. The river Q improves many hands in his calling range (Qx and QJ two‑pair, plus lots of hands that already beat me: trips and flushes). My hand has essentially no showdown value and I don’t block many of the hands that would call a bluff. Betting requires significant fold equity to be +EV (e.g. a ~50% pot bet would need ~33% folds); given his flop call and turn check I don’t expect that fold frequency. I will check and preserve chips — fold to meaningful river aggression. If he somehow checks back we lose the pot but avoid a marginal bluff against a range that largely beats me.

On the river, the board is Jh-2h-Jc-3h-Qs. I have pocket sixes. Minni 3-bet pre-flop, led the flop, and then checked both the turn and the river. Her turn check on a board where the flush completed (3h) suggests she might be pot-controlling an overpair or has given up with missed overcards. The river Queen (Qs) is a scare card that hits some of her range (AQ, QQ). My pocket sixes have some showdown value against her missed AK or lower pocket pairs, but they are not strong enough to bet for value. Betting would likely only result in being called by better hands and folding out worse hands. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and reach showdown for free in a pot that is already quite large.

I’m on the button with 7♠5♣ and ~87.8bb effective stacks in a 3-handed pot. This is a marginal but playable hand in position — I want to seize initiative and either steal the blinds or go heads-up to play postflop in position. A 3x open (to 300) is a good balance: it applies pressure, reduces callers from the blinds and avoids giving them good odds to see a cheap flop. I’ll fold to a large 3-bet (I’m not committed with 7-5 offsuit), but if called I’ll use position to c-bet on favorable low/connected boards, control the pot on dry A/K-high flops, and extract value if I make a pair. Raising to 300 preserves fold equity while keeping the pot manageable given my hand’s limited playability.

Premium pocket kings in the small blind warrant an aggressive 3-bet re-raise against Minni's button raise. Raising to 1000 (approximately 3x her 300 raise) builds the pot while I have the best hand, extracts value, and defines my hand strength. This is a standard poker play that generates profit against a wide button raising range.

I am holding 7d 3h in the Big Blind, which is a very weak hand. The action has been a raise from the button to 300 followed by a 3-bet from the small blind to 1000. This shows significant strength from my opponents, and my hand is essentially unplayable in this situation. Folding is the only sensible move to preserve my stack.

Claude 3‑bet to 1000 after my 300 button open (pot 1400, I must call 700). 7♠5♣ is a weak offsuit connector with poor equity and poor post‑flop playability versus a 3‑betting SB range; to call I need ~33% equity, which this hand rarely has. A 4‑bet bluff here is too risky with deep stacks and Claude’s shown aggression — I don’t want to get committed in difficult multi‑street spots with such a marginal holding. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot in position.

Ac6c on the button is a marginal hand, but calling from the button has significant positional merit. With only 100 chips to call (0.5% of my stack) and action remaining after the blinds, I can play this hand profitably with position advantage. The ace provides reasonable high-card value, and I'll be last to act post-flop, which greatly improves my flexibility. Both opponents' stacks are relatively shallow, so if I catch an ace or strong draw, I'll have good value. Folding would be overly tight given my button position and minimal commitment required.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 8d. Claude has limped from the button. The pot is 250 and it costs me only 50 more to call. This gives me 5:1 pot odds (or 6:1 once I call) to see a flop. While J8o is not a premium hand, the price is too good to pass up, even out of position. I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate based on the board texture.

I have J♥2♦ — a weak jack with a very poor kicker. No one raised preflop and the pot is already 300 with me facing no additional bet, so there's no reason to force the issue. I'll check to see a flop cheaply and only continue with a pair, a strong draw, or a favorable board; otherwise fold to meaningful pressure to protect my stack.

On a very dry, paired board (5s 5d 2h) in a limped three-way pot, I have no piece of the board with Jack-high. The best play here is to check and see what my opponents do. Betting into two players on this texture is unlikely to succeed as a bluff without significant equity or a plan for multiple streets, and I have neither. I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression.

The flop is 5♠-5♦-2♥ and my hand J♥2♦ gives me two pair (fives and twos) with a Jack kicker. That is a strong holding on this paired board versus the wide checking ranges we saw preflop, but I'm still vulnerable to trips/quads (any 5 or a pocket pair of 2s) or a 2 with a higher kicker. Both opponents checked the flop, suggesting weakness — good spot to get value from worse pairs/underpairs and hands that will call (K2, weaker two-pairs with worse kicker, pocket pairs like 3s/4s/6s that might call). I will bet a modest, value-extracting size of about half the pot to charge these hands while keeping the pot manageable versus a possible check-raise from Claude (who has shown aggression). If I face a small raise I will re-evaluate and likely call to see the turn; a large, polarized check-raise will make me proceed with caution since it often represents trips.

Calling with Ac6c on 5s5d2h. I have a powerful nine-nut flush draw offering approximately 30-35% equity against Minni's likely range. Pot odds of 3:1 (needing only 25% equity) make this mathematically sound. Position on the button, both opponents' shallow stacks, and the potential that Minni may have a marginal hand all support the call. I maintain flexibility for future streets while getting direct odds on my draw.

I have Jack-high on a paired, dry board. Minni has bet half-pot into two opponents and Claude has called. While the pot odds are attractive (5:1), I am out of position against two players and my hand has very little equity or potential to improve to the best hand. Calling would likely lead to more difficult decisions on the turn and river without a clear path to winning. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have two pair (5s and 2s) with a Jack kicker on 5♠-5♦-2♥-3♥. I bet the flop and Claude called; Flash folded, so Claude’s calling range is likely underpairs and single-pair hands (and the occasional 2x or slow-played 5x). The 3♥ on the turn is mostly a blank for that calling range except it completes pocket 3s into trips, so trips is my main fear. I want to extract value from worse one-pair/underpair hands and deny a free river card, while keeping the pot sensible against a possible large check-raise. I’ll make a modest value bet of roughly half the pot (≈300). If Claude check-raises big I will re-evaluate and likely fold to a polarized shove; if he simply calls I keep the initiative and evaluate the river.

Calling with Ac6c on a paired board (5s5d2h3h). I have a 9-nut flush draw with approximately 32% equity to hit by river. Pot odds of 3:1 require only 25% equity, making this a clear +EV call. Position on the button provides crucial decision-making advantage on the river. Minni's relatively small turn bet (300 into 600 pot) suggests a marginal holding. Against Minni's likely range of mid-pair or weak draws, my flush draw equity is strong. The call maintains flexibility while getting favorable mathematical odds.

I have two pair (5s and 2s) with a Jack kicker on 5♠-5♦-2♥-3♥-9♦. Claude called my flop and turn bets and has shown a passive call-call line here — his range is likely underpairs, one-pair hands (Kx/Qx), and some weaker 2x holdings; trips/slow-played 5x are possible but less likely given his line. Pot is 1,200 and I'm facing no bet, so I want to extract value from worse hands while keeping the pot reasonable in case I’m up against trips. A half-pot value bet (≈600) is small enough to get called by worse hands and large enough to charge draws/bluffs; if Claude check-raises big I will strongly suspect trips/full house and fold. Given my stack (7,925) this is a low-risk, high-expected-value play.

River decision with Ac6c on 5s5d2h3h9d. I have a pair of fives with an ace kicker. Pot odds are 4:1 (calling 600 to win 2400), requiring only 20% equity to break even. Against Minni's likely range of value hands and bluffs, I estimate 20-35% equity. Minni's aggressive line throughout the hand, particularly the small turn bet (300 into 600), suggests a medium-strength holding rather than a premium hand. The call is mathematically justified and maintains flexibility. My stack position (18,875) allows this marginal call as part of optimal tournament play over 150 hands.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with King-Eight offsuit (K8o). This is a standard opening hand from the button. Opening with a raise allows me to take control of the pot, put pressure on the blinds (Minni and Claude), and potentially win the pot immediately. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard sizing in this tournament structure.

Facing a button open to 300. I’m in the small blind with 2♣6♦ (already posted 50), must call 250 into a 450 pot. Calling would make the pot 700, so I’d need ~35.7% equity to break even (250/700). 2‑6 offsuit has essentially no equity vs a button opening range and plays very poorly out of position, especially with the big blind (Claude) behind who can peel and make it multiway. With ~97 BB effective I can wait for a much better spot — no reason to defend with junk. Fold.

Calling with pocket fours in the big blind against a button raise. Good pot odds (2.25:1), marginal hand with set mining potential. Heads-up post-flop allows for position-based decisions. Small relative investment (200 chips) with healthy stack (18,175). Avoid raising with marginal equity and will evaluate flop texture carefully.

Pocket fours on 8c Jc 3s is an underpair with limited showdown value. Flash's button raise could represent a wide range (AK, AQ, KQ, overpairs, draws). My equity against this range is approximately 30-40%, making this a marginal hand.

Checking is the optimal play because:
1. Out-of-position in a heads-up scenario, I want to control pot size and gather information
2. If Flash barrels, I can evaluate pot odds for calling or folding based on their betting pattern
3. If Flash checks back, I maintain flexibility with set mining possibilities on turn/river
4. Betting would commit chips with marginal equity and invite aggression from an aggressive button raiser
5. With a healthy stack (17,975), I can afford to see what Flash does while minimizing downside risk

This is a set-mining/value-gathering check that positions me for better decision-making on later streets.

On this flop of Jc-8c-3s, I have flopped middle pair with a King kicker (K8o). Claude, the big blind, checked to me. This board is relatively coordinated with a flush draw and potential straight draws (T9, QT). While middle pair is often a candidate for checking back to control the pot, betting for value and protection is also a strong option here against a wide big blind defending range. Claude has shown a tendency to be sticky and call down with marginal hands in previous pots (Hand #47). I will place a continuation bet of 350 (slightly over half-pot) to extract value from flush draws, straight draws, or smaller pairs, and to prevent overcards like Ace or Queen from seeing a free turn.

Calling 350 into favorable pot odds (3.85:1) with pocket fours. While currently behind in equity (~30% against Flash's range), I have two more cards to improve with set mining potential (~8.5% per street). Stack size advantage and Flash's aggressive tendencies make this a mathematically sound call out of position, gathering information for turn decision-making.

Flash's check on the turn is a significant signal of weakness. This is a key moment to take advantage.

Current situation analysis:
- Board: 8c Jc 3s 5s (relatively dry, no obvious draws completed)
- Flash bet the flop aggressively (350 into ~700 pot) but checked the turn
- Turn check suggests Flash missed their draw or has a marginal hand they're not confident about
- My pocket fours are currently an underpair, but have set-mining equity (2 outs = ~8.5% chance to improve)

Strategic reasoning for betting:
1. **Exploit weakness**: Flash's check indicates vulnerability. I should capitalize on this moment rather than check back and go to showdown passively.
2. **Win the pot now**: A reasonable bet of 500 (roughly 1/3 of the current pot) can take down the hand immediately or build the pot if Flash calls with a weaker holding.
3. **Gather information**: If Flash calls, I'll have a better sense of their hand strength for the river decision.
4. **Stack size allows aggression**: With 17,625 chips, I can afford to take calculated risks. A 500 bet is only 2.8% of my stack.
5. **Pot odds**: Even if Flash calls and I miss the river, the final pot size will give me reasonable odds for river decisions if needed.

This aggressive check-raise exploitation is standard poker play when an opponent shows weakness on the turn. Flash's line (flop aggression → turn check) doesn't indicate a strong hand.

Claude has led out with a 'donk bet' of 500 into a 1400 pot on the turn. This small sizing (about 1/3 pot) often represents a marginal hand like a weak Jack, a lower pair, or a draw attempting to set a cheap price to see the river. My middle pair (8s) with a King kicker is ahead of his draws and many of his possible lower pairs. Folding is out of the question given the pot odds (nearly 4:1). Raising is risky because Claude has shown stickiness in previous hands and likely only continues with a Jack or better, or a strong draw. Calling is the most prudent play to keep the pot controlled, allow him to continue bluffing with draws, and see a river where I can evaluate based on his sizing and the final card.

River decision with pocket fours on 8c Jc 3s 5s Qc.

Current situation analysis:
- I have pocket fours (4d 4s), the lowest possible pair on this board
- Board: 8c Jc 3s 5s Qc (relatively coordinated, multiple overcards)
- Pot: 2,350
- Flash called my turn raise to 500
- Stack: 17,125 (healthy position)

Hand strength evaluation:
- Pocket fours is extremely weak here. The Queen on the river gave Flash multiple potential improvements if they held overcards or broadway cards
- Flash's action sequence (flop raise → turn check → call my turn raise) suggests they have a made hand, likely a pair or better
- My hand has minimal showdown value and virtually no bluff equity given Flash's demonstrated strength

Strategic reasoning for checking:
1. **Weak showdown value**: Pocket fours is the weakest hand without improvement. Against Flash's likely holdings (pairs, two-pair, etc.), I'm behind
2. **No credible bluff**: After showing strength with my turn raise, another river bet would polarize my range incorrectly. Flash already called my turn aggression, so they won't fold to river action with a made hand
3. **Escape route**: Checking allows me to fold to a river bet without further loss, or get to showdown cheaply if Flash checks back
4. **Pot control**: With marginal equity, I want to minimize losses. My turn raise was exploitative against weakness; the river has no draw-completing cards to represent
5. **Stack preservation**: At 17,125 chips, preserving my stack is more valuable than risking additional chips on a losing hand

This is a disciplined decision to avoid spilling more chips with a weak holding on an unfavorable river card.

On the river, the Queen of clubs completes a potential club flush and is an overcard to my pair of eights. Claude, who led the turn with a small bet, has now checked. My pair of eights has decent showdown value against missed straight draws or lower pairs, but it is unlikely to be called by anything worse if I bet. Betting would only isolate me against Jacks, Queens, or flushes. Therefore, checking back to take a free showdown is the most profitable and safest play.

Button with J♣3♠ — a weak, non‑suited hand but I'm in position against the blinds and the price to see a flop is small. Both opponents have shown willingness to 3‑bet/raise, and raising now (min to 200) could get me into a bloated pot with a poor hand and out of position against aggression. With ~97BB effective I can afford to see flops and play postflop in position, folding to significant resistance if I miss. I will call the 100 to keep options open and use position to navigate the flop.

Folding 8-3 offsuit from the small blind. This is one of the worst hands in poker with minimal equity against two opponents. While I only need to call 50 more, the hand lacks sufficient equity to justify playing out of position. With a healthy stack, I should preserve chips and wait for better opportunities.

I am in the big blind holding Ten-Seven of diamonds. Minni limped from the button and Claude folded his small blind. T7s is a decent speculative hand that plays well in multi-way pots, but since this is heads-up and I will be out of position post-flop, checking to see a free flop is the most prudent play. This keeps the pot small and allows me to realize my equity without additional risk.

On this flop of J-7-6, I have middle pair with a Ten kicker (7d, Td) and a backdoor diamond draw. Since the pot was limped pre-flop and I am out of position, the best strategy is to check and evaluate Minni's response. My hand is strong enough to call a standard-sized bet, but not necessarily strong enough to lead out for value against a range that could contain a Jack or a slow-played monster. Checking keeps the pot small and allows me to see how the hand develops.

I have top pair (J♣) with a very weak kicker (3♠) on a relatively dry J‑7‑6 rainbow flop. I’m in position and the big blind checked, which indicates a wide, often passive defending range (lots of worse Jx, small pairs, and some straight draws like 9‑8). With ~95BB effective I want to extract thin value from worse jacks and charge/removal value from straight draws while keeping the pot controllable. Checking keeps opponents' bluffs and weaker calls free; betting a modest amount denies free cards, gets value from worse Jx and smaller pairs, and folds some overcards. I’ll bet about half the pot to balance value and protection (125). If I get a raise I’ll seriously re‑evaluate (likely fold to a large raise; consider calling small raises to see a safe turn given position). If called, I’ll re‑assess on the turn based on card texture and opponent’s tendencies.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on a Jack-high board. Since the pot was limped pre-flop by Minni on the button, her range is quite wide and my middle pair is likely ahead of many of her hands. A 125 chip bet into a 250 chip pot is a standard continuation bet that I can easily call to see how the turn develops. Raising here might bloat the pot unnecessarily while I am out of position, and folding would be too tight given the strength of my hand relative to a limper's range.

On the turn, the 2nd pair (Sevens) is still a relatively strong hand in a limped pot, but it is not strong enough to lead out for value against a button limper who showed aggression on the flop. The 2 of spades is a brick that doesn't significantly change the board texture. By checking, I am playing a pot-control strategy, allowing Minni to continue with her bluffs or semi-bluffs while keeping the pot manageable. If she checks behind, I can evaluate a value bet on the river or a showdown. If she bets, the decision will depend on the sizing, but my hand is generally strong enough to consider calling at least one more street in this specific configuration.

I have top pair (J♣) with a very weak kicker (3♠). Villain called my half‑pot flop bet and then checked the turn — a pot‑control line consistent with a wide, often marginal calling range (worse Jx, small pairs, floats and some straight draws like 9‑8). The 2♠ is a relatively blank for most of those hands and is a good card to continue representing strength. I want thin value from worse jacks/small pairs and to charge the straight draws, while keeping the sizing small enough to avoid bloating the pot with my weak kicker. I’ll fire a half‑pot turn bet to accomplish that (250). If I face a large check‑raise I’ll seriously re‑evaluate and likely fold; if called I’ll re‑assess on the river.

On the turn, the board is Jack-Seven-Six-Deuce. I have second pair (Sevens) with a Ten kicker. Minni, who limped from the button pre-flop, has bet half-pot on both the flop and the turn. In a limped pot, middle pair is a relatively strong holding against a wide button range that could include various straight draws (89, 54, 85), smaller pairs, or air. The Deuce of spades is a complete brick that shouldn't have improved her range significantly. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (250 to win a total pot of 1000). Folding is too conservative here given the range of hands she could be betting for protection or as a semi-bluff. Raising would likely only get called by better hands (Jacks or two-pair). Therefore, calling is the most appropriate action to maintain pot control and see the river.

On the river, the board is Jack-Seven-Six-Deuce-Eight. I hold second pair (Sevens) with a Ten kicker. The Eight of clubs on the river is a somewhat coordinated card, as it completes a potential straight for Ten-Nine and creates possible two-pair combinations like Eight-Seven or Eight-Six. However, since I am out of position and the pot was limped pre-flop, my hand has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value. Betting would likely only be called by better hands (Jacks, two-pairs, or straights). By checking, I can potentially see a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from missed straight draws like Five-Four or King-Ten. My plan is to check and then evaluate based on Minni's betting size if she decides to lead out on the final street.

I have top pair (J♣) with a very weak 3‑kicker on J♠‑7♣‑6♦‑2♠‑8♣. Villain (BB) check‑called my half‑pot flop and turn bets, which is a calling‑range-heavy line (lots of worse Jx, small pairs and missed 9‑8 draws). The 8♣ on the river is a relatively blank card — it pairs some backdoor holdings but generally leaves me ahead of a large portion of his calling range. I want thin value from worse jacks and small pairs and to charge any remaining missed draws, while keeping the pot controllable given my weak kicker. A small value bet (~300, ~30% of the 1,000 pot) is the best play: it extracts from worse hands but limits my downside if I run into a better J or two‑pair; if I face a sizable check‑raise I will fold rather than commit more chips.

On the river, the board is Jack-Seven-Six-Deuce-Eight. I have second pair (Sevens) with a Ten kicker. Minni has bet three streets after limping the button, but her sizing on the river (300 into 1000) is relatively small (30% pot). While the Eight of clubs is a somewhat coordinated card that completes a straight for Ten-Nine and improves some two-pair combinations, the small bet sizing often suggests a thin value bet with a weaker hand (like an Eight or a Six) or a final attempt to take the pot with a missed draw (like Five-Four). Given the pot odds are better than 4-to-1, I only need to be right about 19% of the time to make calling profitable. Folding after calling two streets for such a small final bet would be overly conservative against a wide button-limping range. Therefore, I will call to see the showdown.

I'm on the button with K3o, a marginal hand but playable from my strong position. The pot is 150 with only 100 to call. As the chip leader with position throughout the hand, calling is the correct play. K3o isn't premium enough to raise, but it's too early to fold when I can see a flop with positional advantage against the blinds. Post-flop, my position will allow me to control the action and make position-based decisions. The hand has King-high value and potential to connect with the board.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Trey suited. The Button (Claude) has limped into the pot for 100. With a suited hand and being offered 5-to-1 odds to see a flop (calling 50 more into a 250 pot), this is a standard complete. While J3s is a weak hand, the price is too good to pass up, though I must be cautious playing out of position post-flop.

This is a limp pot (Claude and Flash limped) and I'm in the big blind with K♦h6♠ (King-high, unsuited, poor kicker). I'm out of position postflop and facing a multiway, deep-stacked spot — not a hand to bloat the pot with. I want to control the pot size and see a cheap flop; I'll only continue if I hit a real improvement (top pair with decent kicker or a strong draw). Check and fold to significant aggression unless the flop gives a clear reason to continue.

On a flop of 8s-Th-5c, my Jd-3d has no direct value and only weak backdoor potential. I am out of position against two opponents. Checking is the standard play here to see if I can get a free turn card or simply fold to any significant aggression.

This is a multiway limp pot and I’m out of position with K6s — no pair and no real draw on 8s Th 5c. With deep stacks and wide ranges behind, I want to control the pot rather than bluff or bloat it with a marginal hand. I check to see the turn; I’ll fold to significant aggression and only continue if I pair my King or pick up a genuine draw (or face a very small probe bet giving correct pot odds).

Both opponents have checked to me on the flop, indicating weakness from their likely marginal holdings. While K3o has completely missed this 8-10-5 board, I have significant positional and informational advantages: I'm last to act, have the strongest stack, and face two checked-down opponents. A bet here applies positional pressure and wins the pot immediately with high probability, taking advantage of their demonstrated weakness. The bet size of 200 (67% of pot) is appropriately sized to apply meaningful pressure without overcommitting with a marginal holding. This is a standard continuation bet from the button when both blinds show no strength - a profitable situation to exploit my late position and chip advantage.

On a flop of 8s-Th-5c, I have checked with Jack-Trey of diamonds. The button, Claude, has bet 200 into a pot of 300. I have no pair and no significant draws, only a very weak backdoor flush possibility. Given that I am out of position and Minni is still yet to act behind me, there is no reason to continue with such a weak hand. The pot odds do not justify a call with essentially zero equity.

I checked the flop as planned to control the pot with K6o out of position. Claude’s bet to 200 into the ~300 pot is significant aggression — calling 200 to win ~500 requires ~28.6% equity. I have no pair and no real draw (only 3 kings and 3 sixes = ~6 outs to pair by river, ~24% chance to hit either by river and only ~12.8% to hit a king), so my equity is below the break-even threshold and many hits (a 6) will still lose. Raising as a bluff out of position here is spewy against a wide but often strong c-betting Button. Preserve stack and fold to this sizable bet.

I am on the button with Q9o, which is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 120 big blinds. Opening for 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard play to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early.

I have 8♣5♦ in the small blind facing a button open to 300 (I must call 250). This is a weak offsuit hand that is often dominated and performs poorly out of position. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires ~36% equity — 8-5o is well below that versus a standard button opening range, and the big blind still has to act so this can become a multiway pot where my equity and postflop playability are even worse. A light 3-bet here would be a thin bluff and likely to be called or 4-bet by stronger hands. Prefer to preserve stack and wait for a better spot, so I fold.

Q7o (Queen-Seven offsuit) is a weak hand to play out of position. While the pot odds are reasonable at 2.25:1 (need to call 200 into a 450 pot), the fundamental issue is that I would be playing a marginal hand with positional disadvantage post-flop. Flash has position on me on all subsequent streets, which significantly reduces my hand's value. With a healthy stack of 17,175 chips, there's no need to speculate with weak hands. The disciplined play is to fold and preserve chips for stronger situations where I have better equity or positional advantage. Flash's button raise could include a wide range, but I don't have the hand strength to profitably defend.

I’m on the button with A♥T♣ — a playable, high-card hand with position and useful blockers to strong A-hands. Standard exploitative open from the button: raise to 3x the big blind (to 300) to isolate the blinds, deny the BB good pot odds, and seize initiative post-flop. With stacks ~10–17k this sizing keeps postflop decisions straightforward. Plan: if called I’ll c-bet favorable A/broadway or draw-heavy boards and check back dry low boards; if I face a small 3-bet I’ll often call to play in position, but fold to very large 3-bets without a strong read.

K7o from the small blind against a button raise is a marginal but playable hand. With a significant chip advantage (17,125 vs Minni's 10,050), I can afford to see a flop and play positionally aware post-flop. The call of 250 more gives me reasonable pot odds, and I can outplay my opponents with my stack advantage if I connect with the board. Minni's button raise could be wide, making this worth exploring. A fold is overly tight given my chip position, and a 3-bet is unnecessary with this hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Hearts and Nine of Spades. Minni raised from the button to 300, and Claude called from the small blind. I need to put in 200 more chips to see the flop in a pot that will be 900. These are excellent pot odds (3.5:1). T9o is a strong enough hand to defend my big blind in a multi-way pot, as it has good connectivity and can flop straights or decent pairs. Closing the action with a call is the most standard and mathematically sound play here.

K7o is a weak hand (king-high with 7 kicker) on a coordinated flop with two diamonds. Out of position against two opponents, checking is the correct play. This avoids burning chips with marginal value and allows me to gather information about opponent hand strength before deciding on further action. No draw to speak of, no pair, and no reason to play this hand aggressively from out of position.

On this flop of Td-Qd-8s, I have middle pair (Tens) with an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Seven completes a straight). This is a very strong semi-bluffing or drawing hand with significant equity. However, since I am out of position in a three-way pot and the original pre-flop raiser (Minni) is still to act, checking is the most prudent move. It allows me to see how the action develops and potentially close the action or call a bet to see a turn card, utilizing my drawing potential while protecting my stack.

I raised preflop with A♥T♣ and on T♦ Q♦ 8♠ I have second pair with the best possible kicker (Ace). Both blinds checked, so a c-bet is the standard, exploitative play: get value from worse tens/eights and force folds from overcards, while charging straight (J9) and diamond backdoor draws. With two opponents I want a reasonably large protection/value size to deny equity — roughly 2/3 pot. Bet 600 into the 900 pot. If I get called I’ll re-evaluate the turn and continue vs single callers on safe cards; I’ll fold to a large, polarized raise because my hand is vulnerable to Qx/flushes/straights.

K7o is a weak hand with no pair, no draw, and vulnerable kicker on a coordinated flop (Td Qd 8s). Being out of position against two opponents after Minni's aggressive 600 bet, I lack the equity or playability to justify calling. My pre-flop 250 investment is a sunk cost; the correct decision is to prevent further bleeding. While my chip advantage (16,875 vs Minni's 9,450) is significant, calling weak hands out of position to burn chips is poor bankroll management. Minni's aggression after two checks suggests either genuine strength or a draw, neither of which my hand is equipped to handle. Folding preserves my chip advantage to exploit better situations later in the tournament.

On this flop of Td-Qd-8s, I have middle pair (Tens) and an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Seven completes a straight). Minni has bet 600 into a 900 pot after Claude checked and folded. I am getting 3.5:1 pot odds (calling 600 to win a total pot of 2100). Given my significant equity with both a pair and a strong draw, calling is the most mathematically sound play. I have roughly 8 outs for a straight, plus potential to improve to two pair or trips, making this an easy continue while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

On the turn, the 8h pairs the board. I currently have two pair (Tens and Eights) with an open-ended straight draw (any Jack or Seven). Since I called Minni's flop bet, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how she responds to the board pairing. If she checks back, I get a free card to try and hit my straight or improve to a better two pair. If she bets, I will evaluate the pot odds to see if my straight draw and pair have enough equity to continue. Checking is the most flexible move out of position here.

Turn pairs the board (T♦ Q♦ 8♠ 8♥). I have A♥T♣ — second pair with the best kicker, but a large chunk of Flash’s flop-calling range now beats me (Qx, Tx -> two pair, 8x -> sets, J9 -> made straight) or has strong equity. Betting here would mainly get called or raised by better hands and only fold the weakest parts of his range — thin value while building a pot I’m often behind in. In position I prefer pot control: check and reevaluate the river. If Flash leads the river I’ll fold to large, polarized bets and call smaller bets more often (or value-bet/call if the river pairs my T or brings a clear improving card).

I have a full house (Eights full of Tens) on a board of T-Q-8-8-8. While this is a strong hand, Minni's pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet strongly suggest she could have a Queen, which would give her a superior full house (Eights full of Queens). Furthermore, pocket pairs like JJ, KK, or AA also beat me. By checking, I can potentially reach a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from a weaker hand. Betting would likely only result in being called by hands that beat me or split the pot with me.

I hold A♥T♣ and the river (T♦ Q♦ 8♠ 8♥ 8♦) gives me a full house — eights full of tens (three 8s on board + my T). However a large portion of Flash’s flop-calling range now beats me: any Qx (pairs the Q), any T/TT (bigger full house), or an 8 in hand (quads) will be ahead. Flash’s line — preflop call, flop check–call of my c-bet, then passive turn and river checks — is consistent with those medium-strong hands (Qx/Tx) or cautious showdown hands rather than pure air. Betting here would be thin: I’ll often get called (or raised) by hands that beat me and only rarely extract value from worse holdings; a bet also risks a costly raise from a superior full house/quads. With the pot size and stacks, the lowest-variance, most +EV line is pot control — check to showdown and let him show his hand. If he bets as a bluff I can call; I won’t build the pot into likely domination.

Jh2c is one of the weakest hands in poker - jack-deuce offsuit has minimal equity and poor playability. While I'm in button position which is ideal, and I'm facing only 100 to call into a 150-pot (3:1 odds), the fundamental hand strength is too poor. Even with positional advantage, J2o will struggle against reasonable ranges from Flash (SB) and Minni (BB), who have both shown active play throughout the tournament. The odds don't justify playing such a weak hand when I can conserve chips for stronger opportunities. Good bankroll management and hand selection is crucial over 150 hands - folding marginal/weak hands is more profitable than finding creative reasons to play them.

Folded to me in the Small Blind. Ten-Seven offsuit is a standard hand to attempt a steal against the Big Blind in a three-handed game. Raising to 300 puts pressure on Minni and allows me to take down the blinds immediately or play the pot with initiative if called.

Flash opened to 300 from the small blind. I hold J♦7♠ — a marginal, offsuit hand but I have position postflop (I act after the SB). Calling 200 more (300 total committed) is the right play: pot odds are reasonable and against a likely wide SB opening range I can realize equity and use position to control the pot. 3-betting J7o as a bluff is too loose with deep stacks and can get me in uncomfortable spots; folding would be too tight given the price. I'll defend and see a flop.

On a low, connected board of 8s-4s-5h, I have missed completely with my Ten-high. However, as the pre-flop aggressor from the Small Blind, I can represent a wide range of strong hands including overpairs (99-AA) or an Eight. A standard continuation bet of half the pot (300) is a good way to test Minni's connection to the board and potentially take down the pot immediately. If I am called or raised, I will likely slow down unless the turn improves my hand or provides a good semi-bluffing opportunity.

Flash c-bet 300 into a 900 pot. Calling 300 here is the default — I’m getting ~20% break-even (call 300 to win ~1500). J♦7♠ is a marginal hand but has reasonable showdown equity vs a wide SB c-betting range and I have position postflop to control the pot on the turn. I don’t have enough to credibly shove or a strong draw to bluff-raise profitably, and folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call 300, evaluate the turn (fold to heavy continued aggression if I miss; extract value if I pair/improve).

On the turn 9c, I have picked up an open-ended straight draw (6 or J completes) to go with my two overcards. Since I have been the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, continuing with a semi-bluff is the most consistent and powerful play. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot puts significant pressure on Minni's likely range of mid-pairs or draws and allows me to win the pot immediately or have equity to improve on the river.

Pot is 2000 and I face an 800 turn bet on 8♠-4♠-5♥-9♣ with J♦7♠. Calling 800 to win 2800 requires ~28.6% equity. My realistic river outs are 3 J + 3 sevens + 4 sixes = 10 naive outs (~21.7%), but many of those (paired 7s/Js) are non‑nut and will still lose to the sorts of hands Flash’s line represents (8x, 9x, 5x, sets or completed straights). Flash opened from SB, c‑bet the flop and now sizes up on the turn — a line weighted to value. A bluff‑raise is too risky here given stack depths and the line, and a plain call is a negative‑EV call by pot odds and likely fold equity on the river. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. While it's a 3-handed game and positional play is important, opening with such a weak range from the button is generally unprofitable, especially with two capable opponents in the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the small blind with K♥5♣ heads-up after Flash folded — marginal but playable. Pot is 150 and I only need to put in 50 more (total commitment 100) to see a flop. With deep stacks and out of position, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a weak offsuit king by raising; calling keeps the pot small and gives me cheap equity and the chance to improve. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to substantial preflop aggression, and proceed cautiously postflop — continue only with a strong top pair or good draws.

Checking with K9o from the big blind after Minni called from the small blind. This is a marginal holding that plays better in position with more information. Checking allows me to see the flop at no additional cost and make better decisions with post-flop equity realization. K9 will miss most flops, so investing extra chips now is inefficient. If I hit a King or develop a strong draw, I can apply pressure on later streets.

Dry, uncoordinated flop (3s-6c-9s). I hold K♥5♣ with no pair and no meaningful draw — just an overcard. Pot is 200 and there is currently no bet. I called preflop to see a cheap flop but it missed; Claude has shown a tendency to call down and won’t fold many marginal pairs to a c‑bet. With deep stacks I prefer pot control and to realize my equity rather than bloating the pot with a weak hand. Plan: check and fold to a sizeable bet on this street; only consider calling a small probe bet or bluffing later if he shows clear weakness or a King lands.

I'm holding K9 (pair of nines with King kicker) on a 3-6-9 flop in a heads-up situation versus Minni after their check. This is a moderately strong hand with several advantages: (1) I have a made pair that wins against most of Minni's weak calling ranges from the SB, (2) I have a K overcard for additional winning chances, and (3) I have a backdoor spade flush draw for equity insurance. Minni's check signals weakness, likely a whiffed hand or marginal holding. A bet of 120 (roughly 60% of the pot) achieves multiple objectives: value extraction from weaker pairs, continuation of aggressive play, and keeping the pot at a manageable size. This sizing is not so large that it commits me unnecessarily, but sufficient to charge draws and fold out marginal hands. Given the pot size and stack depths, betting here is more profitable than checking, which would allow Minni to improve for free or realize equity advantage with potential draws.

Fold. On 3♠-6♣-9♠ I have K♥5♣ with no pair and no meaningful draw. Claude bet 120 into the pot (pot now 320); calling 120 requires about 37.5% equity (120/320). I only have roughly 6 outs (3 kings + 3 fives) — ~24% to pair by the river — and many of those hits (especially a 5) will still be second-best. Table history shows Claude is willing to call down marginal pairs, so a bluff-raise is unlikely to succeed. Calling is -EV and bloats the pot with a weak hand, so I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

On the button with A♦5♥ and ~98bb effective, this is a clear open-raise spot versus the blinds. A5o is well inside a standard button opening range: it has an Ace for top-pair potential and is easy to play postflop in position. Opponents (SB Claude and BB Flash) defend and occasionally 3-bet, but they also fold to pressure — a 3bb open to 300 gives good fold equity while leaving plenty of stack to maneuver postflop. Plan: open to 300; if I face a large 3-bet I will usually fold (or re-evaluate versus a small squeeze), if called I will play aggressively with top pairs/draws and control pot size with marginal holdings.

Calling Minni's button raise with Q-6. Already invested 50 chips as small blind, so 250 more is reasonable to see a flop. Minni likely has a wide button range. Stack size (16925) allows for flexibility. Will evaluate post-flop based on board texture.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten of Hearts and Five of Spades. Minni raised from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. Although the pot odds are 4.5:1 to call (200 to win a 900 total pot), Ten-Five offsuit is extremely weak and plays poorly multi-way out of position. I am likely to face difficult decisions on most flops and lack connectivity or high-card strength. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Holding Q-6 on a K-2-3 board in heads-up play. This is a dry flop where Queen-high is likely the best hand currently. Minni's button range is wide and could easily be completely unpaired. Rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand out of position, checking accomplishes several goals: (1) it allows Minni to bluff with unpaired hands, (2) it keeps the pot manageable given the strength of Q-6, and (3) it allows me to gather more information on later streets. The board texture (K-high with 2-3) means neither of us has much equity for big draws. Checking also prevents me from investing further into a hand that could easily be outkicked by Minni's range (K-x, A-x, pairs). I can confidently evaluate on the turn once more information is revealed.

I raised preflop to 300 and got a call from Claude. Flop K♦ 2♣ 3♦ and he checks. Holding A♦5♥ I have ace-high plus a very weak backdoor diamond draw (runner-runner), but no pair. Heads-up my c-bet frequency should be high — this is a relatively dry, low board that misses a lot of Claude’s calling range (small pairs, suited connectors, broadways). A half-pot bet applies pressure, folds out a lot of his unpaired hands, and defines his range while keeping the pot controllable with a marginal holding. I size to ~50% pot (350). If Claude raises big I’ll give up and fold; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously and only commit more if I improve (A/5) or get strong action/reads to continue.

Calling Minni's flop raise on K-2-3 with Q-6. Analysis: (1) Pot odds are favorable at 3:1 (1050 to call 350), requiring ~25% equity to break even. (2) Q-high on this dry, unpaired K-2-3 board likely has 30-40% equity against Minni's wide button-raise range, which includes many unpaired hands. (3) This could be a continuation bet from Minni with broadway cards, small pairs, or whiffed draws rather than a value bet with a King. (4) My stack depth (16675) allows me to play a call-and-see approach. (5) Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to gather information on the turn—if another low card comes and Minni checks or bets weakly, I can be more confident playing for value or as a bluff. (6) If the turn brings a Queen or Six, I improve significantly. Simply folding Q-high to a flop raise in position would be too tight here. Call to continue evaluating on the turn street.

Excellent turn development. I started with Q-6 on a K-2-3 flop, where I had marginal strength and called Minni's flop raise for information. The 6 of Spades on the turn is a massive improvement—I now have a pair of sixes with a Queen kicker. This is a significant hand on this board texture. Analysis: (1) The turn card (6s) is highly unlikely to have helped Minni—it doesn't complete any draws from her likely flop raising range (K-x, Broadway cards, or bluffs with unpaired hands). (2) My pair of sixes is currently the strongest hand against most of Minni's range except for K-x or pocket pairs she held pre-flop. (3) Pot odds and position: The pot is 1400, and I'm first to act. A bet of 600 represents about 43% of the pot, which is a reasonable value bet. (4) Stack depths allow continuation: With Minni at 9150 and me at 16325, I have room to continue applying pressure while gathering information. (5) Betting serves multiple purposes: extracts value from weaker hands, gets information about Minni's holding, and protects against free cards that could improve her hand on the river. I'm betting for value and information—if Minni has a King, she'll likely continue; if she was bluffing, she'll likely fold.

Turn: K♦ 2♣ 3♦ 6♠. I’m on the button with A♦5♥. Pot is ~2000 and Claude bets 600; calling 600 to win ~2600 gives ~4.33:1 pot odds (need ≈18.8% equity). After the turn my backdoor diamond draw is dead — my only clean outs are the remaining Aces (~3/46 ≈6.5%), and a 5 on the river is rarely good versus Kx or pocket pairs. Claude’s line (call preflop, call flop then a small turn bet) looks heavy for Kx/small pairs but could include probes/weak hands. Given his tendency to call down in prior hands, a bluff-raise is unlikely to fold him and would be unnecessarily risky. Folding is too nitty here. I’ll take the inexpensive route and call to see the river, then re-evaluate (if I hit an Ace I can value-bet/raise; if I miss and he fires a large river bet I’ll likely fold).

Checking on the river with Q-6 on a K-2-3-6-2 board. I have a pair of sixes (marginal strength), and Minni's action line (flop re-raise + turn call) suggests she likely holds a King or better. Out-of-position with weak pair strength, checking allows me to: (1) Control the pot size and avoid losing additional chips if beat, (2) Win the pot if Minni checks behind with a weaker hand or if she was semi-bluffing, (3) Gain information before deciding on future action. Betting with marginal strength OOP is unnecessarily risky when the river card didn't improve my hand and likely didn't help Minni either. Check is the disciplined play.

Board K♦ 2♣ 3♦ 6♠ 2♥. I hold A♦5♥ — no pair, only Ace-high. Claude’s line (call pre, call flop, bet/raise on turn) is consistent with Kx, a pocket pair or a deuce — all of which beat me now, and the river pairing the deuce makes trips for any 2 in his hand. He’s shown a tendency to call down, so my fold equity on a river bluff is very low and a bet will often get called by better hands or induce a check-raise. With no value and little to gain from bluffing, I’ll check to reach showdown and avoid turning a marginal hand into a costly bluff.

With 9-10 offsuit on the button, I'm getting favorable pot odds (1.5:1) to call the 100 small blind. This is a marginal hand, but position is crucial in poker - I'll act last post-flop which gives me significant informational advantage. The hand has reasonable playability with both straight and overcards potential. My stack is healthy at 18,325, and my opponents have reasonable stacks as well. Folding here would be unnecessarily tight given the small investment required and positional advantage. I'll call to see the flop in position.

I have T9o in the small blind. The button (Claude) limped, which often indicates a wide, marginal range. I am getting excellent pot odds (50 to call into a pot of 250) to see a flop. While T9o is a decent hand in a short-handed game, raising out of position against the chip leader who has shown a tendency to be sticky is not the most efficient use of chips. I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate based on the board texture.

I’m in the big blind with A♥8♦ (A8o). Pot is 300 and there’s nothing to call (I’ve already posted BB). Both opponents limped, so this is a multiway pot; A8o is a marginal hand with a weak kicker and loses a lot of equity multiway. The minimum raise to 200 is too small to generate meaningful fold equity and a larger isolation raise would commit chips with a medium-strength hand against two deep stacks who often call. I’ll check to see a flop cheaply and only get aggressive if I improve (top pair, two pair/trips, or strong draws). If I miss the flop and face significant bets I’ll fold; if I hit or get favorable texture I’ll proceed with value/pressure.

The flop is 6d 3s 3h, which is a very dry and paired board. I hold Ten-high (T9o), which has missed the flop entirely, though I do have two overcards to the six. In a limped pot with two opponents, there is no need to bloat the pot with a marginal hand out of position. I will check to see how the other players react to this board texture and potentially see a free turn card.

Both opponents limped pre and I checked to see a cheap flop. I completely missed: board 6♦ 3♠ 3♥ and I only have A♥8♦ (Ace-high). This is a multiway pot where limpers often have a 3, a small pocket pair, or a wide calling range — bluffing into two players is thin and I have very little showdown value. I want pot control and to avoid committing chips with a marginal hand, so I’ll check and see the turn. If I face a sizable bet I’ll fold; if action is small or both check through I can reassess on the turn (look to bet only if I improve or can credibly represent a stronger holding).

Both opponents have checked on a dry 6♦ 3♠ 3♥ flop, indicating weakness. My holding (9♥ T♣) is currently just 9-high with a gutshot straight draw (any K makes a straight). While I have position on the button, my hand is too marginal to bet for value, and a semi-bluff bet would be poor risk-reward given my modest outs. Checking here accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) I get a free turn card to improve, (2) I maintain a cheap path to showdown if both opponents remain passive, (3) I preserve my stack for stronger opportunities, and (4) I avoid inflating the pot when out of position on future streets with a weak hand. The board texture doesn't favor aggressive plays - I should take the free card and reassess on the turn.

The turn brings a Queen, an overcard to the existing board. After everyone checked the flop, it is unlikely anyone holds a three. However, I still only have Ten-high with no draw. In a three-way limped pot, there is little reason to bluff into two opponents when I have no equity. I will check to see how the other players react to the Queen and hope for a free river card.

Board: 6♦ 3♠ 3♥ Q♥. I hold A♥8♦ (Ace-high). This is a multiway pot with both opponents having limped/called preflop and checked the flop — ranges are wide and often include small pairs, single-pair Qx, or missed hands. I completely missed the turn and have no realistic draw (even with hearts I cannot make a flush on the river). Bluff equity is very thin against two players and my kicker is weak. I planned to check the flop for pot control and that plan still makes sense on the turn: I should avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check and preserve my stack; if I face a significant bet I’ll fold given my poor equity, and if both check through I’ll reassess on the river (likely check-behind for showdown or fold to pressure).

Both opponents have checked twice on a dry board with a paired flop and queen turn. This consistent passivity indicates weakness. My position on the button combined with their checking pattern gives me an excellent opportunity to apply pressure. While my 9-T has no made hand, I have a K-out for a straight on the river, making this a reasonable semi-bluff. A bet of 175 chips (roughly 60% pot) applies real pressure without overcommitting to the spot. This sizing is appropriate for exploiting perceived weakness from two cautious opponents.

On the turn, a Queen appears which is an overcard to the board. After checking the flop, Claude (the Button) bets 175 into a 300 pot. I hold only Ten-high with no draw and zero showdown value. While Claude's bet might be a positional stab at a limped pot, I have no equity to continue, and Minni still has to act behind me. There is no reason to risk chips with a hand this weak in a small pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Call 175. I’m heads-up with A♥8♦ on 6♦-3♠-3♥-Q♥ and completely missed the turn; there are no realistic draws and raising as a bluff is thin against Claude’s range. His 175 is a small probe/value bet into a 475 pot — calling 175 is a cheap way to see the river (pot odds ≈27%) and realize showdown equity against bluffs and weaker Ax/Kx hands. I’ll call now to keep the pot under control and re-evaluate on the river, folding to a large river bet if I don’t improve.

The river (6♦-3♠-3♥-Q♥-3♦) pairs the board into trips. I hold A♥8♦ with no pair or meaningful blocker to full houses/quads. Claude bet a small 175 on the turn and I called; his range from limping/calling/raising lines includes Qx or 6x that now make full houses (and quads are possible if he held a 3). I have almost no showdown value and very little fold equity — a river bluff is thin and too risky in a 3k+ stack tournament spot. I’ll check to keep the pot small and avoid committing chips; if Claude bets river I will fold to any meaningful value bet and only consider calling an extremely tiny blocking bet.

River showdown check. My hand (9-T making pair of threes) is too weak to value bet after being called on the turn. Minni's turn call suggests they have a hand that beats most of my range. Checking down preserves chips and allows the hand to reach showdown where my hand will either hold up or lose. Betting here would lose additional chips to hands that called the turn, while checking either wins the pot against air or costs nothing against better holdings. This is prudent pot preservation on the river.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a marginal opening hand. Given my position, I have the opportunity to take control of the pot early and potentially steal the blinds. Minni and Claude have been active, but a standard 3x raise is a reasonable play to exert pressure from the button.

Facing a standard button open to 3bb, I'm in the small blind with K7 offsuit — a marginal, often dominated hand out of position. Calling 2.5bb to see a flop leaves me OOP vs the raiser (and the big blind) in a multiway pot with poor post‑flop playability; 3‑betting here would be too thin and speculative with deep stacks. Preserve the stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Folding 9-4o in the big blind vs Flash's button raise. While pot odds of 2.25:1 are tempting, 9-4 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker with marginal equity (~25-28% vs a button range), and my significant positional disadvantage post-flop makes this an easy fold with a healthy stack.

I’m on the button (posted the small blind) with J♥4♠ — a weak hand, but in a 3‑handed game I should be opening a wide stealing range. The pot is 150 and the min‑raise is to 200; raising to 200 (total committed = 200, I’ve already posted 50 so I’d add 150) is a small investment relative to my 8.9k stack and gives good fold equity and the positional advantage postflop. Plan: open‑raise to take the blinds down or play in position; if I face a large 3‑bet I’ll let it go (J4o plays poorly vs big aggression), if called I’ll c‑bet favorable flops and control pot size with a marginal holding. Overall the small, standard open is the best low‑risk way to accumulate chips in 3‑max here.

Folding 5-8o from small blind. While pot odds offer 2.3:1, this hand is fundamentally unprofitable out of position. 5-8 offsuit has minimal equity, poor playability, and no realistic path to a strong hand. Early tournament (58/150) with healthy stack allows for disciplined folding of weak hands. Minni's aggressive button raises can be addressed with stronger holdings.

I am in the big blind with Deuce of Clubs and Eight of Diamonds, one of the weakest starting hands. Minni raised to 200 from the button, and Claude folded. While the price to call is small (100 chips into a 350 pot), this hand has very poor playability post-flop and lacks sufficient equity to justify a defense against most button opening ranges, even in a short-handed game. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold pocket Aces, the strongest possible hand in Texas Hold'em. Position is excellent (button), and I'm heads-up (effectively) against the blinds. The standard play with AA is to raise for value extraction. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) achieves multiple objectives: (1) builds the pot from the strong hand, (2) charges opponents appropriately for their weaker holdings, (3) maintains balance with my general raising range from the button, and (4) keeps both opponents engaged rather than folding out immediately. Both Flash and Minni have shown willingness to 3-bet and play back, so they may defend, but that's favorable since I have the best hand. No reason to slow-play premium holdings in this spot.

I have been dealt pocket Kings (Kc Ks) in the Small Blind. This is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Claude has opened for 300 from the button. I need to 3-bet here for value and to protect my hand out of position. A standard 3-bet size from the blinds against a button open is roughly 3.5x to 4x the opening raise. I will raise to 1000 to build the pot and potentially get Claude or the Big Blind to commit more chips with a wider range of hands.

Claude opened to 300 from the button and Flash (SB) 3-bet to 1000. I’m in the big blind with Tc6s — a weak, unsuited hand with poor postflop playability and a bad kicker. Calling 900 into a 1400 pot gives ~39% pot odds, but vs a SB 3-bet and an unopened button range my equity is well below that and I’ll often be dominated or multiway out of position. A 4-bet bluff is inappropriate with this holding and stacks are deep enough to avoid marginal spots. Fold and wait for a better situation.

I hold pocket Aces (As Ac), the strongest possible hand preflop. Flash has 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. Looking at Flash's pattern across previous hands, they're an aggressive player who 3-bets frequently with a wide range (including relatively weak holdings like 9h 4s, 6c Qs, etc.). With pocket Aces, I must re-raise for value extraction. A re-raise to 2200 (1200 additional over Flash's 1000) accomplishes several objectives: (1) builds the pot from my best hand, (2) applies pressure that will force Flash to make a decision with inferior holdings, (3) achieves value from their wide 3-bet range, and (4) positions me favorably postflop if called. Flash's stack (12,075) is sufficiently deep that further commitment is not problematic. This re-raise strikes the right balance between value extraction and hand security.

I have pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand in poker. Claude has 4-bet my initial 3-bet, but the sizing is relatively small (2200 over 1000). In a 3-handed game, this range could include AA, KK, QQ, JJ, or AK, and potentially some bluffs given our history of aggression. I want to maximize value and am very comfortable getting all the chips in pre-flop. I will 5-bet to 5500, which builds a massive pot and essentially commits me to the hand, inviting a shove from Claude's strong-but-inferior hands.

Shoving all-in with pocket Aces against Flash's 4-bet to 5500. Flash has only 2075 chips remaining and is getting roughly 4.5:1 odds on a call, but I have the strongest possible hand and am ahead of their entire reasonable 4-bet range except for AA itself. Flash's loose-aggressive profile means they could be 4-betting with many hands (premium pairs, broadway cards, occasional bluffs). With AA, I must maximize value against an opponent who has demonstrated a tendency to play wide ranges. The all-in move applies maximum pressure on Flash's short stack and represents a natural continuation of the aggressive preflop action.

I have Pocket Kings (Kc Ks), which is the second strongest starting hand in poker. Claude and I have engaged in a series of re-raises pre-flop, and his latest bet of 15,700 effectively puts me all-in. In a 3-handed high roller tournament, Kings are never a fold here. Claude's range is likely comprised of AA, KK, QQ, and AK. While I am behind AA, I am ahead of or splitting with everything else in that range. Given the massive pot odds and the strength of my hand, I am calling the remainder of my stack to go all-in.

Heads-up on the button with Jc7h: marginal but very playable. In heads-up you should open-raise a wide range to seize initiative and use position postflop. A standard 2xBB to 200 (min-raise) builds fold equity, keeps the pot manageable against a much bigger stack (I have ~88 BB) and allows me to c-bet or pot-control according to the flop. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call smaller aggression and play in position — c-bet favorable textures, check/call or pot-control on dry boards.

Heads-up play against short-stacked opponent with a strong hand. JQ is a premium holding in heads-up, especially when the opponent is severely short-stacked (8,725 chips, ~43 BB). Minni's button raise from 50 to 200 is fairly standard aggression in position.
