With JQ, I have a strong hand that's well above the heads-up value threshold. Given Minni's desperation (short stack) and tournament dynamics, I should raise here rather than just call. A raise accomplishes several objectives: (1) puts pressure on the short stack, (2) increases fold equity, (3) maximizes value if they call. A raise to 600 is appropriate - it's about 3x their raise and creates a substantial commitment decision for them with limited chips. This either wins the blinds outright or sets up an all-in situation where my JQ has reasonable equity.

I opened to 200 from the button with Jc7h and Claude 3-bet to 600 (a standard, relatively small 3-bet). Heads-up, J7o is marginal but playable in position — calling keeps the pot controlled, preserves my positional advantage, and gives me good postflop playability against what is likely a wide 3-bet range. Pot math: I need to call 400 more to win ~1,200 (roughly 3:1), so I only need ~25% equity to justify a call. I won't 4-bet here — J7o is too weak to polarize; a 4-bet would either fold out many hands I can outplay or get me committed against stronger holdings. Plan after the call: use position — c‑bet favorable flops (when I hit or when texture is dry), float and apply pressure on turns when appropriate, and fold to heavy action when I miss. Call to see a flop and play postflop in position.

Continuation bet on flop after successful pre-flop aggression. Minni's short stack and flop check suggest weakness. J-Q has decent equity and needs to apply pressure. Bet of 700 creates fold equity while getting value if called. This bet forces Minni into a difficult all-in decision with their limited chips.

I have top pair (7s) with a decent jack kicker (Jc7h) and I'm in position. Claude 3-bet preflop so his range is strong-ish, but his small bet of 700 into a 1900 pot is a relatively cheap c-bet and looks like a wide probe. Pot odds: calling 700 to potentially win ~3300 means I only need ~21% equity. My hand has more than enough equity vs a wide 3-bet/c-bet range and calling keeps the pot controllable while preserving positional advantage. I don't want to 4-bet/raise here — my hand is good but not strong enough to polarize, and a raise could commit me against overpairs or get reraised. Plan: call the 700, evaluate the turn (fold to heavy continued aggression on dangerous cards, value-bet or check back vs weakness).

Betting top pair (JJ with Q kicker) on the turn to apply pressure and extract value from the extremely short-stacked Minni. The Jack on the turn is a favorable card that improves my hand while potentially bricking draws. With Minni having only 7,625 chips remaining (~38 BB), this bet size creates a compelling call/fold decision while building the pot I'm likely to win. This continues the aggressive line established pre-flop and on the flop.

I have Jc7h on 7c 6h 9h Js — that’s two pair (Jacks and Sevens), a very strong holding heads‑up. Claude was the preflop 3‑better and has shown aggression (flop raise, now 1,500 on the turn), so his range contains both strong made hands (sets: 99/66/77, JJ) and some bluffs/draws/one‑pair hands. Very few hands currently beat me (sets/trips or an already‑made straight); I beat almost all single‑pair hands and most draws.

Pot math favors a call: pot is 4,100; calling 1,500 makes it ~5,600 — I need ~26.8% equity to justify the call. My two pair is well above that vs a realistic 3‑bet/c‑bet range. A raise would polarize, risk getting reraised/committed to monsters, and may fold out hands that will pay me off on the river. I prefer to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and reevaluate on the river.

Action: call 1,500 now. Plan: if river is a harmless card and Claude checks, extract value with a river bet (or check back if board gets scary); if he fires a small/medium bet I will call for value; if he jams a huge overbet I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to shove unless other reads/sizing indicate a bluff).

Betting 3,000 for value on the river with pair of Jacks. Minni is critically short-stacked and has shown willingness to call down aggressively. This bet size (54% of pot) is a standard value bet that extracts maximum chips from weaker holdings while putting Minni in a difficult all-in decision with limited chips remaining. The dry board (7-6-9-J-4 with no flush or straight possibilities) means my pair of Jacks is likely the best hand against most of Minni's realistic holdings (AK, AQ, lower pairs). This continues the aggressive line established pre-flop through turn and maintains tournament equity pressure on the short stack.

I have Jc7h for two pair (Jacks & Sevens) on 7c 6h 9h Js 4h — a very strong heads‑up holding. Claude 3‑bet preflop and barreled flop/turn, so his range contains value (sets: 77/99/66, pocket J’s, overpairs like TT+) but also missed hearts/straight draws and some bluffs. The river 4h completes a possible heart flush, but he needs two hearts in hand for that; I also block some of his worst combos because I hold a J and a 7, which reduces JJ/77 combos. Pot math: pot is 8,600 and his bet is 3,000 — calling 3,000 makes the pot ≈11,600, so I need ~26% equity to break even. My two pair has well over that vs a realistic 3‑bet/c‑bet range, so a call is +EV. Raising would polarize and risk committing to sets/quads/straights; folding would be too nitty with this strong hand. Call 3,000 and realize value.

Pocket nines is a strong hand, and I'm in the button position heads-up with a significant chip lead (25,225 vs 14,625). I need to play aggressively in favorable spots. From the button, raising with pocket nines applies pressure on the big blind, allows me to control the action post-flop, and builds the pot while I have a quality hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 total chips is appropriate here - it's not so large as to be reckless, but substantial enough to exert positional dominance and chip advantage. This is a profitable spot to play for value and pot control.

Heads-up vs a 3x open from the button, Claude's opening range is wide. Js6c is marginal but has decent showdown value and can hit top-pair or backdoor improvements. The pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more — calling gives me ~3:1 pot odds, so defending is correct. I don't want to 3-bet with a weak, out-of-position hand that plays poorly postflop; a small re-raise would also be easy for him to exploit. Plan: call to see the flop and proceed only with meaningful improvement (top pair, good draws); fold to heavy aggression on dry boards where I miss.

I have two pair (Jacks and Sixes: Js6c on Jd6d4h) in a heads-up spot — a very strong holding but vulnerable to sets and the diamond draw. Claude’s button open is wide, so his range contains lots of single-pair hands, weak jacks, sixes, and diamond draws that I want to charge. Check would allow free cards to draws and give him a cheap opportunity to take the pot; betting protects my hand and extracts value. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (400 into 600) to charge flush draws and get called by worse Jx/6x and small pairs. If he check-raises large I’ll re-evaluate (likely call a small–medium raise for value, but be cautious vs a very large shove that looks like a set). If called, proceed to extract or protect on the turn unless a diamond or higher overcard changes the situation.

I'm holding pocket nines on a 4♥-J♦-6♦ flop. Minni has check-raised me to 400, and I need to evaluate this spot carefully.

Hand Assessment:
- Pocket nines is a moderate-strength hand that's currently ahead of most unpaired holdings
- However, the flop is somewhat dangerous with the jack (Minni could easily have J-x), and there are two diamonds (potential flush draws)
- The board texture allows for many straight possibilities (any 7 or 5 completes a straight)

Minni's Check-Raise:
- This aggressive action from the big blind after calling my 300 preflop raise could indicate a strong hand (JJ, 4x, 6x), a semi-bluff, or a draw
- In heads-up play, ranges are wide, but the check-raise is a strong action

Stack Dynamics:
- I have a chip lead (24,975 vs 14,025), which gives me flexibility to play marginal spots
- However, I don't want to put excessive chips at risk with a hand that may be dominated

Decision Rationale:
Calling is the strongest line here because:
1. It allows me to gather more information on the turn
2. I maintain pot control while keeping options open
3. I avoid committing heavily with a hand that could be behind (to J-x or possibly AA/KK)
4. With position and a chip lead, I can play more profitably on the turn depending on what comes
5. The pot odds (roughly 2.5:1) make a call reasonable for a speculative hand

This approach balances risk management with positional advantage and information gathering.

I have two pair (Jacks and Sixes) in a HU spot after I bet the flop and Claude called. The turn (2h) is essentially a blank — it didn’t complete the diamond draw and didn’t meaningfully improve most of his range. Claude’s call on the flop keeps in lots of worse Jx/6x, pocket pairs and single-card diamond draws that I want to charge. Checking gives free river cards and lets bluffs/draws see the river cheaply. I should continue for value and protection. I’ll bet a substantial, but not committing, size — ~70% pot (1000 into the 1400 pot) to extract from worse pairs, charge draws, and preserve fold equity. If he raises big I’ll re-evaluate (a large raise is often a set/monster; I’ll be willing to call small-to-medium raises for value but will be cautious to fold to an overcommitment shove unless the sizing/history indicates a bluff).

Calling is the most prudent play on the turn. Pocket 9s has sufficient equity (~40-45%) against Minni's likely range of strong hands and draws to justify the call. The 2♥ doesn't improve my hand, but the board texture suggests Minni may have a draw or a pair. With a significant chip lead (24,575 vs 13,025), I can afford to see the river card and make a final decision with complete information. Folding would be unnecessarily tight, while raising would overcommit chips with a hand that's vulnerable. Calling balances risk management with value accumulation in this heads-up spot.

I have two pair (Jacks and Sixes) on a river that paired the board with deuces. That river does introduce the risk of trips/full houses if Claude holds a deuce or a pocket pair that connected, but he has simply called my flop (400) and turn (1000) rather than showing big aggression — his line looks much more like single-pair hands (Jx/6x), medium pairs or missed diamond draws than an obvious set/quads. Checking gives him a free card/cheaper bluff opportunity and misses value from the hands I beat. I prefer to extract value and deny a free showdown: betting ~60% pot will get called by worse jacks, sixes and medium pairs while folding out only the very strongest hands. I choose a 2000-chip value bet; if he raises big I’ll re-evaluate (large raise often indicates a set/full house and I’ll proceed cautiously).

Calling is correct on the river. The pot odds (2.7:1) require only ~27% equity to break even. Pocket nines beats missed draws, weaker pairs, and unpaired holdings. While Minni's aggressive line suggests strength, in heads-up play this could include semi-bluffs or draws that completed without beating my pair. Against Minni's full river range, my nines have sufficient equity. The risk-reward is favorable: I risk 2,000 to win 7,400+ total pot, with reasonable equity to win this spot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with K♦5♦ — a marginal but playable hand that gains a lot of value from position and suitedness. Against a single opponent I should be aggressive and open a very wide range; a minimum raise to 200 pressures the big blind, generates fold equity, and builds the pot when I'm in position. If Claude 3-bets large I can concede this particular hand; if he calls I can use positional advantage and the backdoor diamond/king blockers to realize equity. Min-raising (200 total) is the +EV play here versus simply calling 50 and allowing him to see a cheap flop for free.

Re-raising with AK to 600 (3x Minni's raise). AK is a premium hand that demands aggression in heads-up play. This raise accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) gets value from weaker hands in Minni's wide raising range, (2) wins the pot immediately against complete air, (3) if called, I'm in position to take the hand down post-flop with my strong holdings. The stack depths are good for this aggressive play.

Claude 3-bet to 600 after my min-raise to 200. The pot is 800 and calling would cost me 400 more (total commit 600), so I need about 400/(800+400)=33.3% equity to justify a call. K♦5♦ has some backdoor diamond/king value, but is frequently dominated by a typical 3-bet range (AK/AQ and big pairs) and is only marginal versus wider 3-bets. A light 4-bet to 1000 is too small to fold out many hands and too thin a bluff with this holding; a larger bluff/shove is over-committing with a marginal hand. With deep stacks and plenty of spots to apply pressure in position later, this is a spot to concede and preserve chips rather than chase a marginal equity call. Folding keeps my stack healthy for better spots.

In heads-up play, position is paramount. A8o is a strong holding when playing from the button against a single opponent. Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) builds the pot while applying pressure. This allows me to establish initiative and control the hand through position. The raise is stronger than calling and gives me multiple paths to win the hand post-flop.

Facing a 2.5x open to 250 from the SB. Pot is 350 and I need to call 150 more (call break-even = 150/(350+150) = 30%). My hand K♦4♥ is marginal but has decent high-card/showdown value heads-up and plays well postflop versus a wide SB opening range. With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot playable and avoid 3-betting with a weak kicker — a raise to 400 would commit more with a hand that often gets dominated. Plan: call to see a flop cheaply; if I pair the K I’ll proceed cautiously (small value bets or check-call vs heavy action), fold to large aggression when missed, and use position/stack to extract value or fold as appropriate.

Out of position (BB) on A♥ T♣ 8♠ with K♦4♥ — I missed the flop and have only K‑high with a weak kicker. Claude opened fairly wide preflop, so his range contains a lot of Ax and broadways that dominate me. With deep stacks and a marginal hand I want to control the pot and see how he reacts rather than build a pot by leading. Plan: check to keep the pot small, call small continuation bets to realize equity, and fold to large aggression or rivered strength. If he checks back I get a free turn to improve or to probe later.

I hold two pair (Aces and Eights) on a flop with significant straight draw possibilities. Minni's check indicates weakness or a marginal hand. Betting 350 extracts value from weaker holdings while preventing opponent from catching draws on free cards. This bet maintains aggression and builds the pot while I'm ahead. The amount is proportional to the pot (500) and applies appropriate pressure in heads-up play.

Out of position on A♥ T♣ 8♠ holding K♦4♥. I checked the flop and Claude (SB/button) put in a sizable C‑bet: 350 into a 850 pot. Calling 350 to win ~1200 requires ~29% equity (350/(850+350) ≈ 29%). My hand has missed the flop entirely, has no realistic backdoor nut draws, and is frequently dominated by the kinds of hands Claude will continuation‑bet here (lots of Ax, Tx, 8x and broadway combos). Even if I hit a King I’m often still behind (A‑K, better Kx lines) or face difficult river decisions. Raising OOP with K4 is thin and riskier — I don’t have the blockers or equity to credibly bluff/charge. Given the bet size, villain’s range, and deep stacks, this is a spot to preserve chips and wait for a better, in‑position hand. Fold.

Raise to 200 total. In a heads-up spot on the button with 6♠9♠ and deep effective stacks (~17.9k) this is a very playable, positionally advantaged hand — I want initiative and fold equity. A min-raise to 200 (I’ll be putting in 150 more chips) pressures Claude’s wide BB defense, builds a pot I can play postflop in position, and keeps the sizing small enough to call with many of his hands. If he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and leverage position; if he 3-bets large I’ll fold this marginal holding, but I’ll consider a call to small 3-bets depending on sizing and SPR. Calling is too passive and folding too tight — raise to 200.

I'm in the Big Blind heads-up with A8o against Minni's button raise to 200. This is a solid hand in a heads-up situation. Minni's 200 raise from the button is a standard steal attempt with a wide range. In heads-up play, I should be playing aggressively with my premium holdings.

A8 is a strong hand that plays well both pre-flop and post-flop. Rather than just calling, I should raise to apply pressure and narrow Minni's range while building the pot with a strong hand. A raise to 500 (total) provides good sizing - it's not overly aggressive but shows strength and forces Minni into a difficult decision.

This approach:
1. Punishes frequent button raises with position
2. Gets value from my strong hand
3. Controls the pot size
4. Maintains aggressive play which has been working well recently

Claude 3-bet to 500 after my min-raise to 200. I have 9♠6♠ — a speculative, positionally advantaged hand with good flop-playability and backdoor flush/straight potential. A 4-bet to 800 with this holding is unnecessary and too thin; folding is too passive given the pot odds and the initiative I want to keep. Calling 300 more (500 total) gets ~30% pot odds (300 into a 700 pot) and allows me to use position postflop to c-bet favorable textures or float and take the pot away. Plan: call, evaluate flop — continue on spade/connected/low boards, and concede to heavy aggression on dry Broadway boards or a large shove without improvement.

Top pair with an ace kicker on a dry 8-high board is a strong holding in heads-up play. I'm ahead of most of Minni's range and should bet for value and protection. A bet around 450 chips (roughly 45% of the pot) applies appropriate pressure while extracting value from worse hands, encouraging calls from weaker pairs and drawing hands. This sizing is consistent with my pre-flop aggression and maintains initiative in a heads-up match.

Flop: 8♠ J♣ 5♠. I hold 9♠6♠ — a strong drawing hand (nine spade outs to a flush plus a backdoor straight/pair outs). Claude's bet is small (450 into ~1000; pot is 1450 now). Calling 450 to potentially win a pot of ~1900 requires ~23.7% equity; my flush draw (~35% to hit by river) + extra pair/straight outs more than covers that. I'm in position, so calling preserves fold-equity on later streets, lets me realize equity, and keeps the pot from bloating with a speculative hand. A raise (min to 900) would be too large and thin versus Claude's wide c-bet range; folding is too weak. Plan: call now (see the turn); if I hit the flush or pair I'll bet/raise for value, if the turn bricks and Claude barrels big I'll fold, if he checks I can take the lead. Call 450 more (950 total committed).

Checking on the turn with a pair of eights. The King is a concerning card that could have improved Minni's range significantly (KQ, KJ, KT, straight draws). With one pair and vulnerability to both made hands and draws, checking allows me to control the pot, see Minni's action, and keep my hand alive without overcommitting chips. This is a balanced play that protects my stack while remaining in the hand.

I just completed a spade flush on the turn (K♠) with 9♠6♠. Claude checked the turn after c-betting the flop, which looks like weakness or pot-control rather than a strong spade-heavy line. Pot is 1,900 and I’m in position — this is a clear spot to bet for value and protection. I’ll bet ~2/3 pot (1,200) to extract value from Kx, Jx and worse made hands that will call, charge any stubborn draw-equity, and avoid giving a free river that could change the dynamic. The size is big enough to get value but not so large that a single check-raise commits my stack; I’ll call a reasonable raise and re-evaluate vs a large shove (folding if the raise commits an unprofitable portion of my stack vs a likely higher flush). Betting also keeps control of the hand in position and lets me plan a river line based on Claude’s response.

Calling 1200 more to reach 2400 total on this turn decision. I have a pair of eights with ace kicker on a 8s-Jc-5s-Ks board. While the King is concerning and Minni's turn raise after calling down suggests strength, my hand has reasonable equity against her wide heads-up range. Pot odds of roughly 2.6:1 (paying 1200 to win 3100) support calling with a legitimate pair. The river card will determine the outcome, but folding here would be too tight given the dynamics and my hand strength.

River with two pair (Aces and Eights, King kicker). The Ace on the river significantly strengthened my hand from vulnerable one pair to a strong two-pair holding. Checking allows Minni to make value mistakes with weaker holdings while keeping the pot manageable. Since this is the river and I have a strong hand, I can safely check and call any reasonable bet, maximizing value against her range while maintaining pot control. Against most of her likely holdings (KQ, KJ, KT, AQ, AJ), I'm ahead or have excellent equity.

I turned my 9♠6♠ into a flush when the K♠ hit and bet 1,200; Claude called. The river A♠ changes the board to 8♠ J♣ 5♠ K♠ A♠ — four spades on board. My flush is now 9-high (As Ks 9s 8s 5s) and is vulnerable to any single hole spade higher than a 9 (T♠, J♠, Q♠). Claude’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, check on the K♠ turn, call my turn bet, then check river) is ambiguous, but his river check is more consistent with pot‑control/weakness than an obvious trap. Betting here would extract from Kx/Jx hands but also gets value‑called by the rare better spade and could commit a lot of chips when I’m beat. With the board so wet and a reasonable chance Claude holds a higher spade, the prudent EV‑maximizing line is to pot‑control and go to showdown — check and evaluate showdown rather than turn this into a bigger, riskier pot.

Raising to 250 (2.5x BB) from the button with K7o in heads-up play. This applies positional pressure, is a standard open size, and gives me initiative pre-flop while building a pot where I retain positional advantage post-flop. Minni should be defending a wide range from the big blind, but this raise makes her decision more difficult and allows me to take down the blinds if she folds.

I’m in the big blind (100 already posted) facing a 250 SB open; pot is 350 and I need to call 150. I have 4♥8♥ — a suited, low connector with decent multi-street equity against a wide button opening range. Calling 150 (total 250) is a cheap defend: pot odds (~30%) and the deep stacks give good implied odds to realize draws. I don’t want to 3-bet light OOP with this hand (min-raise to 400 is too small to accomplish much and will put me OOP in a bloated pot). Plan: call, see the flop, fold to strong aggression on dry boards, and continue only with hearts, pairs, or strong draws.

I hold 8♥4♥ on 8♣ K♠ Q♠ — I flopped a pair of eights. Against a button open in heads‑up this is a hand with decent showdown value: it beats unpaired A/J/T/9 hands in his wide range but loses to Kx/Qx and bigger made hands. The board has two spades but no single‑card spade draw (only a backdoor possibility), so there’s limited immediate draw equity to worry about. With deep stacks I want pot control and to induce bluffs rather than bloat the pot with a vulnerable one‑pair hand. I’ll check to keep worse hands and bluffs in and call small‑to‑medium bets on the flop, folding only to very large, polarizing aggression. Checking also lets me re‑evaluate cheaply on the turn.

Betting 175 on the flop with top pair (K7 on K-Q-8ss). This is a standard c-bet at ~35% of pot that continues my pre-flop aggression, extracts value from weaker holdings in Minni's checking range (pairs, draws), and properly charges any flush draw. The board is relatively dry, and my position allows me to control the action. This bet maintains initiative while prudently sizing to avoid overcommitting with a weak kicker.

I have 8♥4♥ and flopped a pair of eights on 8♣ K♠ Q♠. Claude's small c-bet (175 into a 675 pot) is consistent with a wide, often uncommitted heads‑up continuation range — many bluffs/missed broadways and some thin Kx/Qx. My one pair has decent showdown value heads‑up and I want pot control with a vulnerable single pair against likely better K/Q hands. Raising here bloats the pot and turns many better hands into costing me more; calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in and lets me re-evaluate on the turn. Call 175, fold to large turn aggression, and proceed if I improve or get favorable action.

I have 8♥4♥ on 8♣ K♠ Q♠ 2♥ — one pair of eights with a weak kicker. Claude opened the button and made a very small flop c-bet (175), which is consistent with a wide, often uncommitted heads‑up continuation range (missed broadways, thin Kx/Qx, some bluffs). The turn 2♥ is a blank for most of his value combos and doesn't realistically give me a flush (there are not enough hearts left on board to make a five‑card heart flush). With a vulnerable single pair I want pot control and to keep worse hands and bluffs in — betting or raising here bloats the pot and often gets value from hands that already beat me (Kx/Qx) while folding out some bluffs I want to extract value from on the river. Plan: check and either call small/medium river bets (I’ll mainly call bets up to ~½ pot ≈ 400–450) and fold to large, polarized river aggression (pot‑sized or bigger). If Claude checks back we go to showdown; if he chooses to bluff the river I can call or raise depending on sizing.

Turn card analysis: I have top pair with K7o on a K-Q-8-2 board. Minni checked the turn after calling my flop bet, which suggests uncertainty or a draw-heavy holding. My K-high with the weak 7 kicker is vulnerable, so I should continue applying pressure with a value bet rather than checking and giving free cards. A ~450-chip bet (approximately 50% of pot) maintains my aggression, extracts value from Minni's checking range (weaker pairs, Q-x hands, or flush draws), and charges any remaining equity. The deuce on the turn was a blank that likely didn't help her range significantly. Betting here keeps the initiative and builds the pot when I hold a hand that's ahead of much of her checking range in heads-up play.

I have 8♥4♥ — one pair of eights (very weak kicker) on K♠ Q♠ 8♣ 2♥. Claude (button) opened preflop, made a small flop c‑bet (175) and now continues with a modest turn bet of 450 into a 1300 pot (~35% pot). That line and sizing point to a wide, often uncommitted range (missed broadways, thin Kx/Qx and bluffs). The turn 2♥ is essentially a blank for his value combos and, importantly, it does not give me a realistic flush (I can’t make a heart flush by the river), so my hand remains a single vulnerable pair. Calling 450 gives me ~25–26% pot odds (450 to win ~1750), and my pair has roughly that equity versus his continuing range; plus stacks are deep so I retain implied odds to win bigger if he bluffs or I improve. Raising would bloat the pot with a vulnerable hand and fold out worse hands I want to keep; folding concedes too many thin value/bluff combos. Plan: call 450, control the pot, and re‑evaluate on the river — fold to very large/polarized river bets (pot‑sized or shove), call smaller bets, or value/raise only if I improve.

It’s checked to me on the river. I hold 8♥4♥ — one pair of eights (very weak kicker) on 8♣ K♠ Q♠ 2♥ Q♦. Claude opened preflop and ran a small flop c-bet (175) and a moderate turn bet (450) — a line consistent with a wide heads‑up range that includes Kx, Qx and bluffs. The river pairs the queen: any Q in his hand becomes trips/full house and would very often bet for value; his checking to me makes Q less likely but doesn’t remove Kx or other hands that beat my pair. Betting here would mostly fold out the worse hands I beat (A‑x, missed broadways) and bloat the pot when I’m behind; bluffing has limited clear fold equity and is high variance. With deep stacks and thin value from a vulnerable one‑pair, the lowest‑risk +EV line is to check and take showdown.

With K7 on a K-Q-8-2-Q final board, I hold top pair but with a weak seven kicker. The river card (Qd) paired the board and is dangerous for my range, potentially completing multiple hands in Minni's calling range (any Queen gives her trips/full house). Minni checked to me on the river, suggesting either weakness or a trap. Given my marginal hand strength, betting risks losing additional chips to better hands without gaining meaningful value from worse hands that would likely fold anyway. Checking allows me to reach showdown without further exposure on an unfavorable river card. This is the correct play with a weak top pair against an opponent who checked to me.

Heads-up on the button with K♠4♠ — a suited King is a reasonable, open-raising hand in this spot. I’ve already posted the SB (50); calling would be 50 more (100 total) but that surrenders initiative. With a deep stack and one opponent, I want to open a wide stealing range and get value when I hit. I’ll raise to 3x the BB (300 total) — big enough to generate fold equity and thin out speculative hands, but not so large that I overcommit with a marginal holding. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable spade/high-card or wet boards, use position to control pot size, and fold to a sizable 3‑bet (K4s is often dominated vs strong 3-betting ranges).

Raising to 800 total (700 additional bet). AJ offsuit is strong enough to 3-bet the button steal raise from SB. This move takes initiative, applies pressure, and defines my hand's strength. Even if called, I have a solid hand with multiple ways to improve. The raise also compounds pressure given similar stack sizes - I'm not risking excessive chips but gaining favorable fold equity and a better position if called.

I opened to 300 with K♠4♠ to steal heads‑up, but Claude’s reraise to 800 is a sizeable 3‑bet that represents a fairly strong/polarized range (big pairs, A‑x, broadway, some suited aces). Calling 500 more would require ~31.25% equity (500/(1100+500)), and K4s has only marginal equity and is often dominated versus that range. 4‑betting to 1300+ would be committing with poor blockers and is a thin bluff. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left, preserving my 18.95k and waiting for a better spot is the correct play — I fold.

K9o is a reasonable hand to play in position from the button against Minni in the big blind. I have positional advantage which will be significant postflop, allowing me to outplay most of Minni's range. A standard raise to 250 (2.5x BB) accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) takes the initiative, (2) can win the blinds immediately, and (3) if called, I maintain position with a playable hand. Given the tournament dynamics at hand 67/150 and roughly equal stack sizes, this is a solid aggressive play that fits my winning approach from the previous hands where I've been profitable with position-based aggression.

Heads-up vs Claude (button/SB) who opened to 250 (~2.5bb). I hold 8h2s — a very weak hand but in heads-up the BB should defend fairly wide to avoid being exploited. The pot is 350 and I must call 150 more; calling to 250 total gives good pot odds (~30% equity required) and lets me see a flop cheaply with deep stacks. 3-betting this garbage OOP is unnecessary and folding is too nitty. I’ll call and proceed cautiously on the flop.

Board Jc 8d 7d; I hold 8h2s — middle pair with a terrible kicker and two diamonds on board. Claude opened preflop so his flop range contains lots of Jx (which beat me), better pocket pairs, and many diamond draws/straight draws. Out of position with a marginal pair I want pot control and to realize equity rather than inflate the pot by leading; a bet will often get called or raised by better hands and won’t fold enough worse. I’ll check and evaluate: call a small-to-medium c-bet (keep the pot manageable and extract vs bluffs/worse), fold to heavy raises, and if he checks back I keep my showdown value. Checking is the lowest-variance, +EV line in this spot.

Check on the flop with K9o against Minni's check on a 7-8-J board. My hand is weak (king high with gut-shot draw), and checking allows me to gather information on the turn while avoiding investing in a weak holding. I maintain positional advantage while being selective with my aggression.

I have 8h2s on Jc 8d 7d Ts — second pair with a terrible kicker. Claude opened preflop so his range contains lots of Jx, Tx, and Broadway hands; the turn Ten also gives any 9 a made straight. Out of position with a marginal pair, betting here OOP is thin and just bloats the pot when many of his holdings already beat me. Pot is only 500 and stacks are deep, so the lowest-variance, +EV line is to keep the pot small and see a river. I'll check and plan to call small-to-medium turn bets (he could be firing as a bluff or pricing in worse pairs) but fold to large value bets or raises that strongly indicate Jx/TX/9x. If he checks back we re-evaluate on the river.

I have a K-high straight (8-9-T-J-K) which is a made strong hand on the turn. Minni has checked twice, showing weakness. My K-high straight is ahead of most reasonable holdings in her check-back range. I'm betting 300 (about 60% of current pot) to value-extract from her likely weaker holdings like second pairs, draws, or king-high hands. This sizing keeps her engaged while building the pot with my strong made hand.

Call 300. I have second pair (8s) with a terrible kicker but I'm heads-up against a wide raising range. Claude checked the flop and now fires a small turn bet (300 into ~800) — a sizing that often contains a mix of thin value (Jx, Tx, 9x) and a fair number of bluffs/semi‑bluffs (diamond/straight draws, missed overcards). Calling here gives me good pot odds (~27% required) and keeps worse hands and bluffs in; raising OOP with a marginal hand and bad kicker just bloats the pot and is thin vs his value range. I planned to call small‑to‑medium turn bets and re-evaluate on the river: fold to a large raise, and on a safe river consider a thin value call or small river bet only if checked to me.

I improved to two pair (8s and 2s) on Jc 8d 7d Ts 2c — a strong heads‑up holding but still vulnerable to sets or higher two‑pair combinations. Claude checked the river after a small turn stab, so his river range is wide and includes many single‑pair hands (Jx, Tx, 7x), some missed draws and a few stronger hands. Checking would concede thin value from these worse hands; a full‑pot or large bet would fold out too many callers and only get action from hands that already beat me. I’ll make a medium value bet to extract from one‑pair hands and missed draws while keeping the pot manageable and avoiding big bloats if raised. Bet ~600 (≈half‑pot); if Claude raises large I’ll give credit to a nut/near‑nut holding and fold.

K-high straight is too strong to fold. Minni's check-check line followed by a river raise suggests a bluff or marginal hand rather than a monster. Even if she has a full house, that's a less likely scenario than a bluff given her passive play through two streets. The pot odds of 3.83:1 are excellent for a hand that wins the vast majority of non-full house scenarios. I call for value.

Heads-up on the button (SB) — very wide opening range. 9♣6♦ is marginal but fine to open here rather than limp: raising takes the initiative, applies fold equity, and lets me play postflop in position. I’ll make a standard 2x BB open to 200 (min raise is 200) — big enough to pressure weak big-blind holdings but small enough to keep the pot manageable with deep stacks. If Claude 3-bets heavily I can fold or re-evaluate; if called I’ll use position to c-bet or control the pot depending on the flop.

Folding 2-5o from the big blind. While getting 3:1 odds is mathematically close, I'm facing position disadvantage post-flop and holding the weakest hand in poker. With a chip lead (22,100 vs 17,600), folding marginal hands is the higher EV play. Better spots will come.

With 6-2o (the worst starting hand in poker), I should fold despite having position on the button in heads-up play. While heads-up strategy typically involves playing wider ranges, 6-2 offsuit is still bottom-tier and unplayable. Even with my chip lead (22,050 vs 17,800), I should preserve my advantage by playing strong hands rather than taking marginal spots. The extra 50 chips in cost is not worth the weak equity of this hand against Minni's big blind range. This is a clear fold.

Heads-up on the button with Ad3c is a clear open-raise spot. A3o is strong in heads-up and we have position postflop, so take the initiative rather than limp/call. With deep stacks (~180 BBs) and an opponent who defends widely, raising to 3x the big blind (300) maximizes fold equity, builds the pot when we have the best hand, and gives us easy postflop decision-making in position. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll fold for pot control; if he calls I’ll play aggressively on A-high and low-clutter boards and proceed cautiously on coordinated/paired boards. Calling 50 is too passive and misses value here, so I open to 300.

Raising to 900 with pocket Jacks from the BB. This is a premium hand that should build value against Minni's button raise. A 3x raise puts maximum pressure on the button while building the pot to a size I'm comfortable playing. JJ is well-positioned against Minni's likely range (any two cards from the button), and the raise deters weaker hands while getting value from stronger ones.

I opened to 300 on the button with Ad3c and Claude 3-bet to 900. Heads-up in position with A3o I want to keep the pot manageable and see a flop — A-high has decent equity and the Ace blocker reduces some of Claude’s strongest combos. With deep stacks I can navigate postflop and apply pressure on good boards; a small 4-bet here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and folding surrenders too much. I’ll call the 3-bet and proceed in position: continue aggressively on A-high / dry boards, pot-control or fold on very coordinated/connected boards, and fold to large action if Claude shows heavy strength.

Flopped a set of Jacks with JJ on a board of 5c Jh 4c. This is a very strong hand that beats all hands except AA, KK, and possibly QQ. The board is relatively dry without significant draw possibilities. I'm betting 600 (1/3 pot) to build value while keeping Minni engaged. This sizing allows weaker hands and draws to call while establishing the strength of my position. Standard value bet on a favorable flop with a premium made hand.

Claude’s flop c-bet is very small (600 into a 2400 pot) on a dry 5c–Jh–4c board. I have Ad3c: Ace‑high (the best high card), a weak backdoor club possibility but no pair. Versus a 3‑betting opponent his range contains a lot of strong pairs (JJ/TT/99/55/44), some A‑x and missed broadway hands, and some bluffs. The small bet gives me >20% pot equity vs a fairly wide c‑bet range and the call price is excellent (600 to win ~3000 → break‑even ~20%). Raising here would bloat the pot with only Ace‑high and get me in trouble vs value hands; folding is too tight given my blocker and pot odds. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, use position to see a turn, and re‑evaluate (raise/lead if I improve to top pair or if Claude shows weakness allowing a successful bluff).

Betting 1000 on the turn with my set of Jacks. The board brought a fourth club (8c), but my set remains the strongest hand in most realistic scenarios. Minni's call of the 3-bet and flop bet suggests a drawing hand or mid-tier value hand rather than a premium made hand. I'm betting approximately 1/3 pot (1000 into a 3000 pot) to build value while charging any flush draws. This sizing keeps Minni engaged while maintaining value extraction. The turn is an ideal spot to build the pot before the river when there's limited action remaining. Sets should continue applying pressure in multi-way pots with draws present.

I have Ad3c on 5c–Jh–4c–8c: Ace‑high plus a four‑card club draw (one card to complete a flush). Outs = 9 → 9/46 ≈ 19.6% to hit the river. Claude’s turn bet is 1000 into a 4000 pot, so my break‑even equity to call is 1000/(4000+1000)=20% — the pure flush equity is a hair below that, but I also have extra showdown value with the Ace and I block many A‑combos in Claude’s 3‑betting range. I’m in position and can realize fold equity or extract value on the river depending on the card. Raising here would bloat the pot with a drawing, marginal hand and risks a 3‑bet/commitment against a very strong 3‑betting opponent; folding surrenders too much equity. Call 1000 to see the river. Plan: if I complete the flush I’ll bet/extract value; if I miss I’ll frequently check and fold to large river aggression but may call small river bluffs given my Ace.

Betting 2000 on the river with my set of Jacks. The river Ace doesn't significantly change my hand strength - JJ with a J on board makes three of a kind which remains a premium holding. Minni's check indicates weakness or a marginal hand rather than strength. This bet extracts value from weaker pairs, top pair hands, and marginal holdings in their range. The sizing (2000 into 5000 pot) is approximately 2/5 pot, which is a standard value bet that keeps weaker hands engaged while building the pot. This is consistent with my aggressive line throughout the hand and capitalizes on the informational advantage from Minni's river check.

River changed the hand: I missed the club flush but the Ah pairs the board and I now have top pair (Ad) with a weak 3 kicker. Claude 3‑bet preflop and has fired small bets every street (600 flop, 1000 turn, 2000 river) — a line consistent with both thin value (AK/AQ, sets, two‑club flushes) and a fair number of bluffs/missed hands. Pot = 7000, cost to call = 2000, so break‑even equity ≈ 22.2%. My pair of Aces beats most bluffs and many non‑Ax value hands, and I block some Ace combos by holding Ad. Raising here would be thin and often get called or shoved by better A‑kickers/sets/flushes; folding surrenders too much value. Given the pot odds, board texture and Claude’s sizing pattern, calling 2000 to realize showdown value is the +EV line.

With 2s-9c in the small blind heads-up against Minni, this is one of the weakest holdings in poker. While hand ranges in heads-up play are wider than full-ring, 2-9o is still a clear fold. I have a healthy 26,500 chip stack and can afford to wait for better spots. Minni has shown solid play throughout the tournament, so I cannot exploit this position profitably with such a weak hand. Folding preserves my chips for premium hands where I can generate better value.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening a wide range — K8s is a strong heads-up hand (suited king with decent kicker) and plays well in position. Raising gives me initiative, fold equity and lets me take control postflop. Standard sizing here is 2x the big blind, so I’ll raise to 200 total. Claude is deep-stacked and has shown aggression at times, so if he 3-bets very large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold K8s vs a heavy 3-bet), but if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on Ace-high or very connected boards.

Folding K5o from the big blind. While I have a significant chip lead (26,400 vs 13,300) and reasonable pot odds (calling 100 to win 300), K5 offsuit is simply too weak to defend with. This hand has poor equity against Minni's raising range and lacks playability on most flops. Additionally, Minni has been an aggressive raiser throughout the tournament, so this raise likely represents a somewhat strong hand. With such a large chip advantage, I can afford to fold marginal hands and wait for better spots. Folding unprofitable situations is critical for long-term tournament success.

Complete the small blind with K5o in heads-up position. The 50 chip call is minimal and provides excellent position post-flop. Minni has shown passive tendencies, making this exploitable. With 2:1 chip advantage and button position, this is a standard call to see a flop.

Check. With 2c9s heads-up vs a limp this is a very weak, offsuit holding — no reason to bloat the pot preflop. Pot is only 200 and Claude’s limp range is wide, so I’ll see a flop for free and fold to standard continuation bets unless I pair a 9 or 2 or hit a very favorable multi-street board. Reserve raises/3-bets for real hands (pairs, broadways, suited connectors) and wait for better spots to steal or apply pressure.

Check. Heads-up vs a limp I’ve got trash (2♣9♠) — no pair and only a lone spade on K♦-5♠-3♠, so no realistic one-card draw (only remote runner-runner outs). Pot is small (200) and Claude’s limp range is very wide, so betting for value is pointless and a donk bet just bloats the pot with an inferior hand. I’ll check to keep the pot small and fold to a standard continuation bet; if Claude checks back I can see turn/river cheaply, if he bets I’ll fold unless it’s an unusually tiny probe that gives correct odds to continue.

I hold K5o (King-Five) on a flop of Kd-5s-3s, giving me top pair with a weak kicker. Minni checked from the big blind, indicating weakness. Key considerations: (1) Minni is short-stacked (13,500 vs my 26,300), pushing them toward push/fold ranges; (2) The board is dry with minimal draw potential - only spade draws are possible; (3) I have position and late-stage game momentum; (4) Minni's passive tendencies from the hand history suggest they check weak hands frequently. A standard continuation bet of 120 chips (roughly 60% pot) is appropriate here to: build the pot with what's likely the best hand, charge any drawing hands, and maintain aggression in heads-up play. This bet size is exploitative against Minni's passive play while being reasonable given stack depths.

Fold. I have 2c9s on Kd-5s-3s: no pair and only a remote backdoor flush (needs both turn+river spades, ~4%). I have ~6 outs to pair by the river (~24% equity to improve), but calling 120 into the 320 pot (final pot 440) requires roughly 27% equity to break even. Claude limp-called preflop and this-sized c-bet is consistent with many value Kx/5x hands and standard continuation bets, so my equity is insufficient. A raise would be a thin bluff vs his deep stack and wide range; calling wastes chips with poor multi-street prospects. Fold to preserve chips for better spots.

Heads-up on the button with J♣5♥. With deep stacks and only one opponent, I want to play aggressively and take the initiative rather than just limp/call. J5o is marginal but has decent showdown potential and blocks some J combos — good enough to open a wide heads-up range. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to apply pressure and build the pot when I’m likely ahead. Postflop plan: c-bet frequently on dry boards, continue when I hit a pair or a good draw, and use position to control the pot. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll fold; I’ll consider calling small 3-bets and play in position.

Calling 200 with QsTo. Getting favorable 2:1 pot odds on a marginal but playable hand. While out of position pre-flop, I have last action post-flop and can leverage stack size advantage. QsTo has decent flop potential and if I miss, I can fold to continued aggression. The pot odds justify the call against Minni's aggressive button play.

Betting 400 with top pair (QsTo) on a 4s-As-Qc flop. Minni checked, indicating weakness, and I have significant stack advantage. This bet applies pressure, extracts value from weaker holdings, and takes down pots when Minni can't connect. The bet size (~2/3 pot) is reasonable for my hand strength and board situation.

Facing A♠ Q♣ 4♠ with J♣5♥: pot is 1000 and Claude has bet 400, so calling 400 would require ~28.6% equity (call 400 to win 1400). I only have 6 clean outs to pair by the river (three J’s + three 5’s) → probability to hit at least one by river ≈ 24% (1 - (41/47)*(40/46)), which is below the required equity. Claude called preflop and is betting the flop; his range will be heavy on A‑x, Q‑x and other hands that currently beat me, so my hand has poor equity and little fold equity if I raise. Raising as a bluff with so few blockers is risky versus a deeper, aggressive stack. With negative immediate pot odds and an unfavourable range, folding preserves my stack for better spots.

3c-2s is the worst hand in poker. While I'm on the button with a chip lead and only need to call 50 more chips, this hand is too weak to justify playing even with decent pot odds. I have a significant stack advantage (26,750 vs 13,100) and should be selective with my hands, particularly in early positions. Folding here preserves my chip lead and allows me to win the blind while waiting for better spots to accumulate chips. In heads-up play with a dominant chip position, picking better hands is more profitable than playing marginal hands.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with the best possible starting hand: Ad As. Pot is 150 and it’s 50 to complete. Vs a single opponent with a deep stack I want to build the pot and isolate while denying cheap odds to speculative hands. I’ll make a strong but non-committal open-raise to extract value and discourage limp/call ranges — large enough to get calls from broadways/pairs/suited aces but small enough to leave plenty of postflop maneuvering room (I’ll have ~12,000 left). If Claude 3-bets I can comfortably 4-bet or re-evaluate by sizing; if he calls I’ll proceed to extract on most runouts. Raise to 1200 total.

Td6d is too weak to play from the BB against Minni's button raise. No pair, poor connectivity, weak kickers. Even with heads-up play and short stacks, this hand doesn't justify calling 1,100. I have position advantage in future hands with better holdings. Fold and maintain stack management.

JK is a premium hand in heads-up poker. I'm in position with a significant chip lead (2:1) against Minni. A standard raise to 250 (2.5x BB) applies pressure, builds the pot with a strong hand, and gives me positional advantage post-flop. This is aggressive but appropriate poker in heads-up format where ranges are wider and position is crucial.

Heads-up vs Claude (button) who opened to 250 (2.5x). I have J♠5♦ offsuit in the big blind: marginal but worth defending heads-up. Folding is too tight; 3‑betting with J5o is a poor bluff (weak blockers, little post‑flop playability) and would bloat the pot out of position. I’ll call the 150 more (total 250) to see a flop and then evaluate — continue on a jack/5 or good draw, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy action.

Heads‑up out of position on A♥ 3♦ 9♦ with J♠5♦ — no pair, only a weak backdoor (runner‑runner) diamond possibility. Claude was the preflop raiser and will c‑bet a high frequency on this dry ace‑high flop; I don’t want to bloated the pot with a marginal hand against a bigger stack. I’ll check to control the pot size and keep the initiative with the better hands in his range. Plan: if Claude makes a small probe (min–third‑pot) I can call to see a turn (or continue if I pick up a diamond or a jack); fold to heavy pressure or large turn/river barrels since I have very little showdown value.

I'm in position with a significant chip lead (2:1) and a premium hand (JK). The flop is Ah-3d-9d and Minni has checked to me. This is a textbook spot for a continuation bet. With overcards to most of the board and hand strength, I should maintain aggression in position. A bet of 350 (70% pot) is standard sizing - it applies pressure on Minni's checked range while keeping the bet proportional. This bet defines Minni's holding and wins the pot immediately if she doesn't have a strong hand. If she calls, I maintain position and can reassess on the turn. With my stack advantage and positional edge, consistent aggression is the correct strategy in heads-up play.

I checked the flop to control the pot and see a continuation. Claude now fires 350 into the ~500 pre-bet pot (pot is 850). Calling 350 would require roughly 29% equity (350/(850+350)). With J♠5♦ I have virtually no showdown value — only a very thin runner-runner diamond/straight chance (flush needs both turn+river diamonds, ~4%) and a few unlikely runner-runner straights. Against a standard HU c-bet range (lots of A‑x, broadways and pairs) my real equity is well below the required ~29%. Raising as a bluff is unattractive: poor blockers, out of position, and will bloat the pot vs a deeper, aggressive opponent. I’ll fold and preserve my 13.1k stack for better, more favorable spots (especially in position or with real equity).

Heads-up on the button with A♣J♦ — a strong, proactive hand against Claude’s very wide BB range. I want initiative and fold equity while keeping the size reasonable relative to my 13k stack. Raising to 300 (3x the BB, 150 more chips) builds the pot for value, denies cheap flops to dominated aces/face cards, and puts pressure on draws/weak holdings. Plan: continuation-barrel favorable flops; if Claude 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or call depending on sizing and pot odds), but versus a call I can play postflop for value and position.

5-2o is the worst hand in poker with minimal equity against Minni's raising range. Out of position in the BB, I don't have sufficient odds to call. With a chip advantage (26,800 vs 12,800), I can afford to be selective. This is a clear fold - I'll look for better opportunities in upcoming hands.

In this heads-up situation on the button with a significant chip lead (26,750 vs 13,100), I have position advantage and should be playing aggressively. Jack-Five is a reasonable hand in heads-up play - it's above the median hand strength. From the button, J5o is a standard raise candidate, especially given my positional advantage and chip lead. Minni's play has been somewhat conservative based on the hand history, suggesting I can apply pressure with a standard opening raise. I'll raise to 250 (approximately 2.5x the big blind), which is a standard opening raise that applies consistent pressure while building the pot when ahead.

9♦2♥ is one of the worst hands. Claude opened to 250 from the button (acts last postflop), so I’d be out of position for the rest of the hand. Pot is 350 and I must call 150, so I need roughly 150/(350+150)=30% equity to justify a call. 9-2o is heavily dominated by most of Claude’s opening range and has well under the equity needed, and it has very poor postflop playability OOP on deep stacks. A tiny 3-bet to 400 as a bluff is possible in some spots, but with this hand it’s a losing play long-term. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button with pocket 5s and deep stacks (~130BB). This is a strong heads-up holding — I want to take the initiative, build a pot and deny Claude cheap odds to see flop. Standard heads-up sizing of ~3x the BB is appropriate, so I’ll raise to 300. If Claude flats I’ll play aggressively on low/paired/drawy flops; if he 3-bets small I can call to set-mine, but I’ll re-evaluate or fold to very large 3-bets that commit too many chips. Overall: raise to seize initiative and extract value from overcards and weaker hands.

I'm in the Big Blind facing a raise to 300 from Minni on the button with 7-2, historically the worst hand in poker. While heads-up play calls for a wider range of hands, 7-2 is universally weak and cannot win without significant board help. Given my chip lead (26,800 vs 12,800), there's no strategic necessity to take this marginal spot. I should fold and wait for better opportunities where I have more equity. In heads-up, position and stack pressure matter more than playing marginal hands out of position with weak cards.

Heads-up button raise with pocket 4s. Standard 3x raise (100 BB base) to 300 total. This applies pressure given my 2:1 chip advantage while keeping the raise reasonable for a small pair. Position is excellent postflop if called. Minni is in critical stack territory (~6.5 BB), making fold equity valuable.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I hold 4♠9♥ — a weak, marginal offsuit hand but facing a 200-chip call into a 400-chip pot gives ~3:1 pot odds. Button raising ranges heads-up are wide, and with deep stacks there is some implied odds to realize. I’m out of position postflop, so I won’t 3-bet light (4-9o has poor fold equity and playability as a raise). Plan: call to 300 to see a flop cheaply, fold to heavy, non-savable pressure on dry boards, and continue only on favorable pairs/draws or to float against small c-bets. Pot control and realization of equity is optimal here, so call.

Heads-up out of position with 4♠9♥ on 5♥7♥8♦ — no made hand but reasonable equity. I have ~10 clean outs (3 fours, 3 nines, 4 sixes) — ~21% to hit on the turn and ~38–40% by the river. Claude opened wide from the button and will c-bet frequently, and the current pot (600) means a standard half-pot c-bet (~300–400) is cheap to call and offers the pot/implied odds to realize these outs with my 12.9k stack. I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP or bluff-raise with a marginal hand, so I’ll check to control the pot and call small-to-medium continuation bets to try to hit or float. I’ll fold to large overbets or persistent pressure and re-evaluate on the turn (bet only if I pair or pick up a strong draw).

Continuation bet with pocket 4s on a coordinated but currently-favorable flop. The 5-7-8d board contains multiple straight draws and heart flush possibilities that can hurt my hand if given a free card. Minni checked from big blind in a critical shortstack spot, indicating likely weakness. A ~400 bet (67% pot) applies appropriate pressure, protects my hand, and extracts value while maintaining fold equity given Minni's desperate chip situation. I maintain positional advantage with a premium pocket pair that's ahead of most of Minni's range.

Out of position with 4♠9♥ on 5♥7♥8♦. Claude (button) opened wide preflop and his flop bet of 400 into the ~600 pot means calling 400 asks me to have ~28.6% equity (400/(1000+400)). I have ~10 clean outs (3 fours, 3 nines, 4 sixes) — ~21% to hit the turn and ~38% by the river — and stacks are deep enough (my 12.9k vs his 26.1k) to make this a profitable chase. I don’t have the hand or position to bluff-raise profitably, and a raise to 800 would be thin with poor fold equity and worse postflop prospects. I’ll call 400 to realize equity, keep the pot controlled, and re-evaluate on the turn: continue aggressively only if I pair or complete the straight/draw, fold to large turn overbets or persistent pressure.

Check. I hold 4♠9♥ on 5♥7♥8♦5♣ — no made hand but roughly 10 outs (3 fours, 3 nines, 4 sixes) for ~21–22% to hit the river. I’m out of position with a paired board that reduces my fold equity and increases the likelihood Claude has a 5 or a made/top pair. The pot is already 1,400 and there’s no need to bloat it OOP or bluff into a wide but often-calling button. Checking keeps the pot under control and preserves my ability to call a reasonable turn bet to realize equity; I’ll fold to polarizing overbets that represent a full house/two-pair heavy range.

Betting two pair (5s and 4s) on the turn after Minni checks twice. The paired board helped my hand while reducing Minni's showdown equity. Given Minni's shortstack and weak action pattern, this bet extracts value from drawing hands, pressures weaker pairs, and builds the pot when I'm ahead. A check would be passive against a desperate opponent with likely inferior holdings.

Fold. Pot is 2200 and I must call 800, so I need ~26.7% equity to make a call +EV. My hand (4♠9♥) on 5♥7♥8♦5♣ has at-best nominally ~10 outs (3 fours, 3 nines, 4 sixes → ~21.7% to hit the river), but the 4s and 9s are often not clean (lose to 7x/8x and many of Claude’s value hands). Only the four sixes are reliably strong. I’m out of position and Claude’s line (button open, flop c-bet, turn bet after the board pairs) is consistent with a value-heavy range (top pair/two pair/trips). A call is a (-)EV by pot odds and a raise would be an extremely thin bluff OOP with poor fold equity. Fold to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 3♥4♣ — a weak but perfectly playable hand in heads-up play. I have position and deep stacks (≈124bb), so opening a wide range is +EV. Folding is too tight; calling 50 gives Claude cheap odds and no initiative. I’ll raise to 300 (3×BB) to generate fold equity and take control of the hand. Size: 3× discourages some light 3-bets while keeping the pot manageable. Postflop plan: if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favourable, fold to heavy aggression on turns/rivers when I have little equity, and probe for value when I connect. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll usually fold 3-4 offsuit, but I may defend vs small 3-bets to realize equity.

With 9-6 offsuit from the big blind, I'm holding a weak hand that doesn't warrant a call. While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (2:1), several factors favor folding: (1) This is a marginal hand with poor connectivity, (2) I'm out of position post-flop, (3) I maintain a significant chip advantage over Minni (27,400 vs 12,200), allowing me to play tight and let short stacks take unnecessary risks, and (4) I can preserve my stack and wait for better spots with premium holdings. With a healthy chip position, folding weak hands is the profitable long-term strategy.

K7 suited is a solid heads-up hand, especially given my significant chip advantage. I'm raising to 3x the big blind (300 total) to apply pressure on Minni's big blind. This is standard aggressive play when ahead in chips and position. The hand has decent potential with the King high and suited connector, and if Minni folds, I gain chips. If called, I'll have position and can play the hand accordingly post-flop.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. I hold Js2d in the big blind — a weak hand but it has some showdown value against a wide heads-up opening range. The price to see a flop is small (200 into my 12,500 stack), so folding would be too nitty. 3-betting is thin and not ideal with this offsuit hand (poor blockers, often dominated), so I prefer to keep the pot manageable and realize equity postflop. I will call the 200 to see a flop and then fold to large, committed aggression if I miss; continue only when I pair or pick up meaningful equity.

I have J♠2♦ — bottom pair (pair of deuces) on a 2♣-3♣-Q♣ flop. This is a very wet-looking board for clubs and Qx: Claude’s heads-up opening range contains a lot of clubs and many Qx combos, so I’m behind to a lot of hands (Qc, Qx, any club) but ahead of many missed broadways and some weaker holdings. Leading out OOP with a marginal pair is unattractive because a raise usually represents a hand that beats me and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. I’ll check to control the pot size, induce bluffs, and convert this into a standard check-call situation: call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize value from worse hands (A/K/high cards) but fold to large, committed aggression that strongly represents a made Q or a club flush. Re-evaluate on the turn — bet/raise only if I improve to two pair/trips or if I get a very favorable action from Claude.

Continuation bet on the flop after Minni's check. With a significant chip advantage in heads-up play and position, I should apply pressure with my pre-flop raiser range. The 350 bet represents a 58% pot bet, reasonable for a c-bet that puts Minni in a tough spot with a short stack. If Minni folds, I win the pot. If called, I retain position on the turn and can evaluate further. K7 is marginal but my aggression is justified by position and chip advantage.

Board is 2♣-3♣-Q♣; I hold J♠-2♦ (a pair of deuces). Claude’s small c-bet (350 into a 950 pot) is consistent with a wide heads‑up continuation range (Qx, clubs, draws, overcards, and bluffs) and gives me ~21% pot odds to call (350 to win ~1650). My pair has real showdown value versus that wide range and beats many of his bluffs/overcards. Raising OOP here is too thin — I lack good blockers and would be building a pot on a dangerous monotone board — and folding would be too nitty given the price. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled and realize equity, then re-evaluate on the turn: fold to large, committed aggression if the turn brings a club/queen or he shows strong action; continue only if I improve or get a favorable action.

Heads‑up vs Claude (button) who opened wide preflop and c‑bet a small amount on a three‑club Q‑high flop. I hold J♠2♦ — bottom pair (pair of deuces). The turn (6♦) doesn’t bring a club or a queen, so my hand still has real showdown value vs a wide continuation range (many bluffs/overcards) but is behind Qx and any two‑club combo. Out of position I want to control the pot and allow Claude to bluff; leading or raising OOP on a monotone board without strong blockers is too thin. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs, and call small‑to‑medium bets that are likely from worse hands or bluffs, but fold to large, polarizing bets that represent a made Q or a club flush. Re‑evaluate on the river: value/defend if a safe low card comes, fold or proceed cautiously if a club/queen or heavy aggression appears.

Minni has checked twice on a relatively static board (2c 3c Qc 6d is a blank turn), suggesting a weak hand or draw. In heads-up play with a commanding chip advantage (26.7k vs 11.9k), I should continue applying pressure with another c-bet. My K7s has genuine equity - I'm currently King-high with a 13-out spade flush draw (approximately 27% equity against most reasonable holdings). The turn bet size of 700 represents a 50% pot bet, which is appropriately aggressive while keeping Minni's stack short enough that folding would be costly relative to her remaining chips. If Minni is weak, I win the pot; if she's on a draw, I'm likely ahead; if she's slow-playing, I'll get more chips in with equity. This continues my aggressive, positional strategy that has worked well throughout the tournament.

Board: 2♣-3♣-Q♣-6♦, I hold J♠-2♦ — bottom pair (deuces). Pot is 2000 and Claude bets 700, so calling 700 wins a 2700 pot (≈25.9% break-even equity). Claude opened wide from the button and made a small c-bet on the flop then a modest turn bet — a line consistent with a wide range (Qx, single-club hands, some two-club flushes, and bluffs). My hand has real showdown value vs his wide barreling range and the price is reasonable. Raising OOP with bottom pair on a three‑club board is too thin (I have poor club/queen blockers and risk bloating the pot when frequently behind to a made flush or Qx). Folding here would be too nitty given the pot odds. I will call the 700 to realize equity and see the river, and then fold to a large, polarizing river bet (or if a club/queen arrives and he fires again). If the river is a safe low card and he checks or bets small, I’ll proceed to value/defend; otherwise keep pot control or fold as appropriate.

I hold J♠2♦ — bottom pair with a weak kicker on 2♣-3♣-Q♣-6♦-7♦. Claude opened wide preflop and took a modest c-bet line on flop and turn, so his continuing range is still wide (Qx, some two-club combos that would already have a flush, overcards and bluffs). Betting out of position with a small pair here is thin: I have poor blockers, risk getting raised by better hands (Qx/flush/sets) and will often fold out the very hands I beat. Checking keeps the pot under control, gives Claude the chance to bluff missed club draws/overcards, and lets me call a small river stab for good odds while folding to a large, polarized bet. I’ll check and take it to showdown rather than bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand.

Minni has checked the river after previously checking the turn, which indicates a weak holding or missed draw. The river 7d actually improved my hand to a pair of sevens with King kicker - a solid hand in this spot.

My hand analysis:

Betting strategy:

This is sound aggressive play with position and a made hand against a weak river action.

Pot is 4,100 and Claude has bet 1,400 on the river, so calling 1,400 wins a total 5,500 — I need ≈25.5% equity to break even. Claude opened wide from the button and took a small c-bet line on a very club-heavy flop and a modest turn bet; that line contains a lot of Qx, some made flushes (two clubs in hand), but also many single‑card overcards, K/A high hands, and thin bluffs. My J♠2♦ (pair of deuces) is a very weak pair, but it has real showdown value against a wide continuing range and I beat most pure bluffs and missed overcards. Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with A7o (As7c) — a top-ish heads-up hand. Calling 50 surrenders initiative and lets Claude play pot control; I want to take the lead and build the pot with position. I'm in the big blind with K-T offsuit against a short-stacked opponent (Minni at 9,550 chips). The math is straightforward:

1. **Pot odds**: I'm calling 200 to win 400, needing 33% equity. K-T is well above this threshold in a heads-up scenario.

2. **Stack dynamics**: I have a 3:1 chip advantage (30k vs 9.5k), which means I can afford to play more hands and leverage position postflop. Minni appears to be pushing chips with a raising frequency, likely driven by stack pressure.

3. **Hand strength**: K-T is a solid hand heads-up. It has reasonable playability on most board textures and decent equity against Minni's likely raising range.

4. **Position**: In heads-up play, even the BB has positional advantage postflop in deeper streets.

Calling is the natural play here - the equity and odds strongly favor seeing a flop.

Betting 350 with K-T on Q-T-8 flop. I have a strong pair with excellent kicker. On a heads-up flop, with Minni short-stacked at 9.5k, applying pressure with this hand makes sense. The bet builds the pot when I'm likely ahead and forces decisions from a depleted stack. This is a value bet against his wide short-stack raising range.

I call 350. With As7c I only have Ace-high on Q-T-8 but I have position and a live overcard. A raise to 700 would bloating the pot without a made hand and risks an uncomfortable 3‑bet; with deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot manageable and float rather than overcommit. Plan: call, see the turn — if an Ace hits I can apply pressure; if not I’ll use position to evaluate and fold to heavy aggression.

Betting 1,500 on the turn with K-T for top pair on Q-T-8-4. I have a strong hand against Minni's short-stacked range, and the blank turn card doesn't change my assessment. 6-Th is too weak to play even with button position and chip lead in heads-up. The hand lacks equity and playability post-flop. I should preserve chips by folding marginal hands and focus on playing stronger holdings aggressively to leverage my chip advantage.

I'm on the button (also small blind) heads-up with Ac4d. Folding 7♥6♥ in the big blind vs Minni's 300 raise. This is a weak, marginal hand that lacks playability even in heads-up. Tc3s is a very weak hand, even in heads-up play. While I have position on the button and the odds to call are 3:1, this hand has poor equity and almost no playability post-flop. Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) I should be opening a very wide range. Qc3d is marginal but acceptable in position — it can win preflop and has some queen-high and straight potential. Calling Minni's raise with A2o. In heads-up play with significant stack advantage (30.5k vs 9.2k), A2o has sufficient equity against Minni's raising range. Checking A2o on K-T-7 is correct. This board likely connected with Minni's preflop raising range. Betting 500 on the river with pair of aces (top pair). River blank (4d) doesn't change board assessment. Minni's turn call suggests medium-strength hand like K-x, Q-x, or worse Ax. River decision with Ad3d (pair of threes with Ace kicker) against Minni's short stack. The board is complete (Kc2c3sJhTd) and Minni has checked, indicating weakness. Short-stack opponent raising frequently from button. My Js7s has reasonable equity against Minni's wide range. Getting 4:1 odds (100 to call 400 pot) makes calling correct. Plan to evaluate flop and leverage position post-flop against their remaining ~7,100 chips.

Turn action with Js7s on Ks4h2d9s board. The 9s turn card is favorable - it brings another spade, completing my flush draw to 9 solid outs. Against Minni's short stack (7000 remaining after calling flop), continuation betting is the optimal play here.
