River: board is 9♦-5♠-3♥-4♦-4♥ and I hold A♦6♠ — I have only Ace‑high and no backdoor outs. My line (open pre, c‑bet flop, check turn, call 650) is consistent with a marginal/value‑heavy range for Claude (medium pairs like 9/5/3, small pockets, some K/Qx and sticky calls). Pot is 2500. To make a bluff profitable I need Claude to fold a substantial portion of his range (e.g. a 1000 bet requires ≈29% folds, a 650 bet ≈21%); from his history he calls down wide and is unlikely to fold enough. Betting would bloat the pot out of position with no showdown value and poor fold equity. Best line is pot control and take the showdown — check and see his cards.

River with K-high after Minni checks. Her checking pattern (turn and river) indicates weakness. K-high is marginal—it wins vs air/missed draws but loses to any made pair. Betting costs chips with unfavorable EV; Minni likely folds weak hands or calls with better. Checking goes to showdown where K-high has reasonable equity against her weak range. This balances risk management with pot control, maximizing value from her weakness while protecting chips with a marginal hand. Standard river play with positional and chip advantage.

Heads-up on the button with Q5s is a reasonable open-raise. I’ve already posted the SB and calling 50 concedes initiative; raising to the min (200 total) is standard in heads-up play — it builds the pot, gives me fold equity, and lets me play postflop in position with a suited queen that can make strong 2nd-best hands and nut draws. Claude has shown aggression preflop/postflop in previous hands, so taking the initiative is profitable. Plan: C-bet many flops, proceed cautiously if he 3-bets (fold to large 3-bets; consider calling small ones to see a flop).

KQ is a strong broadway hand in heads-up play. Minni is short-stacked (10,950) and has raised to 200 from the small blind. I'm in the BB with a significant chip lead (28,750). KQ plays well postflop with positional advantage after the flop. Calling 100 more to see a flop is the right play here—I have a strong hand and can realize its equity. If Minni is pushing light due to short stack pressure (which appears likely given recent aggressive play), KQ is well-positioned to win.

Betting 300 with K♣ Q♦ on K♠ 2♦ 8♦ flop. I have top pair with a strong kicker facing Minni's short stack (10,950). Minni has been playing very aggressively short-stacked, suggesting a wide range where I'm likely ahead. The dry flop texture favors my hand. Betting applies pressure, builds the pot with my strong holding, and extracts value from weaker hands in Minni's range. This is a standard continuation bet that maximizes my equity advantage in this heads-up situation.

Facing 300 into a 400 pot (700 total) requires ~30% equity to justify a call. Q♥5♥ on K♠2♦8♦ has very little showdown value — roughly 6 clean outs (three queens and three fives) for ~24% to pair by river, and almost no backdoor prospects against Claude’s likely continuation range (Kx, pocket pairs, 8x/2x, and some strong diamond combos). A raise to the 600 minimum is a risky bluff against a deep, often-aggressive opponent who can comfortably call or reraise; it’s unlikely to be +EV here. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

In heads-up play from the button, position is critical. Q6o is a reasonable raising hand given the positional advantage and the fact that we act last postflop. With Minni at 10,850 (a relatively short stack) and my stack at 29,000, we can afford to take pots with marginal holdings when in position. A raise to 250 applies reasonable pressure while maintaining pot control. This is consistent with aggressive heads-up strategy where we can widen our raising range significantly from the button. The raise is sized to be substantial enough to apply pressure but not so large as to be reckless with a Q6 hand.

Heads-up vs the button (SB) opened to 250. I’m in the big blind out of position with A♠7♠ — a very playable hand heads-up with an ace blocker and a suited card. Rather than calling OOP and playing multi-street passively, I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to a wide SB opening range. I’ll 3-bet to 900 (≈3.6x the open): big enough to fold out weaker speculative hands and get value from worse aces/pairs, but small enough relative to my ~108bb stack that I can fold or re-evaluate if Claude 4-bets big. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously OOP but use the ace blocker and suit to continuation-bet or control pot size depending on texture.

Q6 offsuit is a marginal hand that doesn't warrant continuing against a significant 3-bet from the big blind, even in heads-up play. While aggressive play from the button is important in heads-up, we should maintain reasonable hand standards when facing re-raises. Minni's stack (10,050) is shorter than mine (28,800), but that doesn't justify getting it in with Q6o - a hand that would have poor equity against Minni's re-raising range. With 28,800 chips, we can afford to be selective and fold marginal holdings, waiting for premium hands or better spots to accumulate chips. This fold preserves our stack while maintaining positional discipline.

Heads-up on the button with A♣6♠ — this is a strong heads-up holding: an ace with a decent kicker and the benefit of position postflop. With stacks 11,150 (me) vs 28,700 (BB) and the pot already 150, I want to take the initiative, build the pot when appropriate, and generate fold equity. A standard 3x-ish open is the right play: it’s large enough to discourage limp/calls and to get value from worse aces and wide calling ranges, but not so large to overcommit against a 3-bet. If Claude calls I’ll have position and can c-bet most dry flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards; if he 3-bets big I can fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing. Raise to 300 total to seize initiative and maximize expected value in this heads-up spot.

I'm holding Ts Ah (Ten-Ace) in the Big Blind heads-up against Minni on the button. This is a premium holding in heads-up play. Minni raised to 300 from the small blind, and I need to call 200 more.

With Ace-Ten, I should be playing aggressively in heads-up. Raising builds the pot with a strong hand, applies pressure to Minni, and gives me the initiative. A raise to 700 total (400 more on top of the 300 already out there) is appropriately sized - not too small to min-raise, but not so large that I'm overcommitting with a non-premium situation.

This is a profitable spot to build the pot with a strong hand in heads-up play.

Heads-up on the button with A♣6♠. I opened to 300 and Claude 3-bet to 700 (I need to call 400 more). With position and a single ace plus decent kicker, this is a hand I want to see a flop with rather than fold or thin 4-bet. Calling keeps the pot manageable, uses my positional advantage postflop, and the pot odds (400 into a 1400 pot ≈28.6%) justify a call against a likely wide-ish heads-up 3-betting range. I’ll avoid a small, non-committal 4-bet (1100) since A6o is marginal as a 4-bet bluff and a larger 4-bet would overcommit. Plan postflop: continue when I hit an ace or get favorable texture, fold to heavy aggression on very bad boards, and extract value when appropriate.

Betting 450 on the flop with Ace-Ten. This is a standard continuation bet after my pre-flop raise. The flop is dry (Qc 4s 2c), and I likely have the best hand against Minni's range of calling hands. A ~1/3 pot bet maintains aggression, builds the pot when ahead, and can win immediately. Heads-up play favors aggressive play with premium holdings.

Call 450. The flop bet is small into a ~1400 pre-bet pot (pot now 1850) so calling 450 requires ~24.3% equity. With A♣6♠ I have 3 outs to an ace and 3 outs to a six (≈24% to pair by the river) plus a ~4% chance to hit a backdoor nut club flush — overall my raw equity to improve is in the neighborhood of the break‑even point, and I also have position to realize that equity and extract value or fold later. Claude 3‑bet preflop so his range contains many value hands, and a flop raise here risks getting 4‑bet or committed with a marginal hand; folding is too tight given the price, and raising is too speculative. Plan: call and see the turn — if I pair the A or 6 or pick up a club I will press for value; if I miss and face significant aggression on the turn I will fold to large bets to protect my stack.

Continuing with value bet on the turn. Board is Qc 4s 2c 3c - a dry board where my Ace-Ten likely has the best hand. The 3c is a harmless card. I've established aggression preflop (raised to 700) and on the flop (bet 450), and Minni has called both times, suggesting a moderate hand rather than a premium one. A ~700 bet (roughly 1/3 pot) maintains pressure, builds the pot when ahead, and extracts value from worse holdings like King-high or mid-pairs. In heads-up play with chip advantage, continuing aggression is profitable in this spot.

I have the nut club flush (A♣ with clubs on Qc–4s–2c–3c). Claude has been aggressive (3‑bet preflop and firing small bets), so his range is wide and likely contains many club combos and pocket pairs that will call a sizable value raise. The turn bet is small into a ~3k pot, so calling keeps weaker hands in but under‑prices my made hand. I want to extract maximum value without committing my entire stack yet, and still retain the ability to call a shove if he 4‑bets. I’ll raise for value to charge Kc/Jc/Tc and pairs that call down with his sticky tendencies — raising to 3,000 total is large enough to pull value from worse clubs and medium pairs but not so large as to fold out too many callers or overcommit pre‑river. If he jams over I will call because I have the nuts.

Calling 2300 with Ace-Queen on a dry board. I have a strong hand that beats much of Minni's realistic range, I'm getting good pot odds (3.6:1), and I maintain a chip advantage. Minni is short-stacked (4050 left) and their turn raise on an innocuous 3c suggests either a desperation play or a made hand. Calling allows me to see the river and make an informed decision there, rather than overcommitting with just ace-high at this street. This balances my aggression with sound bankroll management in a heads-up match.

Checking on the river with Ace-high. After Minni's aggressive turn check-raise to 3,000, my Ace-Ten is a marginal hand. The final King doesn't improve my situation. Checking accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) Allows me to see the showdown without risking additional chips against her likely strong range, (2) Potentially induces a bluff if Minni was semi-bluffing, (3) Gets me to showdown efficiently given my modest hand strength. I maintain my chip advantage (24,650 vs 7,050) while avoiding unnecessary losses on a hand that's likely behind to her turn-raise range but could win against certain holdings. This is prudent river play in a heads-up match with a marginal holding.

I have the nut club flush (A♣) — the turn gave me the best possible flush and the K♦ on the river changes nothing. Claude checked after calling my large turn raise, which looks like pot control with medium strength (Kx, Qx, KQ, or a club draw that missed) or a reluctant call-down hand. This is an unbeatable hand vs any holding Claude can reasonably have, so I should bet for value rather than give a free showdown. The pot is 8,300; I want a size that induces calls from single-pair and worse club hands without folding them out. I’ll bet ~half‑pot (4,000). That size targets Kx/Qx and medium clubs, keeps worse hands in, and still leaves me room to call a shove if he somehow jams — which I would because I have the nuts.

Calling 4,000 on the river with Ts Ah (Ace-high). Pot odds of 4.075:1 mean I only need approximately 20% equity to break even. Against Minni's turn check-raise range (likely value hands or occasional bluffs given her short stack), I have reasonable equity—she could have broadway cards, pairs, or be bluffing. The King on the river doesn't significantly alter the dynamics. My massive chip lead (24,650 vs 3,050) makes this a low-risk, positive-expectation call. In heads-up play, calling marginal hands when getting the right price is standard strategy, especially against an aggressive short-stacked opponent.

Raising to 300 total applies pressure on Minni's critically short stack (2,950 chips). In heads-up poker, positional advantage and aggression against a short stack are more important than hand strength. A raise here either wins the pot immediately or puts Minni in a difficult spot. Even if called, 9-4 has reasonable equity against the wide range a desperate short stack must play. This is a standard aggression play with position against a vulnerable opponent.

Heads-up in the big blind with pocket Aces (Ah Ac) facing a button open to 300. This is the best preflop hand — I want to build the pot and get value while denying cheap odds to speculative callers. A standard 3‑bet sizing versus a 3x open is about 3–4x the open; I’ll 3‑bet to 1000 total (I’ve posted 100, so I’m putting 900 more in). That size extracts value from worse hands, reduces implied odds for draws, and keeps plenty of chips behind to play postflop. If Claude 4‑bets big I’ll re‑evaluate (AA will often continue or commit depending on sizing).

With a critical short stack, Minni is committing chips out of desperation. Though 9-4o is weak, calling 700 more (1.9% of my 36k stack) to see a flop with positional advantage is standard. I control the action postflop and can leverage position to win against their wide range without hitting. This is optimal risk management while maintaining tournament momentum.

Heads‑up vs a deep-stacked caller: I have AA on 3c 3d Ks. Against Claude’s 3‑bet‑call range I’m far ahead — only very rare 3x/quads beat me. Pot is 2000 and I have 2050 behind, so I want to extract value from Kx and medium pairs while not committing my whole stack unnecessarily. I’ll bet a large but not all‑in size (1500, ~75% pot) to charge worse kings/pairs and deny free cards. If he shoves or raises massively, those are polarized lines (sets/trips or bluffs) and I’ll be prepared to call most reasonable shoves with AA; if he flats I continue to get value on later streets.

Minni is all-in with a critically short 550-chip stack after betting 1500 into a 3500 pot. Getting 3.3:1 odds with 9-4o against her wide desperate range justifies the call. She's forced to gamble with nearly any hand due to stack pressure. Even with a weak holding, pot odds make this mathematically sound - I likely have 30-35% equity which beats the 25% needed. In a tournament context at hand 95/150, securing this pot significantly boosts my stack. Standard aggression+equity play against a desperate opponent.

I have pocket Aces on 3c 3d K s 6h. With only 550 behind into a 5000 pot I'm effectively pot-committed — my shove now maximizes EV. Claude called my 3‑bet preflop and called a large flop bet, so his calling range includes many Kx and medium pairs that I beat; the only common hands that currently crush me are holdings with a 3 (trips/quads). If I shove 550 and he calls, he needs ~9% equity to justify the call (550 / (5000+550) ≈ 9%), so this all‑in is profitable versus a wide calling range. Checking to the river surrenders value and gives him free cards; folding is obviously wrong. I will push my remaining 550 to get maximum value and fold equity.

Minni is all-in for 550 chips. Getting 11:1 odds on a minuscule 1.6% of my stack call. Even with 9-4 offsuit, pot odds make this an automatic call. The river will determine the winner, but mathematically this decision was already made when the chips went in the middle. Winning this pot before the final 55 hands significantly improves my tournament position.

Heads-up on the button with Q8o — this is a reasonable opening hand in position. I want to seize initiative, get fold equity and keep pot control with a modest commitment relative to my 60bb stack. A standard 2x BB open (raise to 200 total) achieves that: it pressures the big blind, keeps the pot small if called, and preserves stack depth for postflop play. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call smaller 3-bets depending on sizing and SPR, and play postflop aggressively with position when called.

Pocket tens in heads-up play is a premium hand, especially when facing a short-stacked opponent. Minni has only 5,900 chips compared to my 33,800, giving me a 5.7x chip advantage. Their min-raise to 200 from the small blind is a typical short-stack aggression move.

With TT, I want to:
1. Build the pot while holding a strong hand
2. Apply pressure to Minni's marginal stack
3. Maximize value from this premium holding

Raising here accomplishes all three objectives. A raise to 600 (3x their raise) is appropriately aggressive while still leaving room for further action. This raise is:
- Strong enough to represent genuine strength
- Proportional to the pot size
- Large enough to create meaningful pressure on their short stack
- Not so large that I'm overcommitting unnecessarily

In heads-up play with a chip lead, I should be playing premium hands aggressively to win pots and accumulate chips steadily toward tournament victory.

I opened to 200 on the button with Q8o and Claude 3-bet to 600. With ~59bb effective, Q8o is marginal but perfectly defendable heads-up in position. Claude’s 3-bet sizing is small and likely contains a wide range, so folding is too tight and a light 4-bet isn’t attractive (no strong blockers and it risks facing a shove). I’ll call the extra 400 (600 total) to keep the pot controllable and use position postflop. Plan: continue aggressively when I connect (pair of queens, good backdoor turns); otherwise proceed cautiously — check/call small C-bets and fold to sustained heavy pressure if I miss.

Continuation bet on flop after pre-flop aggression. Despite the ace being a scare card, Minni's min-raise and call line suggests they likely don't have premium aces. The short-stack dynamics require applying maximum pressure. A 600 bet (50% pot) continues my aggressive posture, forces difficult decisions from opponent, and leverages my chip advantage. Need to win pots and accumulate chips in this heads-up matchup.

Call 600. Heads-up Claude’s small preflop 3-bet and his tiny flop stab (600 into a 1800 pot, ~1/3) represent a fairly wide, often uncommitted range — not just premium Ax. I have Q8o: no made hand yet but a queen out on the turn or backdoor hearts/runner-runner hearts give me workable equity. The price is right — calling 600 into 1800 means I need ≈25% equity to continue, which Q8o has frequently vs a wide C-bet range, and I retain position. Raising is unattractive (no strong blockers, would bloat the pot with a marginal hand) and folding is too tight heads-up. Plan: call, use position to see the turn and re-evaluate — bet/raise if I improve (Q/8/heart) or check-fold to heavy turn/river aggression if I miss.

Continuing aggressive play on the turn with pocket tens in a dominant heads-up position. Minni's short stack (4,900) and calling pattern suggests weak to medium holdings rather than strong hands. A 1,200 bet (50% pot) maintains my aggressive line from preflop and flop, applies critical pressure to their dwindling stack, and positions me to win the pot or get favorable chip accumulation. With a 6.7x chip advantage and a premium hand, consistent aggression is optimal strategy to close out this short-stacked opponent and accumulate chips toward tournament victory.

Call 1200. The turn Qs pairs my Qd and gives me top pair, but with a weak 8 kicker and my backdoor heart draw is gone (turn is a spade). Claude’s line (small preflop 3‑bet, small flop stab, turn bet 1200) is wide — it can contain Ax, sets, medium pairs and bluffs. The pot is 3600 so calling 1200 requires ~25% equity; top pair has enough equity versus a broad continuing range to justify a call. A raise would bloat the pot and commit too many chips with a vulnerable kicker and allow Claude (deep-stacked) to shove or play back aggressively; folding is too tight with a made hand heads‑up. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, use position to see the river, and then re-evaluate: extract value if I improve or face only modest river bets, but fold to heavy river aggression on obvious value lines (e.g. Ace/set indications).

Checking the river on a board with Ac-Ad against a short-stacked opponent who has called all streets. My pocket tens is weak on this board and likely behind if Minni has any ace or queen. Minni's river check suggests weakness. With my dominant 8.5:1 chip lead, I should take the safe path to showdown. If my tens are good, I win the pot; if not, I've minimized losses. Betting would only win against complete bluffs while risking stacks against made hands. The conservative check maximizes expected value in this chip-leader scenario.

Recap and hand reading: I opened preflop, Claude 3‑bet to 600 and I called. Flop (2h Ac 4h) he made a small continuation stab (600) and I called. Turn Qs gave me top pair (Q8) and I called his 1200. River paired the board with Ad, so the board is 2h Ac 4h Qs Ad and Claude checked. Assessment: I have top pair with a very weak 8‑kicker. Claude’s line (small preflop 3‑bet, small flop stab, a turn bet) is wide but contains a lot of Ax and medium‑strength hands that now beat me because the river paired an ace. Options: betting thin for value risks getting check‑raised or jammed by any ace or full house and commits a big chunk of my 3700 stack into a 4800 pot; checking keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize showdown equity against bluffs and worse Qx/medium pairs. Plan: check to control the pot size and avoid being stacked by a river check‑raise/jam from trips/full house. If Claude leads out, call small/medium bets for value but fold to polarizing large shoves that represent a made monster.

Heads-up play on the button with significant chip advantage (31k vs 8.4k). Raising with 5-Ten applies positional pressure against Minni's short stack. In heads-up scenarios, position dominates over hand strength. A minimum raise to 150 total forces Minni to act out of position with limited resources, which is where we want to be. This is standard aggressive button play.

Facing a button/SB open to 200 while I'm in the big blind with 2♦9♥. This is a very weak offsuit hand that will be dominated a large portion of the time and plays terribly out of position. Calling 100 to see a flop gives Claude the positional advantage and puts chips at unnecessary risk; a small 3-bet would be a thin, low EV bluff. With a healthy 84BB stack there's no need to gamble with junk — concede the 100 already posted and fold.

Raise to 300 (3x BB). Heads-up on the button I should open very wide — Td8o is a playable hand with good equity and the positional advantage postflop. I’ll take the initiative rather than limp: raising gains fold equity, denies cheap flops, and lets me play in position. 300 is large enough to apply pressure and define a polarized range but small relative to stacks (I remain deep ~80 BB), so I’m not overcommitting with a marginal hand. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll fold; I’ll call smaller 3-bets and proceed cautiously postflop (c-bet on favorable flops, check/controls on overcard-heavy boards).

I'm in the Big Blind with KhAc, one of the strongest starting hands in poker. Minni has raised to 300 from the button with 8100 chips remaining. With a significant chip advantage (31500 vs 8100) and a premium hand, I should apply pressure here. KA is a hand that plays well against a wide range and should be played aggressively, especially when my opponent has a relatively short stack. A standard raise to 900 total (adding 600 to her bet) accomplishes several things: it applies meaningful pressure on her stack, it extracts value from weaker hands she might be raising with, and it gives me the initiative heading into the flop. This is clearly a raise situation - calling would be too passive with such a strong hand.

I opened wide to 300 on the button with Td8h. Claude’s 3-bet to 900 is a small-ish sizing and represents a mixed range heads-up, not just premiums. With 81BB effective and position, I’ll call rather than fold or 4-bet: calling 600 more (900 total committed) gets ~33% pot odds and allows me to use position post-flop to realize equity, c-bet favorable dry boards, and control/meditate pot size on scary overcard-heavy flops. A 4-bet or shove with T8o is too thin and will commit too much against likely stronger hands. I’ll call and proceed cautiously on the flop, folding to large, non-blocker-heavy aggression when appropriate.

I flopped top pair with top kicker (AK on A-5-T board) against a short-stacked opponent who called my preflop raise. Minni has 7500 chips remaining and faces a critical decision. I should bet for value to build the pot with my premium hand, charge her draws, and apply pressure on her marginal holdings. A bet of 1200 achieves all these objectives while maintaining pot control. This is a clear value bet that extracts maximum EV from her desperate situation.

Flop: Ad 5s Ts. I hold Td8h — second pair (pair of tens) with a thin kicker. Claude 3-bet preflop to 900, so his range is polarized heads-up (Ax, big pairs, some broadways and light 3-bets). His flop bet of 1200 into a 3000 pot is relatively small (≈40%), which is consistent with a mixed range — both value (Ax, sets) and a bunch of bluffs/turn-barrel candidates. Folding is too tight with a made pair; raising is thin and dangerous because it will get value from almost no worse hands and will commit me huge when he actually has an Ace or better. Calling is the best line: it keeps worse hands and bluffs in, uses my positional advantage, and the price is attractive (call 1200 to win ~4200 ≈ 28.6% pot equity needed). Plan: call now, see the turn and re-evaluate — on a blank I can apply pressure or extract thin value; on an Ace or heavy action I can fold and preserve most of my stack.

Turn card (Qd) is relatively neutral for my range. I'm holding top pair with top kicker (AcAd with K kicker) - an extremely strong hand on this board. Minni is severely short-stacked with only 6300 chips remaining and has already committed significantly by calling my preflop raise and flop value bet. The turn brings a Queen which may have helped some of her holdings (AQ, QQ, KQ) but doesn't change my strong equity position. By betting 2400, I accomplish several objectives: (1) extract maximum value from her weaker holdings (lower pairs, AT, KT with Q kicker), (2) maintain aggressive momentum and initiative, (3) apply maximum pressure on her dwindling stack - after this bet she'll have 3900 remaining with ~6600 in the pot, making her pot odds poor if she calls, forcing her into difficult decisions with marginal hands. Given her stack size and her tendency to call down previously, a value bet here is the optimal play to build the pot while we potentially move toward showdown.

I have Td8h — second pair (tens) with a thin kicker on Ad 5s Ts Qd. Claude 3‑bet preflop so his range contains a lot of Ax and strong hands, but his small flop/turn sizings (1200 then 2400 into a 6600 pot) are consistent with a mixed range that includes bluffs and probing bets. Folding would be too tight with a made hand and bad use of pot odds: calling 2400 to potentially win the current pot gives me very attractive odds (break‑even equity ≈21%). Raising is thin and will either fold out only the very weakest of his holdings or get me stacked by Ax/sets that dominate me. I’ll call 2400, keep the pot under control and use position on the river to decide — fold to heavy action if an Ace/obvious value hand shows up or extract thin value/check‑call against bluffs on a blank. After calling I’ll have ~3900 left to play with.

River with complete board A♦-5♠-T♠-Q♦-2♥. I hold A♣K♥ for top pair, top kicker - an extremely strong hand. Minni has 3,900 chips remaining and has called aggressively throughout the hand (calling preflop raise, flop value bet, and turn aggression). With her short stack and demonstrated calling pattern, betting her entire remaining stack creates a mathematically difficult situation for her to fold given pot odds while maximizing value from my premium hand. She's likely holding weaker ace combinations, medium pairs, or drawing hands given her range, making this bet highly profitable. This is the final street with all community cards dealt, so optimal play is to extract maximum value on the river.

I have Td8h on Ad 5s Ts Qd 2h — second pair (tens) with a weak kicker. Claude 3‑bet preflop and then made small, consistent bets on flop (1200) and turn (2400), which is a line that contains a lot of Ax/Qx/value hands but also a mix of missed broadways and thin bluffs. The river bet is 3,900 into a 12,900 pot (~30% sizing). Calling 3,900 to win 16,800 (12900+my call) requires ~23.2% equity (3,900 / 16,800).

Against Claude’s polarized 3‑bet line I’m behind most Ax and Qx and JJ+/sets, but I still beat a fair share of his missed broadways, weaker pairs, and bluffs. The board is relatively dry and the price is attractive — I estimate my showdown equity vs his plausible betting range is in the neighborhood of, or above, the break‑even 23% threshold. Raising would be a thin, largely bluffing line with a poor kicker and folding here would be overly nitty given the pot odds and heads‑up dynamics.

Decision: call the 3,900 — it’s +EV given the pot odds and my reasonable showdown equity with second pair.
